reason: Within an 80+ cohort, residual age variation may weakly track frailty/renal reserve. Likely adds limited unique signal once perioperative physiology and comorbidities are included, so low sparse-selection priority.

Sex relates to baseline muscle mass/creatinine generation and AKI risk differences. However, the outcome is a creatinine ratio (partly normalizing baseline), so incremental predictive value is modest.

Race category is a coarse demographic proxy and not a direct perioperative renal insult marker. In sparse selection it is usually dominated by baseline creatinine/comorbidities and hemodynamics, with risk of encoding non-causal variation.

Race may correlate with baseline CKD burden and access-to-care factors but is indirect for postoperative AKI trajectory. Likely redundant with measured clinical variables and low priority for a sparse linear model.
Demographic category with weak direct linkage to acute postoperative renal hypoperfusion/injury. Expected to contribute little unique predictive value relative to physiologic and lab features.
Indirect demographic proxy with limited mechanistic connection to hour-60 creatinine rise. In sparse modeling it is unlikely to survive against stronger renal/illness-severity signals.
Coarse demographic indicator and typically low-prevalence, making estimates unstable. Not a direct renal biomarker and likely redundant with comorbidity/physiology features.
Heterogeneous catch-all category that is hard to interpret and usually noisy. Minimal unique predictive value for postoperative creatinine ratio in a sparse model.
Ethnicity is an indirect sociodemographic proxy rather than a perioperative renal injury signal. Typically dominated by direct clinical measurements and thus low sparse-selection priority.

Height mainly reflects body size and is only indirectly related to creatinine kinetics. With weight available and outcome defined as a ratio to baseline creatinine, it is unlikely to add unique predictive value.
Weight captures body size and may relate to hemodilution, dosing, and AKI risk (e.g., obesity). Still indirect and often redundant with more proximal physiologic/lab predictors, so modest priority.

Represents inherited cardiovascular risk but has little direct connection to acute postoperative kidney injury. Expected to be weak and dominated by actual comorbidities and perioperative course variables.

Diabetes is a strong predisposing factor for CKD and postoperative AKI via microvascular disease and reduced renal reserve. It provides relatively unique baseline risk information that can persist beyond immediate post-op noise.

Diabetes control status is a coarse proxy for chronic glycemic burden and may be inconsistently defined. Adds limited incremental value beyond the diabetes indicator and perioperative glucose/insulin features (if present), so lower priority.

Dyslipidemia reflects long-term atherosclerotic risk but is not a direct driver of acute postoperative creatinine rise. Likely redundant with other vascular comorbidity markers and low value for sparse selection.

Hypertension is a common contributor to chronic kidney disease and reduced renal autoregulation, increasing AKI susceptibility. Predictive but less specific than diabetes and often correlated with other comorbidities, so moderate priority.

History of infective endocarditis implies prior severe infection/valve pathology and possible baseline frailty. It is an indirect, low-prevalence predictor of AKI compared with perioperative hemodynamics/labs, so unlikely to survive sparse selection.

Chronic lung disease reflects limited pulmonary reserve and higher risk of hypoxemia/ventilator dependence that can contribute to renal hypoperfusion. Moderately useful as a comorbidity signal but largely indirect and often dominated by post-op physiologic variables.

Home oxygen use is a strong marker of advanced pulmonary disease and baseline functional limitation. It can add unique severity information beyond a generic COPD flag, but remains an indirect proxy for AKI risk.
Bronchodilator therapy is a treatment marker for obstructive lung disease. It is more clinician-behavior/proxy and redundant with chronic_lung_disease/home oxygen, so low priority for a sparse model.

Sleep apnea indicates chronic intermittent hypoxia and cardiometabolic risk. For predicting 60h creatinine ratio it is a weak, indirect signal and typically dominated by immediate post-op hemodynamics/renal markers.

Pneumonia reflects active infection/inflammation and higher likelihood of shock, nephrotoxin exposure, and prolonged ventilation. It can contribute incremental risk information, but is still indirect relative to direct renal/perfusion features.

IV drug abuse is a social/behavioral history that may correlate with infections or comorbidities. It is nonspecific and likely low-prevalence/noisy in this cohort, offering little unique predictive value for AKI trajectory.
Depression is a chronic psychosocial comorbidity. It has minimal direct mechanistic linkage to early post-op AKI and is unlikely to add signal beyond physiologic severity measures.

Alcohol use can associate with liver disease, malnutrition, and perioperative complications. As coded here it is typically heterogeneous and indirect, so only a weak candidate unless no better comorbidity markers exist.

Liver disease captures impaired perfusion reserve, inflammation, and altered drug handling, all of which increase AKI susceptibility after cardiac surgery. It is a relatively strong baseline risk modifier that can add unique value beyond generic comorbidity counts.

Immunosuppression indicates higher infection risk and altered inflammatory response, potentially increasing AKI risk via sepsis or nephrotoxic exposures. Useful but indirect and often mediated through post-op complications captured elsewhere.

Prior mediastinal radiation is a marker of complex prior thoracic disease and potentially more difficult surgery. It is an indirect, low-frequency predictor and usually dominated by intra/post-op physiologic signals.
Cancer history reflects chronic illness burden and possible nephrotoxic treatments. As a broad label it is heterogeneous and mostly a proxy, so low-to-moderate priority for sparse selection.

Peripheral artery disease is a strong marker of systemic atherosclerosis and renovascular vulnerability, correlating with baseline CKD risk and perioperative hypoperfusion sensitivity. It often provides unique baseline risk information relevant to creatinine rise.

Thoracic aortic disease implies complex aortic surgery with higher embolic and perfusion-related renal risk. Moderately strong but partly a proxy for operative complexity that may be better captured by intra/post-op hemodynamics and labs.
Syncope is a nonspecific symptom with many etiologies and limited direct connection to postoperative renal function. It is unlikely to add unique predictive value in a sparse AKI model.

History of unresponsive state is a nonspecific neurologic comorbidity marker. It is largely an indirect frailty/illness-severity proxy and unlikely to add unique AKI prediction beyond stronger hemodynamic/renal signals.

Cerebrovascular disease reflects systemic atherosclerosis and baseline vascular risk. It may weakly correlate with renal vulnerability but is indirect and typically dominated by baseline renal/liver function and perioperative physiology.

Baseline hemoglobin captures anemia/hemodilution reserve and can relate to renal hypoxia and transfusion needs. It can add some unique pre-op risk signal, though it is still indirect for Hour-60 creatinine change.

Pre-op albumin is a marker of nutrition/inflammation and oncotic pressure, associated with frailty and postoperative complications including AKI. Useful baseline risk context but partly a general severity proxy rather than a direct renal marker.

Baseline bilirubin reflects hepatic dysfunction/cholestasis and overall organ reserve. It is an indirect predictor of AKI risk and is usually less uniquely informative than composite liver scores (e.g., MELD) or direct renal/hemodynamic features.

HbA1c reflects chronic glycemic control and diabetic microvascular disease, which increases susceptibility to AKI. It can contribute unique baseline risk information, though it is not a near-term physiologic precursor.

HIT antibody status mainly affects anticoagulation strategy and thrombosis risk rather than directly driving postoperative creatinine trajectory. Likely rare and a clinician-behavior/management proxy with low sparse-model yield.

MELD score summarizes hepatic/renal-related reserve and correlates with perioperative mortality and AKI risk. As a composite capturing multi-organ dysfunction, it is a strong baseline risk feature likely to survive sparse selection in this batch.

BNP reflects cardiac dysfunction and venous congestion, which are strong drivers of cardiorenal syndrome after cardiac surgery. It provides relatively unique pre-op hemodynamic/volume-status risk information for later creatinine rise.

5-meter walk test is a frailty/functional status measure associated with postoperative complications. It is indirect for AKI and often redundant with other comorbidity/physiology markers, so lower priority for a sparse model.

History of prior cardiovascular intervention is a broad marker of coronary/vascular disease burden. It is nonspecific and likely redundant with more specific prior CABG/PCI variables and physiologic measures.

Prior CABG indicates advanced CAD and potentially more complex redo surgery risk. It may weakly relate to AKI via operative complexity but is indirect and often dominated by perioperative hemodynamics and organ function markers.

Prior cardiac surgery/redo status is a proxy for surgical complexity and baseline disease. It can contribute some risk signal but is indirect for renal injury and typically secondary to physiologic predictors.

Prior PCI reflects CAD burden and prior contrast exposure history. For predicting Hour-60 creatinine ratio, it is indirect and likely redundant with BNP/MELD and perioperative renal perfusion signals.

PCI during the same episode implies recent contrast load and acute coronary instability, which can precipitate early renal injury. This is more temporally relevant than remote PCI and may add unique risk signal in a sparse model.
Stent presence is a subtype of prior PCI and adds little beyond the broader PCI indicators. It is largely redundant and unlikely to survive sparse feature selection.

Recency of prior cardiac procedures is a proxy for baseline cardiovascular complexity and scar/redo risk. It may weakly relate to periop instability and AKI risk but is indirect and likely dominated by post-op hemodynamics/labs in a sparse model.
Count/history of prior interventions reflects comorbidity burden and redo status. Predictive value for creatinine rise is mostly indirect and likely redundant with more proximal physiologic signals.

Recent ACE inhibitor exposure can predispose to perioperative hypotension and functional creatinine rise in older patients. It can add some unique baseline susceptibility signal, though it remains a pre-op proxy and may be outweighed by post-op perfusion variables.
Recent P2Y12 inhibitor use mainly reflects antiplatelet management and bleeding risk. Any association with AKI is indirect (transfusion/bleeding pathways) and unlikely to survive sparse selection.

Amiodarone use is a marker of arrhythmia history rather than renal physiology. Its link to postoperative creatinine change is weak and largely mediated by other variables (hemodynamics, vasopressors).

Anticoagulant use indicates atrial fibrillation/VTE risk and may correlate with bleeding/transfusion needs. It is an indirect clinician-behavior/comorbidity proxy with limited unique renal predictive value.

Aspirin use is common and mostly reflects baseline CAD prevention. It provides little specific information about evolving postoperative kidney dysfunction and is likely non-informative in a sparse model.

Beta-blocker use reflects baseline cardiac disease and may influence periop heart rate/cardiac output. Any AKI signal is indirect and likely redundant with measured post-op hemodynamics and vasoactive support.
Duration of beta-blocker therapy is largely redundant with the indicator of beta-blocker use. It adds minimal incremental information for predicting Hour-60 creatinine ratio.
CCB therapy duration is a chronic medication marker with weak mechanistic linkage to postoperative AKI. It is unlikely to add unique predictive value beyond other comorbidity/hemodynamic features.
GPIIb/IIIa inhibitor use is uncommon and mainly signals acute coronary syndrome/PCI-related management. Its relationship to creatinine rise is indirect and likely too sparse/noisy for selection.

Pre-op inotrope requirement indicates low cardiac output/shock physiology and high renal hypoperfusion risk. This is a strong baseline severity marker that can add unique susceptibility information even before post-op trajectories are observed.

Statin/lipid-lowering therapy is prevalent and primarily reflects chronic preventive care. It has limited direct relevance to short-term postoperative creatinine changes and is unlikely to be selected sparsely.
Chronic nitrate therapy is a marker of angina management rather than renal risk. Any association with AKI is indirect and likely redundant with broader cardiac severity indicators.
IV nitrates suggest active ischemia/heart failure management and potential hemodynamic fragility. It may weakly predict AKI via perfusion risk but is still an indirect treatment proxy.

Other antianginal therapy duration is nonspecific and overlaps with CAD severity and medication patterns. It is unlikely to contribute unique predictive signal for Hour-60 creatinine ratio in a sparse model.

Pre-op steroid exposure may reflect chronic inflammatory disease and can affect glucose/infection risk, indirectly influencing AKI risk. It is a weak, nonspecific proxy and likely dominated by direct hemodynamic/lab signals in a sparse model.

Recent direct thrombin inhibitor use mainly signals anticoagulation management and bleeding risk. It is largely a clinician-behavior/medication-history proxy with limited unique linkage to creatinine rise at 60h.

Warfarin use reflects baseline comorbidity (e.g., AF) and perioperative anticoagulation planning. It has little direct mechanistic connection to postoperative AKI and is unlikely to survive sparse selection.

Factor Xa inhibitor use is primarily a marker of anticoagulation history and perioperative bleeding management. Predictive value for 60h creatinine ratio is indirect and typically redundant with broader comorbidity/severity features.
Thrombin inhibitor use within 5 days is another anticoagulation-history indicator. It is unlikely to add unique predictive signal for AKI compared with physiologic and renal-proximal variables.

Statin use is a proxy for atherosclerotic burden and chronic cardiovascular risk, with mixed/weak associations with AKI. It may add slight baseline-risk information but is not a high-priority sparse feature.

Pre-op catheterization implies recent contrast exposure and higher CAD workup intensity, which can contribute to pre-renal vulnerability. It can add some unique AKI-risk signal beyond generic CAD labels, but remains indirect.

Known coronary anatomy disease indicates underlying CAD and chronic vascular disease. It is a broad comorbidity proxy and likely redundant with more granular CAD severity measures (e.g., number of diseased vessels).

Extent of CAD captures cardiovascular disease severity and correlates with operative complexity and risk of low-output states that predispose to AKI. More informative than a binary CAD flag, but still indirect relative to perioperative hemodynamics/labs.

Aortic valve disease (often AS) can drive perioperative hemodynamic instability and low renal perfusion risk. It may contribute unique baseline physiologic risk, though much of its effect is mediated through post-op hemodynamics.

Mitral valve disease relates to chronic congestion/low forward flow but is heterogeneous and often mediated by perioperative course. It is a moderate, indirect predictor and likely dominated by direct ICU trajectory features.

Tricuspid disease can indicate right-sided failure/venous congestion, a plausible contributor to renal dysfunction via elevated renal venous pressure. This may add somewhat unique risk information compared with left-sided lesions, warranting mid-level priority.

Pulmonic valve disease is uncommon and typically contributes limited incremental information in adult cardiac surgery cohorts. Likely low prevalence and weak unique association with 60h creatinine ratio.

Number of prior cardiac surgeries is a strong marker of redo complexity, adhesions, longer bypass/ischemic times, and higher risk of perioperative instability that drives AKI. It is a robust baseline risk feature likely to survive sparse screening.

Status is likely an administrative/clinical category (e.g., elective vs urgent) reflecting overall acuity. It can correlate with AKI risk but is a coarse severity proxy and often redundant with more specific physiologic variables.
Case canceled is an operational outcome and not a physiologic marker of renal risk for those who underwent surgery. It is likely irrelevant/noisy for predicting postoperative creatinine ratio.

Conversion (e.g., minimally invasive/robotic to open) is a marker of intraop complexity/complications that can increase AKI risk via longer ischemic time/bleeding. Useful as a relatively unique procedural-severity signal, but still indirect compared with hemodynamics/renal markers.

Robotic approach mainly reflects case selection and operative approach rather than renal physiology. Likely redundant with procedure type/complexity variables and weakly linked to Hour-60 creatinine ratio.

CABG indicates a specific surgical phenotype often associated with CPB exposure, hemodynamic swings, and embolic risk that can affect kidneys. Moderately useful as baseline procedural risk, but indirect and often dominated by direct perfusion/hemodynamic features.

Valve surgery is associated with longer CPB/cross-clamp and postoperative low-output states that predispose to AKI. Provides moderate unique procedural-risk information, but remains a coarse proxy versus physiologic trajectories.

Additional cardiac procedures generally increase operative duration/complexity and inflammatory burden, raising AKI risk. Helpful as a complexity marker, though heterogeneous and less specific than direct perfusion/renal signals.
AF ablation/maze adds operative time but is not strongly kidney-specific and often occurs in otherwise stable elective cases. Likely a weak, partially redundant complexity proxy for sparse selection.

Aortic procedures (especially arch/ascending) often entail deep hypothermia, circulatory arrest, and higher embolic/ischemic burden, strongly linked to postoperative AKI. This is a high-yield, relatively unique procedural risk factor likely to survive sparse screening.
Non-cardiac concomitant procedures are a broad marker of complexity but heterogeneous and not kidney-specific. Adds limited unique predictive value beyond other procedure/instability indicators.

Appropriate prophylactic antibiotic choice is a quality/process metric and only weakly related to early AKI at 60h. Mostly clinician-behavior/protocol adherence rather than a physiologic renal signal.
Timing of prophylactic antibiotics is a periop process measure with minimal direct linkage to creatinine rise by 60h. Unlikely to add unique predictive value in a sparse model.
Discontinuation ordering reflects stewardship/workflow and may correlate with length of stay or complications but is not a direct AKI precursor. High risk of being a noisy clinician-behavior proxy.
An extra intraop dose can proxy longer cases or higher blood loss, which can relate to AKI risk. Still indirect and likely redundant with more direct measures of duration/instability if available.

Lowest temperature reflects hypothermia strategy and may correlate with CPB complexity (e.g., aortic work) and systemic stress affecting kidneys. Moderately informative but partly redundant with aortic procedure indicators and other perfusion variables.

Nadir intraop hemoglobin captures hemodilution/bleeding and reduced oxygen delivery, a plausible direct contributor to renal ischemia and AKI. Strong, relatively specific physiologic insult marker that can add unique value beyond procedure labels.

Intraoperative hyperglycemia reflects stress response/diabetes and illness severity, which can associate with AKI but indirectly. Likely weaker and more confounded than hemodynamics, anemia, or perfusion-related features.

Use of cardiopulmonary/coronary perfusion indicates CPB/perfusion strategy and operative complexity that can influence renal perfusion and inflammatory injury. Moderately useful procedural/perfusion proxy, but less specific than direct post-op hemodynamic/renal markers.

Central aortic arterial cannulation reflects operative approach and case type more than renal physiology. Likely adds limited unique predictive value for Hour-60 creatinine beyond bypass time/arrest variables.

Femoral arterial cannulation often marks higher-complexity/aortic pathology and greater embolic/ischemic risk that can track with AKI. Some unique signal as a procedure-pattern proxy, but still indirect and partly redundant with arrest/CPB duration.

Axillary cannulation is commonly used in complex aortic surgery with altered perfusion strategies that can influence renal hypoperfusion risk. Moderately useful as a case-mix marker, though indirect and correlated with circulatory arrest/cerebral perfusion.

Innominate cannulation suggests arch/aortic procedures and selective perfusion strategies associated with longer/complex CPB and potential renal ischemia. Provides some incremental case-type information but is not a direct renal signal.
Other arterial sites are heterogeneous and mainly encode unusual operative technique. Likely noisy and dominated by more direct complexity measures like CPB time and arrest duration.
Femoral venous cannulation reflects access strategy and procedure logistics rather than kidney perfusion directly. Weak incremental value for sparse selection compared with CPB/arrest variables.
Jugular venous cannulation is typically an access/technique choice and may correlate with specific operations. Mostly indirect and likely redundant with other surgical complexity indicators.
Right atrial venous cannulation is common in standard CPB and thus has limited discriminatory power. Low unique predictive value for AKI compared with duration/ischemia variables.
Left atrial venous cannulation suggests specific surgical setups but does not directly encode renal insult severity. Likely sparse-model redundant with procedure type and CPB time.
Pulmonary venous cannulation is uncommon and may indicate specialized procedures, but the category is heterogeneous. Expected to be a weak, indirect proxy for AKI risk.
Bicaval cannulation is tied to certain intracardiac procedures and bypass setup. Adds limited unique renal prediction beyond CPB duration and arrest-related ischemia.
Other venous sites are heterogeneous and likely reflect documentation/technique variation. Very low expected unique signal for Hour-60 creatinine ratio in a sparse model.
Total CPB duration is a strong, well-established driver of postoperative AKI via inflammation, hemolysis, and renal hypoperfusion. High unique predictive value and likely to survive sparse selection.

Whether circulatory arrest occurred captures a major ischemic insult and high-complexity aortic surgery, both linked to AKI. Strong predictor, though some redundancy with arrest time and CPB duration.

Duration of DHCA directly quantifies the magnitude of systemic ischemia/reperfusion exposure, a potent precursor to AKI. Provides more granular, high-signal information than the binary arrest indicator.

Use of cerebral perfusion indicates specific arch strategies and case complexity that correlate with arrest physiology and longer CPB. Moderately informative but largely a procedural proxy and partially redundant with DHCA time/arrest.

Duration of selective cerebral perfusion is a marker of complex arch surgery and prolonged bypass exposure. It is an indirect AKI risk proxy and likely redundant with more general ischemic-time variables (e.g., cross-clamp/circulatory arrest), so lower sparse-selection priority.

Minutes of circulatory arrest reflect profound systemic hypoperfusion/ischemia during surgery, a strong upstream driver of postoperative AKI. Adds relatively unique signal about extreme intraop insult beyond routine clamp time, so a strong sparse-model candidate.

Binary indicator of aortic occlusion/cross-clamping is a coarse marker of operative technique. It is dominated by the continuous cross-clamp time and other duration/intensity measures, so limited incremental value.

Cross-clamp duration captures myocardial ischemic time and correlates with CPB duration, hemodynamic instability, and inflammatory load that contribute to AKI. Strong, widely predictive intraop exposure likely to survive sparse screening (though partially correlated with other complexity markers).

Use of cerebral oximetry is primarily a monitoring/clinician-practice indicator and correlates with case complexity rather than renal physiology. High risk of being a proxy with poor transportability and low unique predictive value.

Diffuse aortic calcification reflects vascular disease burden and may correlate with baseline renal vulnerability and operative complexity. However it is indirect and often redundant with comorbidity/complexity signals, so modest priority.

Whether/extent the ascending aorta/arch was assessed is largely documentation/monitoring behavior and case workflow. It provides little direct renal signal and is likely redundant with other complexity indicators.

Plaque assessment is mainly a procedural/documentation variable reflecting surgical evaluation rather than physiology. Weak and proxy-heavy for AKI prediction, unlikely to be retained in a sparse model.

Indicates that aortic pathology changed the operative plan, capturing a step-up in complexity that can increase ischemic time and embolic/inflammatory risk. Still indirect, but may add unique information beyond raw times in some cases.
Refusal of blood products is rare and reflects patient preference/logistics more than renal pathophysiology. Likely sparse, noisy, and not a stable predictor of creatinine trajectory.

Any intraoperative transfusion indicates bleeding/hemodilution and physiologic stress associated with AKI risk. Useful but somewhat redundant with specific component volumes (RBC/FFP/platelets/cryo), so mid-tier priority.

RBC transfusion volume is a strong marker of bleeding, anemia, hemodilution, and inflammatory/hemolysis exposure, all linked to postoperative AKI. More specific and typically more predictive than the generic 'any blood products' indicator, so high priority.

FFP transfusion reflects coagulopathy/bleeding severity and large-volume resuscitation, which can associate with AKI. Adds some nuance beyond RBCs but is often correlated with overall transfusion burden, so moderate priority.

Platelet transfusion is a marker of bleeding/coagulopathy and complex surgery, but its relationship to renal injury is less direct than RBC volume. Often redundant with overall transfusion/bleeding severity signals.

Cryoprecipitate use indicates severe hypofibrinogenemia/bleeding and high-acuity resuscitation. It is relatively infrequent and largely overlaps with other transfusion burden measures, limiting sparse-model value.

Clotting factor administration (e.g., PCC/factor concentrates) signals significant coagulopathy/bleeding management and high operative acuity. Indirect for AKI and likely correlated with transfusion volumes and surgical complexity, so modest priority.

Use of aminocaproic acid is mainly a bleeding-management practice pattern rather than a direct renal physiology marker. Likely redundant with stronger downstream signals (hemodynamics, transfusion/bleeding severity) and unlikely to survive sparse selection.

Tranexamic acid administration reflects antifibrinolytic strategy and bleeding risk, not kidney function directly. Any association with AKI is indirect and typically dominated by hypotension/vasopressors, lactate, and fluid balance variables.

TEE performed is a procedural/monitoring indicator and largely a proxy for case complexity and clinician behavior. It provides little unique predictive value for Hour-60 creatinine ratio compared with actual post-op hemodynamics and renal markers.

Residual aortic insufficiency can worsen forward flow and contribute to low effective perfusion, indirectly increasing AKI risk. Useful as a cardiac-output surrogate, but still downstream/indirect and often captured better by EF, pressor needs, and lactate.

Residual mitral regurgitation can drive pulmonary congestion and low forward output, which can impair renal perfusion. Moderately informative but likely redundant with EF and other hemodynamic severity features in a sparse model.

Residual tricuspid regurgitation is a strong marker of right-sided failure/venous congestion, which is closely linked to postoperative AKI via elevated renal venous pressure. This can add unique value beyond generic hypotension markers, especially in older cardiac surgery patients.

Indicator of valve surgery type/case mix, which correlates with bypass time/complexity and AKI risk only indirectly. Likely redundant with more proximal physiologic signals and other procedure descriptors.

AFib procedure (e.g., MAZE/ablation) mainly reflects operative complexity and atrial disease burden. Any renal signal is indirect and typically dominated by postoperative hemodynamics and congestion measures.
General anesthesia is near-universal in cardiac surgery and offers minimal discrimination. It is not a direct renal risk marker and would be dropped early in sparse selection.

PCC use suggests significant bleeding/coagulopathy and higher acuity, which can associate with AKI through shock/transfusion burden. Still a treatment proxy and usually redundant with direct perfusion/oxygen debt variables.

Post-repair aortic mean gradient reflects residual stenosis/afterload and potential low-output physiology. It is a weaker, context-dependent surrogate and often less predictive than EF and overt hemodynamic/vasopressor features.

Paravalvular leak can cause hemolysis and/or reduced effective forward flow, both plausibly contributing to AKI risk. Moderately useful but not as consistently informative as global function/congestion markers.

Mitral mean gradient indicates residual stenosis and potential elevated left atrial pressures. Renal impact is indirect and likely captured by downstream congestion/ventilation/hemodynamic variables, limiting sparse-model priority.

Mitral PVL can drive hemolysis and heart failure physiology, which may contribute to AKI. Provides some unique structural information but remains an indirect predictor relative to direct renal perfusion/congestion measures.

Combined residual mitral/tricuspid leak suggests significant valvular dysfunction and higher risk of low output and venous congestion. Moderately predictive but partially redundant with the individual regurgitation variables and EF.

Post-procedure EF is a key summary of cardiac pump function and a strong determinant of renal perfusion and need for vasoactive support. While indirect, it is a high-yield, relatively non-redundant physiologic anchor likely to survive sparse selection.

Planned PCI is a care-plan/procedural intent variable rather than a physiologic renal signal. It is likely weakly related to creatinine trajectory and mostly redundant with overall cardiac disease severity and procedure complexity proxies.

Intra-aortic balloon pump use indicates significant perioperative low-output/hemodynamic instability with risk of renal hypoperfusion. It can add unique predictive value for AKI risk beyond routine vitals because it captures a threshold of shock severity and escalation of support.

Baseline cath lab assist device use reflects pre-op instability/complexity but is indirect for post-op renal injury. Likely overlaps with stronger post-op support markers (IABP/ECMO) and general illness severity.

ECMO use is a strong marker of profound cardiopulmonary failure, systemic inflammation/hemolysis, and high AKI risk. In a sparse model it is a high-priority, relatively non-redundant indicator of extreme severity closely tied to subsequent creatinine rise.

Blood product transfusion volume indicates bleeding/hemodilution and inflammatory/ischemic burden, and is associated with AKI after cardiac surgery. It often captures operative complexity and physiologic insult not fully explained by hemoglobin/hematocrit alone.

Extubation in the OR is a proxy for uncomplicated surgery and better immediate cardiopulmonary status. It is indirect for renal outcomes and likely redundant with other severity/support variables (reintubation, postop intubation, ECMO/IABP).

Re-intubation reflects postoperative respiratory failure and clinical deterioration, which correlates with shock/sepsis and AKI risk. It is still an indirect severity proxy and may be partially redundant with ECMO/IABP and transfusion signals.
ICU visit is essentially universal in this cohort and mainly reflects workflow/triage rather than renal physiology. It is unlikely to add discriminative value for creatinine_ratio in a sparse model.

Postoperative hemoglobin reflects anemia/hemodilution and bleeding, which can contribute to renal hypoxia and AKI risk. However it is often redundant with hematocrit and transfusion volume, and may be confounded by fluid balance.

Postoperative hematocrit is another anemia/hemodilution marker overlapping strongly with hemoglobin. In sparse selection it is typically dominated by either hemoglobin (more commonly used) or transfusion volume as a stronger insult proxy.

Postoperative intubation status is a broad marker of immediate postoperative acuity and sedation/ventilation needs. It is indirect for kidney injury and likely redundant with more specific escalation markers (re-intubation, ECMO/IABP).

History of prior MI captures baseline cardiac disease and potential chronic low cardiac reserve. It is a distal risk factor and usually weaker than perioperative hemodynamic support variables for predicting a 60-hour creatinine ratio.

Timing of prior MI is a nuanced baseline-history detail with limited direct linkage to acute postoperative renal trajectory. Any signal is likely small and redundant with broader cardiac severity/support features.

Symptom timing at admission is a pre-op presentation descriptor and largely a documentation/triage proxy. It is unlikely to add unique predictive value for postoperative creatinine changes compared with direct perioperative instability markers.
Symptom timing at surgery is another pre-op context variable with weak mechanistic connection to AKI at 60 hours. It is likely noisy and dominated by intra/post-op support and transfusion indicators.

Angina class reflects baseline coronary symptom burden and functional limitation. It is an indirect, chronic severity marker and typically provides limited incremental value once perioperative support (ECMO/IABP) and bleeding/transfusion signals are included.

NYHA functional class reflects baseline heart failure symptom burden and overall cardiac reserve. It is an indirect AKI risk marker and likely dominated by more proximal hemodynamic/renal signals, so lower sparse-selection priority.

Cardiogenic shock indicates severe low cardiac output and renal hypoperfusion risk, a strong precursor to postoperative AKI. It can add unique predictive value beyond generic comorbidity flags, though it may overlap with vasopressor/hemodynamic features not shown here.

Resuscitation status suggests major peri-arrest instability and high illness severity, which can precede renal injury. However it is partly a clinician/event proxy and may be redundant with shock/vasopressor exposure, so moderate priority.

A general arrhythmia indicator captures baseline rhythm disease but is nonspecific. It is likely redundant with the more granular arrhythmia subtype variables in this batch and only indirectly related to creatinine rise.

Recent VT/VF implies severe electrical instability and potential low-flow episodes that can contribute to renal hypoperfusion. It can be informative but is episodic and often mediated through shock/resuscitation pathways, limiting unique value.
Sick sinus syndrome mainly reflects conduction system disease and pacing need rather than systemic hypoperfusion. It has weak direct linkage to AKI and is unlikely to survive sparse selection.

Atrial flutter reflects atrial arrhythmia burden and may correlate with cardiac disease severity. It is a relatively indirect AKI predictor and overlaps with atrial fibrillation and heart failure signals.

Second-degree AV block is primarily a conduction abnormality and often managed with pacing without major systemic hypoperfusion. It is unlikely to add unique predictive information for Hour-60 creatinine ratio.

Third-degree AV block can cause bradycardia and low output if not paced, which could affect renal perfusion. Still, it is typically corrected quickly and is a weaker, more indirect predictor than shock/heart failure.
Being paced is largely a treatment/device status and a proxy for conduction disease management. It is not a direct renal physiology marker and is likely redundant with underlying block/SSS variables.

Heart failure indicates chronic low cardiac reserve and venous congestion, both strong contributors to postoperative AKI risk in elderly cardiac surgery patients. It is a high-yield baseline risk feature likely to remain useful even in sparse models.

Recent atrial fibrillation is common and associates with worse cardiac function, hemodynamic instability, and thromboembolic/renal risk. It provides moderate unique signal but overlaps with heart failure and general arrhythmia burden.
Preoperative pain score is not a direct marker of renal function or perioperative renal perfusion. Any association would be indirect (frailty/analgesic use) and unlikely to survive sparse selection.

Cell saver volume reflects intraoperative blood loss/hemolysis exposure and surgical complexity, which can relate to AKI risk. However it is a noisy proxy and often dominated by more direct perfusion/hemolysis/lactate/pressors features.

Heparin dose before bypass mainly reflects protocolized anticoagulation and patient size rather than renal injury mechanisms. It is unlikely to add unique predictive value for postoperative creatinine trajectory.

Protamine dose reflects heparin reversal and can be associated with hemodynamic reactions, bleeding, and transfusion needs. Still, it is largely procedural/protocol-driven and indirect compared with direct perfusion or renal markers.

Intraoperative viscoelastic testing (e.g., TEG/ROTEM) mainly reflects bleeding/coagulopathy management. It is an indirect severity/procedure proxy and unlikely to add unique signal for Hour-60 creatinine ratio versus hemodynamics/renal markers.

Indicator/amount of any volatile anesthetic exposure. Volatile choice/exposure is weakly and indirectly related to AKI risk and is largely redundant with the more specific volatile-agent variables in this batch.
Use of isoflurane intraoperatively. Anesthetic selection is mostly clinician-preference/case-context and provides little unique predictive value for postoperative creatinine trajectory.
Use of sevoflurane intraoperatively. Any renal effect is small relative to perfusion/vasopressor/renal-function signals, so it is unlikely to survive sparse selection.
Use of desflurane intraoperatively. Primarily reflects anesthetic strategy rather than kidney injury physiology and is not a strong unique predictor of Hour-60 creatinine ratio.
Use of other volatile agents. Heterogeneous and largely a documentation/category artifact, making it a weak and noisy predictor for AKI-related creatinine rise.

Whether volatile anesthetic was used before bypass. Timing is intraoperative and mainly reflects anesthetic workflow; it is an indirect proxy and unlikely to add incremental value for later creatinine ratio.

Whether volatile anesthetic was used during cardiopulmonary bypass, which can correlate with bypass management style and case complexity. Still largely a clinician-behavior/protocol proxy with limited direct renal signal, but slightly more informative than agent identity alone.

Whether volatile anesthetic was used after bypass, reflecting immediate post-CPB management and hemodynamic stability. It remains indirect and likely dominated by direct perfusion/vasopressor variables (not in this batch), but may carry modest contextual signal.
Use of volatile anesthetic for maintenance anesthesia. Highly redundant with other volatile-use indicators and not a direct marker of renal hypoperfusion or evolving AKI.

Intraoperative dexmedetomidine infusion, often tied to sedation strategy and hemodynamic effects (bradycardia/hypotension). It is an indirect proxy for management/instability and may add small unique signal but is not a top sparse-model keeper.

Intraoperative propofol infusion, reflecting anesthetic technique and potentially hypotension burden. Predictive value for creatinine ratio is indirect and likely redundant with direct hemodynamic summaries, but may weakly capture instability/vasopressor need.
Intraoperative midazolam infusion, mainly indicating sedation choice and case workflow. It has minimal direct linkage to renal injury physiology and is unlikely to survive sparse selection.

Total insulin given intraoperatively/at exit, a proxy for perioperative hyperglycemia and metabolic stress. Dysglycemia is associated with AKI risk and may add some unique signal beyond anesthetic-choice variables, though still indirect.

Pre-anesthesia systolic blood pressure, reflecting baseline hemodynamic reserve and chronic hypertension/vascular disease. It can contribute modest unique baseline risk information for AKI, but is less proximal than postoperative perfusion measures.

Pre-anesthesia diastolic blood pressure, another baseline hemodynamic marker correlated with systolic BP. Likely redundant with pre_anesth_bp_sys and offers limited incremental value for sparse selection.

Pre-induction heart rate reflects baseline sympathetic tone/fitness and may correlate with frailty or hemodynamic reserve. It is an indirect, nonspecific severity marker and is usually dominated by postoperative hemodynamics/vasopressor signals for AKI prediction.

Use of a PA catheter is largely a clinician-choice proxy for perceived cardiac complexity or shock risk. It may weakly stratify AKI risk but is not a direct renal physiology signal and is prone to confounding by indication in sparse selection.

Maximum core temperature is a nonspecific perioperative stress/inflammation marker and often reflects warming practices rather than renal perfusion. It is unlikely to add unique predictive value for Hour-60 creatinine ratio compared with hemodynamics, fluids, and renal labs.

Inhaled nitric oxide use indicates pulmonary hypertension/RV strain and higher cardiopulmonary complexity. It can relate to renal congestion/low output but is an indirect treatment/proxy variable and typically redundant with direct RV function/hemodynamic features.

Total intraoperative crystalloid reflects volume loading/hemodilution and can contribute to renal congestion or signal hypotension/bleeding management. It has moderate predictive value but is confounded by practice patterns and overlaps with other fluid/blood loss and hemodynamic indicators.

Synthetic colloid volume captures a distinct exposure sometimes used in higher-risk/bleeding cases and may associate with AKI risk. It can add unique signal versus crystalloid/albumin totals, though still partly a clinician-behavior proxy.

Albumin administration reflects intravascular volume strategy and capillary leak/vasoplegia contexts that can affect renal perfusion and congestion. It may contribute incremental information beyond crystalloid, but remains treatment-pattern dependent and not a direct renal biomarker.

Intraoperative glucose trough reflects metabolic control and stress response, with weak associations to outcomes. It is indirect for AKI and likely dominated by hemodynamic/renal-perfusion variables in a sparse model.

Intraoperative vasodilator use suggests hypertension management or afterload reduction and can relate to hemodynamic lability. As a medication proxy it is confounded and generally less informative than direct blood pressure/vasopressor requirement for predicting AKI.

Intra-procedural EEG monitoring mainly reflects anesthesia/neurologic monitoring intensity and case complexity. It has minimal direct linkage to renal injury and is unlikely to survive sparse feature selection.

Preoperative/intraoperative TEE use is a procedural/monitoring choice indicating complexity or need for detailed cardiac assessment. It is an indirect proxy and typically redundant with the actual echo-derived functional measures (e.g., LVEF/RV function) for AKI risk.

Pre-incision LVEF estimates baseline LV systolic function and risk of low cardiac output, a key driver of renal hypoperfusion after cardiac surgery. It is a relatively direct physiologic risk marker and likely to add unique value beyond generic monitoring/procedure flags.

Baseline RV function captures propensity for venous congestion and low forward flow, both strongly linked to postoperative AKI. It provides complementary information to LVEF and is a strong sparse-model candidate.

Mitral regurgitation severity reflects chronic volume overload and can correlate with heart failure physiology. Its AKI signal is indirect and often mediated through LVEF/RV function and perioperative hemodynamics, making it less likely to be retained sparsely.

Mitral stenosis indicates elevated left atrial pressure/pulmonary hypertension risk and may relate to RV strain. It is relatively uncommon and its renal-risk contribution is usually captured better by RV function and hemodynamic variables.

Aortic regurgitation reflects chronic LV volume load and can influence perioperative hemodynamics. For AKI prediction it is indirect and typically redundant with LVEF and postoperative perfusion/vasopressor features.

Pre-incision aortic valve disease severity can relate to baseline hemodynamics and operative complexity. It is an indirect AKI risk proxy and likely dominated by more proximal post-op perfusion/vasopressor/renal markers in a sparse model.

Indicator that aortic valve area was assessed pre-incision (measurement/availability flag). This is largely a documentation/monitoring proxy with minimal unique physiologic signal for Hour-60 creatinine ratio.

Preoperative tricuspid regurgitation reflects right-sided dysfunction/venous congestion that can predispose to renal dysfunction. Still indirect and often redundant with post-op CVP/pressors/fluid balance signals, so lower sparse priority.
Presence of a preoperative PFO is generally unrelated to renal perfusion or AKI biology. Any association would be weak and mediated by procedure complexity, making it a poor sparse-model candidate.
Flag that the ascending aorta was assessed (imaging/TEE completeness). Primarily a process-of-care variable and unlikely to add unique predictive value for creatinine trajectory.

Ascending aortic size reflects aortopathy and may correlate with more complex surgery/circulatory arrest risk. It is a distal proxy for AKI risk and likely redundant with direct intra/post-op perfusion and hemodynamic features.

Aortic atheroma burden can indicate systemic vascular disease and embolic risk, which may weakly relate to renal outcomes. However it is indirect and typically outperformed by direct renal/perfusion markers for sparse selection.
Mobile atheroma suggests higher embolic potential and operative complexity. This is still an indirect pathway to AKI and likely not a top sparse predictor versus CPB/hemodynamics/fluids.

Whether the aortic arch was visualized is mainly an imaging completeness/process variable. It provides little direct physiologic information about renal injury risk and is unlikely to survive sparse screening.

Maximum arch atheroma severity reflects systemic atherosclerosis and potential embolic burden. It is an indirect risk marker and likely redundant with other comorbidity/complexity indicators, so modest priority.

Arch atheroma mobility indicates embolic risk and potentially more cautious/complex operative management. The link to creatinine rise is indirect and typically weaker than direct perfusion/renal-function features.

Use of cardiopulmonary bypass is a strong upstream driver of AKI risk via inflammation, hemolysis, and nonpulsatile flow. It is a high-yield, nonredundant surgical-exposure variable likely to persist in a sparse model.

Autologous priming/retrograde autologous priming reflects perfusion strategy affecting hemodilution and transfusion needs. It may carry some AKI signal but is a technique proxy and often redundant with fluid/transfusion/hemodynamic variables.

Perfusion-team crystalloid volume captures intraoperative hemodilution and fluid exposure during CPB, which can influence renal congestion and AKI risk. Useful but partially redundant with overall fluid balance and CPB-related variables.

Synthetic colloid exposure (if used) can be nephrotoxic or reflect attempts to manage oncotic pressure during CPB. It may add unique signal beyond crystalloids, but usage is often low/center-specific and can act as a practice-pattern proxy.

Albumin volume reflects oncotic resuscitation strategy and may correlate with capillary leak/illness severity and fluid management during CPB. Moderately informative but likely shares variance with other fluid and severity indicators.

Indicates hemofiltration/ultrafiltration performed on bypass, reflecting major fluid management needs and/or early renal vulnerability. This is a high-signal, relatively unique perioperative marker tightly linked to subsequent AKI risk and likely to survive sparse selection.

Captures difficulty weaning inotropes from cardiopulmonary bypass, a proxy for low cardiac output physiology. Predictive for renal hypoperfusion but indirect and partly redundant with other hemodynamic/vasopressor difficulty signals.

Represents vasopressor dependence/difficulty weaning off bypass, reflecting vasoplegia and hypotension risk. Stronger and more directly tied to renal perfusion pressure than many generic severity markers, with good sparse-model value in this batch.

Whether/extent a post-bypass TEE was performed is largely clinician-behavior/monitoring intensity and case complexity. It is an indirect proxy and likely redundant with explicit cardiac function variables, so low priority for sparsity.

Presence of SAM suggests dynamic LVOT obstruction that can cause episodic hypotension. Clinically relevant but relatively uncommon and typically captured downstream by vasopressor/inotrope difficulty, making it a weaker sparse predictor here.

Return-to-CPB/echo-related event reflects intraoperative instability or technical issues. It is a coarse, heterogeneous proxy for severity and likely overlaps with vasopressor/inotrope wean difficulty and lactate.

Baseline intraoperative LV systolic function estimate relates to cardiac output reserve and risk of low-flow states. Useful context but indirect for AKI and often less predictive than direct perfusion/vasopressor dependence signals.

Postoperative RV dysfunction can drive low cardiac output and venous congestion, both relevant to AKI. Moderately useful but somewhat redundant with global hemodynamic support requirements and lactate.

Intraoperative cardiac arrhythmia indicates instability and potential low-flow episodes. It is nonspecific and typically mediated through more proximal predictors (vasopressors, lactate), so lower sparse priority.

Immediate postoperative core temperature reflects rewarming strategy and perioperative management more than renal physiology. Weak and noisy for Hour-60 creatinine ratio prediction compared with perfusion and metabolic markers.

Post-op INR reflects coagulopathy/hemodilution and transfusion burden, which can correlate with complexity. It is indirect for AKI and likely redundant with other severity markers (platelets, fibrinogen, hematocrit).
Post-op WBC is a nonspecific stress/inflammation marker with high perioperative variability. Generally weak for sparse prediction of later creatinine ratio versus perfusion/metabolic indicators.
Platelet count can reflect hemodilution/consumption and overall surgical complexity. Indirect for renal injury and overlaps with other coagulation/hemodilution labs, so modest priority.
Hematocrit reflects hemodilution/bleeding/transfusion and oxygen delivery capacity. Some relevance but largely a proxy and often redundant with lactate and coagulopathy markers for sparse selection.
Fibrinogen reflects inflammatory state and bleeding/coagulopathy management. Indirect for AKI and likely redundant with INR/platelets as a general complexity marker.

Postoperative lactate is a strong marker of global hypoperfusion/low cardiac output and correlates with renal ischemic risk. High-yield physiologic signal with good unique predictive value in a sparse model for later creatinine rise.

Postoperative propofol exposure is mainly a sedation/ventilation management marker rather than a direct renal physiology signal. It may weakly proxy illness severity or hemodynamic instability but is likely redundant with more direct perfusion/AKI predictors.

Pain score on POD3 is temporally misaligned with features limited to 36h and is largely a comfort/analgesia marker. It is unlikely to add unique predictive value for Hour-60 creatinine ratio in a sparse model.

Current smoking reflects baseline vascular/pulmonary risk and may correlate with perioperative complications that affect kidneys. However it is an indirect, static comorbidity signal and usually dominated by perioperative hemodynamics/labs for AKI prediction.

Former smoking is a weaker, more heterogeneous baseline risk marker than current smoking. It is indirect and unlikely to survive sparse selection once stronger perioperative renal/perfusion signals are available.

Paroxysmal AF indicates underlying cardiac disease and risk of low cardiac output/embolism that can contribute to renal hypoperfusion. It can add some baseline risk information but remains indirect relative to postoperative hemodynamic and renal markers.

Persistent AF more strongly reflects chronic atrial/ventricular dysfunction and potential for perioperative low output affecting renal perfusion. Still a baseline proxy and likely secondary to direct postoperative perfusion/AKI indicators in sparse selection.

Antegrade cardioplegia delivery is primarily a surgical technique variable and not a direct kidney injury marker. Any association with AKI is likely weak and confounded by procedure complexity, making it low priority for sparse models.
Retrograde cardioplegia is another technique/complexity proxy and does not directly encode renal physiology. It is unlikely to add unique predictive value beyond other operative severity variables.

Blood cardioplegia type reflects operative preference/strategy and only indirectly relates to systemic inflammation or perfusion. It is a weak, likely redundant proxy for surgical complexity rather than a strong AKI predictor.

Crystalloid cardioplegia type is similarly a technique variable with limited direct linkage to postoperative renal dysfunction. It is low-yield for sparse selection compared with hemodynamics, fluids, and renal labs.

Acute heart failure timing suggests recent decompensation and higher risk of perioperative low cardiac output and renal hypoperfusion. This baseline context can add unique predictive value for AKI trajectory in very elderly cardiac surgery patients.

Chronic heart failure indicates reduced renal reserve and susceptibility to congestion/hypoperfusion. It is useful baseline risk information but may be partially redundant with more specific HF type variables and postoperative hemodynamics.

Systolic HF (reduced EF phenotype) is strongly linked to low forward flow and perioperative renal hypoperfusion risk. It is a high-priority baseline predictor likely to survive sparse screening when direct postoperative renal markers are limited.

Diastolic HF relates to congestion and sensitivity to volume shifts, which can contribute to AKI risk. Predictive value is moderate and often less specific than systolic HF for low-output renal injury, so slightly lower priority.

Mean SpO2 in the immediate 0–12h window is often influenced by transient postoperative factors (ventilation, atelectasis) and is not kidney-specific. As a mean aggregation it can miss hypoxemic extremes and is a weak, indirect proxy for AKI risk.

Mean SpO2 at 13–24h is more stable than 0–12h and may reflect ongoing respiratory failure that can worsen renal perfusion. Still indirect and mean-based (not capturing hypoxemia extremes), so only modest sparse-model priority.

Average SpO2 in the 25–36h window reflects overall oxygenation but smooths over brief hypoxemic insults. Likely redundant with SpO2 minima/maxima and generally an indirect AKI predictor, so low sparse-selection priority.

Peak SpO2 in the immediate 0–12h post-op period mostly reflects supplemental oxygen/ventilator settings rather than renal-relevant physiology. Highly non-specific and dominated by hypoxemia extremes, so very weak for sparse selection.
Peak SpO2 at 13–24h is largely a care/FiO2 artifact and provides little unique signal about renal hypoperfusion or evolving AKI. Redundant and low value compared with SpO2 minima.

Peak SpO2 closest to the target window still mainly captures oxygen delivery/FiO2 titration rather than pathologic insult. Adds minimal unique predictive value for creatinine ratio versus hypoxemia minima and other hemodynamic features.

Minimum SpO2 in 0–12h captures early hypoxemic episodes that could contribute to renal ischemia, but this period is noisy post-bypass/anesthesia. Some potential signal, yet timing and confounding by ventilation make it a weak sparse-model keeper.

Minimum SpO2 in 13–24h better reflects clinically meaningful hypoxemia after immediate post-op instability. Still an indirect AKI driver and may be redundant with stronger perfusion/vasopressor signals, but could survive screening if few direct renal markers exist.

Minimum SpO2 in 25–36h is the closest window and best captures late hypoxemic insults that may precede AKI. Indirect and potentially confounded by respiratory management, but among SpO2 features this is the most plausible sparse-model candidate.
Count/frequency of SpO2 measurements in 0–12h is primarily a monitoring-intensity/acuity proxy rather than physiology. High redundancy with ICU workflow and weak direct link to creatinine rise.
SpO2 measurement frequency at 13–24h reflects clinician behavior and device sampling rather than oxygenation burden. Low unique predictive value for AKI in a sparse linear model.

SpO2 measurement frequency at 25–36h is still mainly a proxy for monitoring/respiratory support complexity. Not a direct renal signal and unlikely to be selected when physiologic extremes are available.

Mean right pulmonary artery pressure (PAP) early post-op reflects RV afterload/volume status and can relate to venous congestion affecting kidneys. However 0–12h is noisy and the mean is less informative than extremes, so modest priority.

Mean PAP at 13–24h may capture sustained pulmonary hypertension/RV dysfunction contributing to renal venous congestion and low forward flow. Useful but partially redundant with PAP maxima and other hemodynamic markers, so mid-tier.

Mean PAP at 25–36h is temporally closest and may reflect ongoing RV strain/volume overload relevant to AKI risk. Still a smoothed measure and likely secondary to stronger congestion/perfusion variables, so moderate score.

Peak PAP in 0–12h captures acute RV afterload/volume spikes that can impair renal perfusion via low output or congestion. Early timing limits reliability, but the max is the best aggregation within this epoch.

Peak PAP at 13–24h captures clinically meaningful episodes of RV failure/pulmonary hypertension and venous congestion, which are plausible drivers of subsequent creatinine rise. Less redundant than means and in a more informative window, making it a strong sparse-model candidate.

Peak PAP at 25–36h is closest to Hour 60 and best captures late hemodynamic congestion/afterload insults that can precede AKI. Among PAP features this extreme in the latest window is most likely to add unique predictive value.

Minimum mean PAP in the immediate post-op window reflects early RV/pulmonary vascular load and congestion that can contribute to renal venous hypertension. However it is indirect for AKI and early (0–12h) is noisy; later epochs would be preferred in a sparse model.

Minimum mean PAP at 13–24h may better reflect evolving RV dysfunction/volume status than 0–12h. Still an indirect renal signal and typically dominated by more direct perfusion/congestion markers (e.g., CVP) in sparse selection.

Minimum mean PAP closest to the prediction horizon can capture persistent pulmonary hypertension/RV strain contributing to low forward flow and renal congestion. Among PAP-min variants this is the most temporally relevant, but remains a secondary/indirect AKI predictor.

Count/frequency of PAP mean measurements is mainly a monitoring-intensity/illness-severity proxy (PA catheter presence/attention). It adds little unique physiologic information and is likely redundant with actual hemodynamic values.
Measurement frequency of PAP in 13–24h reflects clinician behavior and device use more than renal physiology. Low priority for sparse modeling compared with hemodynamic extremes/levels.

PAP measurement frequency at 25–36h is still primarily a proxy for monitoring intensity and ongoing instability. Even though later timing is closer to Hour 60, it is unlikely to add unique predictive value beyond actual pressures and other severity markers.

Frequency of continuous cardiac index device readings reflects use of advanced monitoring rather than cardiac output itself. As a clinician-behavior proxy in the noisy immediate post-op window, it is a weak sparse-model candidate.

Measurement frequency of continuous cardiac index at 13–24h indicates monitoring intensity/complexity, not direct renal perfusion. Likely redundant with actual hemodynamics and other acuity indicators.

Ongoing frequent cardiac index monitoring at 25–36h may weakly signal persistent shock/low-output concern, which relates to AKI risk. Still largely a proxy variable and would usually lose to direct measures (CI values, MAP, lactate, urine output) in sparse selection.

Mean CVP in 0–12h reflects venous congestion and right-sided filling pressures, which can impair renal perfusion via elevated renal venous pressure. Useful but early post-op values can be confounded by ventilation/fluid shifts and may be redundant with later CVP.
Mean CVP at 13–24h captures more stable congestion burden than 0–12h and is mechanistically linked to AKI after cardiac surgery. Still somewhat redundant with CVP max (extremes) and later epoch CVP.

Mean CVP at 25–36h is a strong near-term marker of sustained venous congestion, a key driver of postoperative AKI in cardiac surgery patients. Later timing increases relevance to Hour 60 and it can add unique value beyond generic severity markers.

Peak CVP in 0–12h can capture acute congestion episodes that may contribute to renal injury. However early peaks may reflect transient post-bypass/ventilator effects, reducing reliability versus later peaks.

Peak CVP at 13–24h captures clinically important congestion extremes more sensitively than the mean and is plausibly predictive of evolving AKI. Strong candidate, though partially redundant with 25–36h CVP metrics.

Peak CVP at 25–36h is the most temporally proximate and extreme-based congestion signal in this batch, closely tied to renal venous hypertension and low effective renal perfusion. Highest priority among these features for sparse selection.

Minimum CVP in 0–12h may reflect relative hypovolemia/underfilling, but it is less directly linked to AKI than congestion (high CVP) and is prone to early post-op artifact. Likely dominated by CVP max/mean in later windows.

Minimum CVP in 13–24h reflects venous congestion/right-sided filling pressures that can impair renal perfusion. Moderately useful but somewhat redundant with the closer 25–36h CVP_min and influenced by ventilation/volume management.

Minimum CVP in 25–36h is a relatively proximal marker of venous congestion/volume status affecting renal perfusion before the 60h creatinine. Among CVP summaries, this is the most time-relevant and likely to add unique signal for AKI risk.

Count/frequency of CVP measurements in 0–12h mainly reflects monitoring intensity and early post-op workflow rather than physiology. Weak direct linkage to later creatinine and dominated by actual CVP values.
CVP measurement frequency in 13–24h is largely a clinician-behavior/acuity proxy. Unlikely to survive sparse selection versus CVP_min itself or other hemodynamic variables.

CVP measurement frequency in 25–36h may weakly proxy ongoing instability/concern closer to the outcome window. Still indirect and typically redundant with the CVP_min value and other severity markers.
Mean FiO2 in 0–12h reflects early ventilator support and post-bypass oxygenation practices. Early values are noisy and largely a care-pattern proxy with limited unique renal predictive value.

Mean FiO2 in 13–24h captures sustained oxygen requirement/respiratory dysfunction, which correlates with overall illness severity. Indirect for AKI and likely redundant with FiO2_max or other severity/hemodynamic features.

Mean FiO2 in 25–36h is closer to the 60h outcome and reflects ongoing respiratory support needs. Still an indirect severity marker and usually less informative than an extreme (FiO2_max) for sparse selection.

Max FiO2 in 0–12h captures episodes of high oxygen requirement but is confounded by immediate post-op management and transient instability. Some severity signal, but early timing limits specificity for 60h creatinine.

Max FiO2 in 13–24h reflects significant respiratory compromise and systemic stress that can co-occur with renal hypoperfusion/inflammation. More informative than FiO2_mean/min and less noisy than 0–12h, so a moderate sparse-model candidate.

Max FiO2 in 25–36h is the most time-proximal extreme oxygen requirement and can indicate ongoing critical illness associated with AKI risk. Still indirect and partly clinician-driven, so not top-tier but may add unique severity signal.
Minimum FiO2 in 0–12h mostly reflects weaning practices and baseline ventilator settings rather than organ dysfunction. Highly redundant with mean/max and weakly related to later creatinine.
Minimum FiO2 in 13–24h is primarily a ventilation/weaning proxy and less pathophysiologically informative than FiO2_max. Unlikely to add unique predictive value in a sparse model.

Minimum FiO2 in 25–36h reflects lowest support achieved and is strongly influenced by clinician weaning decisions. Generally dominated by FiO2_max/mean for capturing respiratory severity relevant to AKI risk.
FiO2 measurement frequency in 0–12h is a monitoring/workflow artifact (ventilator charting density) rather than a physiologic biomarker. Very weak candidate for sparse selection.

FiO2 measurement frequency in 13–24h mainly reflects documentation/monitoring intensity and ventilator management. Adds little unique information beyond FiO2 values themselves and other severity indicators.

Count/frequency of FiO2 documentation in 25–36h, mainly reflecting monitoring intensity and ventilator management rather than renal physiology. Weak, proxy-heavy signal and unlikely to survive sparse selection for creatinine ratio.

Average set ventilator respiratory rate in the immediate 0–12h post-op period. Mostly reflects ventilation strategy/anesthesia effects and is early/noisy and indirect for AKI, so low sparse-model priority.
Average set respiratory rate during 13–24h. Still largely clinician-driven and nonspecific for renal hypoperfusion; likely redundant with other severity markers and not a strong keeper.

Peak set respiratory rate in 0–12h may capture episodes of acidosis/respiratory distress. However it is early and largely management-driven, so only a weak indirect predictor of later creatinine rise.
Peak set respiratory rate in 13–24h can reflect evolving metabolic acidosis/severity that may correlate with AKI risk. Still indirect and clinician-mediated, so modest value only.

Minimum set respiratory rate in 0–12h is typically sedation/vent mode related. Provides little unique information about renal injury and is dominated by more direct hemodynamic/renal features (not in this batch).
Minimum set respiratory rate in 13–24h mainly reflects weaning/sedation practices. Indirect and low-yield for predicting creatinine ratio, with high redundancy to other ventilation settings.
Measurement frequency of set respiratory rate in 0–12h is a documentation/monitoring proxy. Not a physiologic biomarker and unlikely to add unique predictive value in a sparse model.
Measurement frequency of set respiratory rate in 13–24h reflects care intensity rather than kidney function. Weak proxy signal and low priority for sparse selection.

Measurement frequency of set respiratory rate in 25–36h is still primarily a monitoring/EMR artifact. Despite closer timing, it remains an indirect proxy and not a strong predictor of creatinine ratio.
Average set tidal volume in 0–12h reflects ventilator strategy and body size rather than renal perfusion. Early post-op and indirect, so minimal sparse-model value.
Average set tidal volume in 13–24h is largely protocolized and clinician-driven. Weak and nonspecific for AKI prediction, and likely redundant with other respiratory support indicators.
Average set tidal volume in 25–36h may weakly reflect ongoing respiratory failure/ARDS severity that can co-occur with AKI. Still indirect and management-dependent, so only modest priority.
Maximum set tidal volume in 0–12h is typically determined by initial ventilator settings and transient post-op conditions. Not a direct renal signal and early/noisy, so low priority.

Peak set tidal volume in 13–24h could reflect less lung-protective ventilation or changing compliance, a general severity marker. Indirect for AKI and likely not selected unless respiratory variables are otherwise absent.

Peak set tidal volume in 25–36h is the closest window and may capture ongoing respiratory failure/ventilator dependence associated with multi-organ dysfunction. Still a clinician-driven, indirect proxy for renal injury, so moderate at best.

Minimum set tidal volume in the immediate post-op window reflects ventilator strategy/body size rather than renal physiology. Weak, indirect signal for AKI and likely dominated by hemodynamics/renal labs in sparse selection.

Minimum set tidal volume during 13–24h is mainly a ventilation management choice and only loosely related to renal outcomes. Adds little unique predictive value for creatinine ratio compared with perfusion/renal markers.

Minimum set tidal volume at 25–36h may weakly proxy ongoing respiratory failure/ARDS severity, which can correlate with AKI risk. Still indirect and likely redundant with stronger severity/perfusion features, so low priority.
Count/frequency of tidal volume documentation is a monitoring-intensity/EMR artifact rather than a physiologic biomarker. Poor direct relevance and low sparse-model value.
Measurement frequency of set tidal volume reflects clinician behavior and charting density more than kidney injury risk. Unlikely to survive sparse feature selection.
Measurement frequency at 25–36h is still primarily a proxy for monitoring intensity/ventilator management complexity. Not a direct renal signal and low unique predictive value.

Mean PEEP early post-op reflects oxygenation strategy and cardiopulmonary status; higher PEEP can affect venous return and renal perfusion but effects are indirect. Early window is noisy and mean smooths extremes, so limited sparse-model priority.

Mean PEEP at 13–24h may capture sustained respiratory failure severity, which correlates with AKI risk. Still indirect and likely redundant with a more informative extreme (max) and with hemodynamic variables.

Mean PEEP at 25–36h is closer to the Hour-60 target and can reflect sustained high intrathoracic pressure/critical illness that may contribute to renal hypoperfusion. Moderate value, but still an indirect severity proxy and partly redundant with PEEP max.

Maximum PEEP in 0–12h captures acute respiratory support intensity, which can associate with shock/AKI risk. However, immediate post-op instability is common and may be transient, limiting predictive specificity for Hour-60 creatinine.

Maximum PEEP at 13–24h better captures clinically meaningful respiratory failure severity than the mean and may relate to renal hypoperfusion via hemodynamic effects. Moderate sparse-model candidate but still indirect.

Maximum PEEP at 25–36h is the most informative aggregation/timing here, reflecting ongoing high ventilatory support near the prediction horizon and potential hemodynamic compromise. Strongest candidate in this batch as a severity/hemodynamic stress marker.

Minimum PEEP in the immediate post-op period often reflects baseline ventilator settings rather than pathologic extremes. Low specificity for AKI and dominated by PEEP max/mean if any ventilatory signal is retained.
Minimum PEEP at 13–24h is typically a floor setting and less informative than maximum PEEP for capturing respiratory failure severity. Likely redundant and low priority for sparse selection.

Minimum PEEP at 25–36h may weakly indicate readiness to wean (lower severity), but it is still less informative than PEEP max for capturing insults. Some limited complementary value, but generally redundant.
PEEP measurement frequency is mainly a proxy for monitoring intensity and ventilator management activity. Not a direct physiologic renal predictor and unlikely to add unique value in a sparse model.
Count of PEEP documentation in 13–24h is mainly a monitoring/intensity proxy rather than physiology. Unlikely to add unique AKI signal in a sparse model.

Measurement frequency of PEEP in 25–36h reflects ongoing ventilator management/acuity and clinician attention. Slightly more informative due to proximity to Hour 60, but still largely a proxy and low priority.

Average plateau pressure in the immediate 0–12h post-op window reflects early ventilator mechanics and post-bypass physiology. Likely noisy and indirect for renal outcomes, and dominated by later/peak signals.

Mean plateau pressure at 13–24h may capture sustained lung stiffness/ventilator intensity that correlates with illness severity. Still indirect for AKI and less informative than extremes, so modest sparse-model priority.

Peak plateau pressure in 0–12h can reflect acute high intrathoracic pressures that may reduce venous return/renal perfusion. However early post-op values are often transient and less predictive than later trajectory.

Max plateau pressure in 13–24h captures episodes of high intrathoracic pressure/poor compliance, a plausible contributor to renal hypoperfusion and severity. Among plateau summaries here, this extreme is the most likely to survive sparse selection.
Minimum plateau pressure in 0–12h mostly reflects periods of low support or measurement variability rather than a renal insult. Adds little unique predictive value for creatinine rise.
Minimum plateau pressure in 13–24h is not a clear pathophysiologic driver of AKI and is hard to interpret clinically. Redundant/low-signal compared with max plateau pressure.
Frequency of plateau pressure measurements in 0–12h is primarily a clinician-behavior/monitoring proxy. Early post-op measurement intensity is common and not specific for later AKI.
Plateau pressure measurement count in 13–24h reflects monitoring intensity and ventilator management complexity. Weak, indirect, and unlikely to be retained in a sparse model.
More frequent plateau measurements in 25–36h may indicate persistent respiratory failure and higher acuity closer to the outcome window. Still a proxy variable, so only low-to-modest priority.

Mean minute ventilation in 0–12h reflects early ventilator settings and metabolic demand immediately post-op. This is indirect for kidney injury and likely noisy/redundant with later severity markers.

Average minute ventilation in 13–24h can reflect sustained hypercapnia compensation, dead space, or metabolic stress (severity proxy). Indirect for AKI and less informative than extremes, so modest score.
Peak minute ventilation in 0–12h may capture acute metabolic/respiratory stress episodes. Early timing limits reliability for Hour 60 creatinine, so it’s a weaker sparse-model candidate.

Max minute ventilation in 13–24h captures episodes of high ventilatory demand (stress/sepsis/ARDS proxy) that can correlate with evolving multiorgan dysfunction including AKI. Among minute ventilation features here, this extreme is the most selection-worthy.
Minimum minute ventilation in 0–12h is often driven by sedation/weaning or brief ventilator changes rather than renal-relevant physiology. Low unique predictive value and likely redundant/noisy.

Minimum minute ventilation in 13–24h reflects lowest ventilatory demand/support and can proxy respiratory failure/sedation. It is an indirect illness-severity signal for AKI and likely dominated by hemodynamics/renal-specific features, so low sparse-model priority.

Count/frequency of minute ventilation measurements in 0–12h is mainly monitoring intensity/ventilator management behavior. This is a weak proxy and early post-op noise, so unlikely to survive sparse selection.

Measurement frequency of minute ventilation in 13–24h reflects clinician attention and ventilator documentation rather than physiology. As a behavior/complexity proxy it adds little unique predictive value for creatinine ratio.

Measurement frequency of minute ventilation in 25–36h may capture ongoing ventilator dependence/ICU complexity closer to the outcome window. Still largely a monitoring proxy and redundant with direct respiratory/hemodynamic variables, so modest at best.

Average oxygen delivery device level in 0–12h reflects early post-op respiratory support. It is indirect for renal injury and early values are often routine/transition-related, making it low value for sparse selection.

Mean oxygen device level in 13–24h indicates sustained respiratory support needs. This is still a nonspecific severity marker and likely redundant with more direct oxygenation/ventilation metrics, so low-moderate priority.

Mean oxygen device level in 25–36h captures persistent respiratory support closer to Hour 60 and may correlate with ongoing shock/inflammation affecting kidneys. Among device summaries, this timing makes it the most plausible keeper, though still indirect.
