Maximum oxygen device level in 0–12h captures peak early respiratory support (e.g., intubation/high-flow). Extremes can reflect acute severity, but early post-op support is common and may be transient, limiting unique AKI prediction.

Maximum oxygen device level in 13–24h reflects episodes of escalated respiratory support beyond immediate post-op routine. This can proxy complications and systemic illness that track with AKI risk, making it a moderate sparse-model candidate.

Maximum oxygen device level in 25–36h captures late escalation/persistence of high respiratory support close to the prediction horizon. As an extreme in the closest window, it is the strongest respiratory-support proxy in this batch and most likely to add signal.

Minimum oxygen device level in 0–12h largely reflects brief periods on low support or documentation artifacts during transitions. It is weakly related to renal outcomes and dominated by max/late-window measures.

Minimum oxygen device level in 13–24h indicates the lowest support achieved, which is less informative than escalation extremes for predicting AKI. Likely redundant with max/mean device measures and overall trajectory.

Minimum oxygen device level in 25–36h may reflect ability to wean respiratory support closer to Hour 60. Still less informative than max/mean for capturing decompensation and is a relatively indirect renal predictor.
Frequency of oxygen device documentation in 0–12h is primarily a workflow/monitoring proxy. Early post-op charting intensity is high and not a direct physiologic renal signal, so very low priority.
Oxygen device measurement frequency in 13–24h reflects care intensity rather than oxygenation status itself. Adds little unique value compared with actual device level (max/mean), so low score.

Oxygen device measurement frequency in 25–36h may weakly indicate ongoing respiratory instability/ICU complexity near the outcome window. Still a clinician-behavior proxy and generally dominated by max/mean device level features.

Average early postoperative chest tube output reflects bleeding/volume loss and potential hemodynamic stress. It is an indirect AKI risk signal and likely redundant with more informative extremes and later windows for sparse selection.
Mean chest tube output in 13–24h captures ongoing bleeding/drainage burden. Still indirect for renal injury and typically dominated by max output and closer (25–36h) physiology in a sparse model.

Mean chest tube output in 25–36h reflects persistent bleeding/drainage closer to the creatinine outcome window. More temporally relevant than earlier means, but remains an indirect proxy for renal hypoperfusion and may be redundant with max output.

Peak chest tube output in 0–12h captures acute bleeding that can drive hypotension/transfusion and renal hypoperfusion. Extremes are more informative than means, but early post-op noise and downstream management reduce unique predictive value.

Peak chest tube output in 13–24h indicates significant ongoing bleeding with potential hemodynamic compromise. Useful as an extreme-value insult marker, though still indirect and partly redundant with later (25–36h) peaks.

Peak chest tube output in 25–36h is a near-term marker of ongoing bleeding/instability closest to Hour 60, which can precede AKI via hypoperfusion and transfusion burden. Among chest-tube features, this extreme and late window is most likely to survive sparse selection.

Minimum chest tube output in 0–12h is usually near zero and reflects periods of low drainage rather than a renal-relevant insult. It adds little unique information and is dominated by max/mean summaries.
Minimum chest tube output in 13–24h is typically uninformative (often zero) and not a direct physiologic driver of AKI. Redundant and unlikely to be selected in a sparse model.

Minimum chest tube output in 25–36h mainly reflects intermittent low drainage and provides little signal about bleeding severity or renal perfusion. Dominated by max output and other hemodynamic variables.

Count/frequency of chest tube output measurements is primarily a monitoring/ICU workflow proxy. It is not a direct biomarker and is unlikely to add stable predictive value beyond actual output values.
Measurement frequency reflects clinician attention and documentation patterns more than physiology. As a proxy variable it is low priority for sparse selection.
Measurement frequency in 25–36h is still mainly a care-intensity proxy rather than a renal injury signal. Typically redundant with severity markers and inferior to the actual output extremes.

Mean systolic pulmonary artery pressure early post-op reflects RV/pulmonary vascular load and can relate to low cardiac output states affecting renal perfusion. However, early values are noisy and mean aggregation may miss transient hemodynamic insults.

Mean systolic PAP in 13–24h better reflects evolving RV/pulmonary hypertension physiology that can contribute to venous congestion and reduced renal perfusion. Moderately useful but indirect and potentially redundant with more direct perfusion/vasopressor signals.

Peak systolic PAP in 0–12h captures acute RV afterload spikes that may precipitate low output and renal hypoperfusion. Extremes are informative, but early post-op instability and treatment effects limit uniqueness versus later-window hemodynamics.

Peak systolic pulmonary artery pressure (PAP) in 13–24h reflects RV afterload/pulmonary hypertension and venous congestion risk. Indirect for AKI and likely redundant with other hemodynamic/volume signals; mid-window max is only a weak sparse-model keeper.

Peak systolic PAP in 25–36h captures later RV/pulmonary vascular stress closer to the 60h creatinine outcome. Still indirect, but the late-window extreme may add some unique congestion-related signal, making it a moderate candidate within this batch.

Minimum systolic PAP in 0–12h is heavily influenced by immediate post-bypass/anesthesia effects and measurement artifacts. Early minima are noisy and not a direct renal perfusion marker, so low priority for sparse selection.

Minimum systolic PAP in 13–24h may reflect low RV output/low pulmonary pressures that could correlate with systemic hypoperfusion. It is still an indirect proxy and generally less informative than later-window extremes, so only weak-to-moderate value.

Minimum systolic PAP in 25–36h could indicate low RV output/low flow state closer to the prediction horizon. As a late-window extreme it may carry some unique hypoperfusion signal, but remains indirect for kidney injury.

Count/frequency of systolic PAP measurements in 0–12h is mainly a monitoring-intensity/line-presence proxy. Early measurement frequency is not a physiologic renal signal and is dominated by actual pressure values.

Frequency of systolic PAP measurements in 13–24h reflects clinician behavior and ongoing invasive monitoring rather than kidney physiology. Low unique predictive value for creatinine ratio in a sparse model.

Frequency of systolic PAP measurements in 25–36h may weakly proxy persistent hemodynamic instability/complexity closer to outcome time. Still largely a behavior/monitoring signal and typically dominated by the pressure extremes themselves.

Mean diastolic PAP in 0–12h reflects average pulmonary vascular/venous pressures during a highly transient post-op period. Means smooth short insults and early values are noisy, so low sparse-selection priority.

Mean diastolic PAP in 13–24h can reflect sustained pulmonary pressures and potential venous congestion. However, it is redundant with max/min extremes and remains an indirect AKI predictor, so only modest value.

Mean diastolic PAP in 25–36h captures sustained later pulmonary/venous pressure burden closer to the 60h creatinine measurement. Compared with earlier means it is more temporally relevant, but still somewhat redundant with diastolic max.

Peak diastolic PAP in 0–12h may indicate early pulmonary hypertension/venous congestion, which can contribute to renal venous congestion. Early post-op peaks are less reliable and often transient, so not a top sparse feature.

Peak diastolic PAP in 13–24h is a better extreme marker of congestion/RV strain than the mean and may relate to renal venous congestion. Mid-window timing is reasonably informative but still indirect, giving moderate priority.

Peak diastolic PAP in 25–36h is the strongest within-batch marker of late venous congestion/RV failure physiology closest to the 60h outcome. As a late-window extreme it is the most likely PAP-derived feature to survive sparse selection for AKI prediction.
Minimum diastolic PAP in 0–12h is susceptible to immediate post-op hemodynamic swings and measurement noise. It is an indirect and low-quality signal for later creatinine rise.

Minimum diastolic PAP in 13–24h may reflect low-flow/low filling pressures that could correlate with hypoperfusion. Still indirect and generally less informative than late-window maxima for congestion-related AKI risk.

Minimum diastolic pulmonary artery pressure at 25–36h reflects RV afterload/volume status and can relate to venous congestion affecting renal perfusion. Moderately useful but indirect for AKI and likely dominated by more direct renal/hemodynamic features not in this batch.
Count/frequency of diastolic PAP measurements in 0–12h is mainly a monitoring-intensity/line-presence proxy. Early post-op and behavior-driven, so low unique predictive value for creatinine ratio.
Measurement frequency of diastolic PAP in 13–24h reflects clinician concern and device availability more than physiology. Weak and redundant as a proxy signal for sparse selection.

Measurement frequency of diastolic PAP in 25–36h may weakly proxy ongoing hemodynamic instability closer to the outcome window. Still primarily clinician-behavior/monitoring intensity, so only a modest score.
Average heart rate in the immediate 0–12h post-op period reflects early stress, pacing, pain, or inotrope use. Mean smooths extremes and early window is noisy, so limited sparse-model priority.
Average heart rate at 13–24h captures more stable illness trajectory than 0–12h but remains a nonspecific severity marker. Likely redundant with more informative extremes and later-window vitals.

Average heart rate at 25–36h is closer to Hour 60 and may reflect sustained sympathetic drive/low output states relevant to renal hypoperfusion. Still indirect and less informative than extremes, but the best mean-HR candidate in this batch.
Peak heart rate in 0–12h may indicate acute stress/arrhythmia but is often transient and influenced by immediate post-bypass factors. Early timing and nonspecificity reduce sparse selection value.

Peak heart rate at 13–24h can capture clinically meaningful tachyarrhythmia or compensatory response that may accompany low output and renal risk. More informative than mean HR, but still an indirect proxy.

Peak heart rate at 25–36h is a near-term marker of ongoing instability (e.g., AF with RVR, shock physiology) that can precede AKI by hypoperfusion/congestion pathways. Among pulse features, this extreme and closest window offers the strongest unique predictive signal.
Minimum heart rate in 0–12h may reflect pacing, sedation, or transient bradyarrhythmia rather than renal physiology. Early post-op confounding and indirectness limit usefulness.
Minimum heart rate at 13–24h can indicate bradyarrhythmia or heavy sedation, which are weakly related to renal outcomes. Generally less informative than tachycardic extremes for AKI prediction.

Minimum heart rate at 25–36h may capture clinically significant bradycardia/pacing dependence that can reduce cardiac output and renal perfusion. Still indirect, but timing makes it a moderate candidate.
Heart rate measurement frequency in 0–12h is largely routine ICU monitoring and reflects documentation density more than physiology. Very low unique value for sparse prediction.
Heart rate measurement frequency in 13–24h is a monitoring/EMR artifact with minimal physiologic specificity. Unlikely to survive sparse feature selection.

Heart rate measurement frequency in 25–36h remains primarily a proxy for monitoring intensity rather than a renal-risk biomarker. Adds little beyond actual pulse values and is not a strong sparse-model feature.

Mean inspired O2 fraction/oxygen agent exposure in the immediate 0–12h post-op window. Largely reflects ventilator/anesthesia management and illness severity rather than a direct renal signal, and is likely redundant with other oxygenation/hemodynamic features.

Peak oxygen agent/O2 exposure in 0–12h, which may mark transient respiratory failure or high support. Extremes can capture acuity, but it is an indirect proxy and early post-op values are noisy for predicting Hour-60 creatinine ratio.

Lowest oxygen agent/O2 exposure in 0–12h, often reflecting weaning or routine settings. Provides little unique information about evolving AKI risk and is dominated by more direct perfusion/renal markers.
Count/frequency of documenting oxygen agent/O2 in 0–12h. Primarily a monitoring/clinician-behavior proxy and not a physiologic biomarker for kidney dysfunction.
Measurement frequency of oxygen agent/O2 in 13–24h. Still mainly reflects monitoring intensity and care complexity rather than a direct or unique predictor of creatinine rise.

Measurement frequency of oxygen agent/O2 in 25–36h, closer to the Hour-60 target and may weakly proxy ongoing ventilatory support/instability. However it remains a clinician-behavior signal and is unlikely to survive sparse selection versus direct renal/hemodynamic features.
Average nitrous oxide exposure in 0–12h, reflecting anesthetic practice rather than renal physiology. Minimal expected unique predictive value for AKI/creatinine trajectory in a sparse model.
Peak nitrous oxide exposure in 0–12h. Not a recognized driver or near-term marker of postoperative AKI and is likely noise/redundant with broader anesthesia variables.
Minimum nitrous oxide exposure in 0–12h (often zero), mainly indicating whether it was used. Offers negligible discriminatory value for Hour-60 creatinine ratio.
Frequency of recording nitrous oxide in 0–12h. Pure monitoring/documentation proxy with little physiologic linkage to renal outcomes.
Frequency of recording nitrous oxide in 13–24h. Nitrous oxide is typically not a key ICU exposure; measurement frequency is unlikely to add unique predictive value.
Frequency of recording nitrous oxide in 25–36h. Remains a weak documentation proxy and is unlikely to contribute meaningfully in sparse selection.
Mean medical air fraction/exposure in 0–12h, essentially complementary to FiO2 settings. Indirect and highly redundant with oxygen-related variables, with little direct renal relevance.
Maximum air fraction/exposure in 0–12h, reflecting ventilator gas-mixing choices. Not a direct AKI precursor and redundant with oxygen agent measures.

Minimum air fraction/exposure in 0–12h, largely the inverse of high FiO2 periods. Provides little unique information for predicting creatinine ratio and is dominated by more direct perfusion/renal indicators.
Measurement frequency of air fraction/exposure in 0–12h. Primarily a documentation/monitoring intensity proxy and not expected to survive sparse-model feature selection.
Count/frequency of recorded “air” as an anesthetic agent is essentially a documentation/monitoring artifact. It has no direct renal physiology signal and is unlikely to survive sparse selection.

Measurement frequency of “air” as an anesthetic agent is a clinician/documentation proxy rather than a kidney-relevant exposure. Even though it is closer to Hour 60, it adds little unique predictive value for creatinine rise.

Average isoflurane exposure in the immediate post-op window mainly reflects anesthesia management and early instability. It is indirect for AKI risk and likely dominated by hemodynamics/vasopressors if present.

Peak isoflurane dose early post-op is more about anesthetic depth than renal perfusion per se. Any association with AKI is indirect and likely redundant with stronger perfusion/illness-severity variables.
Minimum isoflurane dose in 0–12h is not a meaningful renal insult marker and is heavily influenced by weaning/extubation timing. Low unique value for predicting Hour 60 creatinine ratio.

Frequency of isoflurane documentation is a monitoring/clinical workflow proxy rather than a physiologic biomarker. Sparse models should deprioritize this compared with direct renal perfusion or kidney function signals.

Measurement frequency of isoflurane in 13–24h mostly captures ongoing sedation/ventilation practices. It is an indirect clinician-behavior proxy and unlikely to add unique predictive information for AKI.

Continued isoflurane measurement at 25–36h may weakly proxy prolonged ventilation/critical illness, which correlates with AKI risk. Still indirect and redundant with better severity/hemodynamic features, so low priority.

Mean SvO2 in the first 12h reflects global oxygen delivery/consumption balance but is noisy immediately post-bypass. It is a nonspecific perfusion proxy and likely less informative than later-window extremes.

Mean SvO2 at 13–24h captures sustained adequacy of cardiac output/oxygen delivery, relevant to renal perfusion. Moderately useful but still somewhat redundant with SvO2 extremes and other hemodynamic markers.

Peak SvO2 in 13–24h is generally less informative for AKI than low SvO2 (hypoperfusion). It is likely redundant/noisy and not a strong sparse-selection candidate.

Minimum SvO2 in 13–24h is a strong marker of inadequate oxygen delivery/low output and renal hypoperfusion risk. As an extreme in a more stable window, it has high unique value for sparse prediction of creatinine rise.

Indicates whether mixed/central venous O2 saturation was checked in 25–36h, mainly reflecting monitoring intensity and hemodynamic concern. As a clinician-behavior proxy (not the value), it adds limited unique renal signal for sparse selection.

Average maintenance IV fluid rate in the immediate 0–12h post-op period, when fluid shifts and protocolized care dominate. Likely weakly related to later creatinine and redundant with later-window fluid exposure.

Average maintenance IV fluid rate in 13–24h, reflecting ongoing fluid administration. Mean rate is a blunt exposure metric and is typically less informative than later (25–36h) values or more direct renal perfusion/urine output signals.

Average maintenance IV fluid rate in 25–36h, closest window to the Hour-60 creatinine outcome and may reflect evolving hemodynamics/renal support strategy. Still indirect and potentially confounded by clinician response, but more likely to survive screening than earlier means.

Peak maintenance IV fluid rate in 0–12h, which may capture brief resuscitation but is often driven by immediate post-bypass practice patterns. Early extremes are noisy and less reliable for predicting Hour-60 creatinine than later-window exposure.

Peak maintenance IV fluid rate in 13–24h can capture episodic hemodynamic instability or attempts to augment renal perfusion. More informative than the mean, but still indirect and somewhat redundant with the closer 25–36h window.

Peak maintenance IV fluid rate in 25–36h captures late episodic fluid escalation, which can reflect ongoing shock/low output or attempts to treat oliguria—both linked to subsequent AKI. Among maintenance-IV summaries, this extreme in the closest window is the strongest sparse-model candidate.

Minimum maintenance IV fluid rate in 0–12h is often near-zero due to interruptions (OR transfer, line issues) and is not a meaningful physiologic renal signal. Highly noisy and dominated by later-window features.

Minimum maintenance IV fluid rate in 13–24h mainly reflects brief holds rather than sustained perfusion strategy. Provides little unique information beyond max/mean and is unlikely to survive sparse selection.

Minimum maintenance IV fluid rate in 25–36h could reflect deliberate fluid restriction in the setting of overload/heart failure, which may correlate with AKI risk. Still a weak and interruption-prone metric compared with the max in the same window.
Count/frequency of recorded maintenance IV volume in 0–12h is primarily a documentation/monitoring proxy. Adds minimal direct renal physiology and is unlikely to be selected sparsely.
Measurement frequency of maintenance IV volume in 13–24h reflects charting intensity rather than kidney function. Low unique predictive value for creatinine ratio.

Measurement frequency of maintenance IV volume in 25–36h is still mainly a clinician-behavior proxy. Even though temporally closer, it is not a physiologic marker and is dominated by actual dose/rate summaries.

Whether systolic PAP was measured in 13–24h reflects invasive monitoring and clinician concern rather than renal physiology. Low priority for sparse selection.

Whether systolic PAP was measured in 25–36h may proxy persistent hemodynamic instability/low-output management closer to the outcome. Still indirect and confounded, so only modest value in a sparse model.

Binary flag for PAP diastolic being measured in hours 13–24 (PA catheter/monitoring). Still primarily a monitoring proxy and unlikely to add unique renal signal in a sparse model.

Binary flag for PAP diastolic measurement in hours 25–36, reflecting ongoing invasive monitoring and possibly persistent hemodynamic instability. Slightly more informative due to proximity to outcome, but remains indirect and proxy-heavy.

Indicates whether CVP was measured in the first 12h (line presence/monitoring). Largely reflects care setting and acuity rather than a direct renal perfusion/AKI signal; low sparse-selection priority.
Binary flag for CVP measurement in hours 13–24 (monitoring intensity). Redundant proxy for illness complexity and unlikely to survive sparse selection versus actual hemodynamic values.

Binary flag for CVP measurement in hours 25–36, suggesting continued invasive monitoring and potential ongoing shock/volume issues. Closer timing gives modest value, but still indirect compared with true CVP levels or urine output/creatinine.

Mean end-tidal CO2 in the first 12h, reflecting ventilation/perfusion and cardiac output indirectly. Early post-op values are noisy and the mean smooths extremes, so it is a weak, indirect predictor of later AKI.

Mean ETCO2 in hours 13–24 as an indirect marker of perfusion/ventilation adequacy. Still nonspecific and likely dominated by more direct hemodynamic/renal features; mean aggregation reduces insult sensitivity.

Mean ETCO2 in hours 25–36, closer to the 60h creatinine and may reflect sustained low perfusion or ventilatory issues. Moderate sparse-model value, but remains indirect and potentially confounded by ventilator management.

Minimum ETCO2 in hours 13–24 may better capture low-perfusion episodes (low CO) that precede AKI. More informative than mean/max and less dominated by immediate post-op noise, giving moderate sparse-model value.

Count/frequency of ETCO2 measurements in the first 12h, reflecting monitoring intensity and ventilation status rather than physiology itself. Primarily a clinician-behavior proxy and unlikely to survive sparse selection.
Count/frequency of ETCO2 documentation in hours 13–24, mainly reflecting monitoring intensity and ventilation status. Weak, indirect proxy for renal risk and unlikely to survive sparse selection.
ETCO2 measurement frequency in the closest window (25–36h) may proxy ongoing ventilatory support/instability. Still largely clinician-behavior/monitoring driven and indirect for AKI, so low priority.

Average FiO2 in the immediate post-op period reflects oxygenation/respiratory support needs. Early window is noisy and mean smooths extremes; mostly a general severity marker rather than a direct renal signal.

FiO2 measurement frequency in 25–36h may indicate ongoing mechanical ventilation and instability closer to the outcome time. Still a monitoring proxy and less useful than actual FiO2 level/extremes.

Mean tidal volume in 25–36h is closer to Hour 60 and may proxy persistent ventilatory dependence. However it is nonspecific and typically dominated by hemodynamics/renal markers for AKI prediction.

Peak tidal volume in 13–24h may indicate worsening respiratory mechanics or ventilator requirements. Still an indirect proxy for AKI and likely redundant with other severity indicators.

Heart rate measurement frequency in 13–24h is a near-universal ICU monitoring artifact. It is unlikely to contribute meaningfully in a sparse linear model beyond other severity markers.

Whether mean arterial pressure was measured in 0–12h reflects monitoring intensity/A-line presence, not the MAP level or hypotension burden. Early measurement proxies are noisy and weak for sparse selection.

MAP measurement in 25–36h is closer to the outcome and may weakly track ongoing critical illness. But as a measured/proxy variable it is far less informative than true hypotension extremes, so low priority.

Respirations recorded by anesthesia in the first 12h is largely a documentation/monitoring artifact around the immediate post-op period. It is not a direct renal signal and is likely redundant with general ICU status.

Recorded tube intake in the 25–36h window. Closest timing helps, but it remains an indirect nutrition/management marker and not a strong unique predictor of creatinine rise.

Average maintenance/bolus IV volume administered in the first 12h. Fluid exposure can relate to hemodilution and renal congestion, but the mean smooths clinically important bolus extremes and is early/noisy.

Frequency of IV bolus volume documentation in 13–24h. Still a clinician-behavior proxy and unlikely to be selected when actual volume or hemodynamic variables are available.
Frequency of IV bolus volume documentation in 25–36h. Even with better temporal proximity, it remains a proxy for care intensity rather than a direct driver/marker of evolving AKI.

Temporary pacing presence/use in 25–36h, potentially capturing ongoing instability closer to the target time. Useful mainly as a proxy and likely redundant with stronger physiologic predictors, so kept low.

Measurement frequency for pacemaker-related data in 25–36h. Despite being closer in time, it remains a documentation/monitoring proxy with limited unique renal predictive signal.

Whether/ how often CRRT/SLED blood pump rate is recorded in 0–12h, which strongly implies early need for renal replacement therapy. Even though it is a “measured” proxy, the presence of CRRT-related measurements is a high-salience indicator of severe AKI, tempered by early timing.

Measurement frequency of right drain output during 25–36h. Despite being closer to Hour 60, it remains a weak proxy variable with little direct mechanistic link to creatinine rise.

Indicator of documentation of an arrival-time variable in 13–24h. Pure measurement/recording artifact with minimal expected unique predictive value for AKI trajectory.
Indicator of documentation of an arrival-time variable in 25–36h. Even with temporal proximity, it is a workflow proxy and not a physiologic precursor of creatinine rise.

Proportion of time in normal sinus rhythm in 0–12h, a coarse marker of rhythm stability/hemodynamic risk. Indirect for renal injury and early post-op rhythm variability is common; likely dominated by more direct perfusion/vasopressor/urine output features (not in this batch).

Proportion of time in normal sinus rhythm in 13–24h, reflecting ongoing arrhythmia burden that can affect cardiac output and renal perfusion. Still indirect and somewhat redundant with other rhythm summaries; modest sparse-model priority.

Proportion of time in normal sinus rhythm in 25–36h, closest window to the Hour 60 creatinine and may capture sustained arrhythmia-related low output risk. Among rhythm summaries, this mean is the most temporally relevant and could add some unique predictive value.
Binary indicator that sinus rhythm occurred at least once in 0–12h. This is a weak, non-specific extreme (almost everyone will have some sinus beats) and is dominated by the mean proportion measures.
Binary indicator that sinus rhythm occurred at least once in 13–24h. Low information content and redundant with sinus rhythm mean; unlikely to survive sparse selection.
Binary indicator that sinus rhythm occurred at least once in 25–36h. Still low-entropy and dominated by the mean proportion of sinus rhythm in the same epoch.

Binary indicator that sinus rhythm was absent at some point in 0–12h (i.e., any non-sinus rhythm), capturing early arrhythmia exposure. Indirect for AKI and early window is noisy, but more informative than max because it flags any arrhythmia.

Binary indicator of any non-sinus rhythm during 13–24h, a marker of arrhythmia burden that may relate to renal hypoperfusion. Some signal but still redundant with the mean proportion and not kidney-specific.

Binary indicator of any non-sinus rhythm during 25–36h, closest to the outcome and may capture ongoing arrhythmia/low-output risk. Potentially complementary to the mean (flags any exposure), giving it moderate sparse-model priority within this batch.

Count/frequency of documentation of normal sinus rhythm in the first 12h. This is mainly a monitoring/EMR-density proxy and early post-op rhythm documentation is noisy and not a direct renal signal, so low sparse-model priority.

Frequency of recorded normal sinus rhythm during 13–24h. Still largely reflects documentation intensity rather than physiology tied to AKI, and is dominated by more direct hemodynamic/renal features (not present here).

Frequency of recorded normal sinus rhythm during 25–36h (closest window to outcome). Slightly more informative as a proxy for stability/absence of arrhythmia near-term, but remains a clinician-behavior/monitoring proxy and weak for sparse selection.

Average burden of sinus rhythm variants (e.g., sinus brady/tachy/arrhythmia variants) in 0–12h. Early post-op rhythm variability is common and the mean smooths extremes, making it a weak and likely redundant severity proxy for AKI prediction.

Average burden of sinus variants in 13–24h. Could weakly reflect evolving instability affecting renal perfusion, but mean aggregation dilutes short insults and is likely dominated by a max/any-occurrence representation.

Average burden of sinus variants in 25–36h. Closest window and sustained rhythm instability may correlate with lower cardiac output/renal hypoperfusion, but it is still indirect and potentially redundant with a max/any-occurrence feature.
Indicator of any sinus variant occurring in 0–12h. Early transient rhythm changes are common after cardiac surgery and are an indirect AKI signal, so unlikely to survive sparse selection.
Indicator of any sinus variant occurring in 13–24h. More reflective of early trajectory than 0–12h, but remains a nonspecific instability marker and not a direct renal precursor.

Indicator of any sinus variant occurring in 25–36h. As the closest epoch, presence of rhythm instability may add some unique prognostic information for renal hypoperfusion, and is preferable to mean/min/measured within this family.
Minimum (binary) for sinus variant in 0–12h, effectively redundant with max/mean representations. Provides little additional information and is early/noisy, so very low priority.
Minimum (binary) for sinus variant in 13–24h, which is not a clinically meaningful extreme here and is redundant with max/mean. Unlikely to add unique predictive value in a sparse model.
Minimum (binary) for sinus variant in 25–36h; conceptually unhelpful and dominated by the max (any-occurrence) signal. Redundant and weak for sparse selection.
Frequency of sinus-variant rhythm documentation in 0–12h. Primarily reflects monitoring intensity/EMR behavior and early post-op noise rather than a causal renal signal.
Frequency of sinus-variant documentation in 13–24h. Still mostly a documentation/attention proxy and likely redundant with whether a variant occurred (max) rather than adding physiologic detail.

Frequency of sinus-variant documentation in 25–36h. Slightly more informative as a proxy for ongoing rhythm issues/clinical concern near the outcome window, but remains indirect and weaker than the presence (max) signal.

Average burden of atrial arrhythmia (e.g., AF/flutter) in 0–12h. Atrial arrhythmias can reduce cardiac output and prompt diuresis/vasopressors affecting renal perfusion, but early timing and mean aggregation make it a moderate, indirect candidate.

Represents proportion of time in atrial arrhythmia during 13–24h, a marker of hemodynamic instability/illness severity. Likely indirect for AKI and largely redundant with simpler presence/absence indicators, so low sparse-selection priority.

Proportion of time in atrial arrhythmia during 25–36h, closer to the creatinine outcome window and may reflect sustained low-output physiology. Still an indirect renal signal and often redundant with arrhythmia presence, but timing makes it a moderate candidate.

Indicates any atrial arrhythmia occurred in the immediate 0–12h post-op period. Early post-bypass arrhythmias are common/transient and are an indirect proxy for instability, so limited unique value for Hour-60 creatinine.

Binary indicator of any atrial arrhythmia in 13–24h, which can correlate with hemodynamic compromise and renal hypoperfusion risk. More informative than 0–12h and less noisy, but still indirect and may be dominated by direct perfusion/renal markers not in this batch.

Binary indicator of any atrial arrhythmia in 25–36h, the closest window and a plausible marker of ongoing low cardiac output/instability affecting renal perfusion. Among this family it is the most time-proximal, non-smoothed summary and thus the best sparse-model keeper.

Minimum value for a binary atrial arrhythmia flag in 0–12h is typically uninformative (often 0 unless arrhythmia is continuous). It adds no unique signal beyond max/mean and is especially noisy early post-op.

Minimum of a binary atrial arrhythmia indicator in 13–24h is largely redundant and usually reflects any time in sinus rhythm rather than arrhythmia burden. Dominated by max/mean summaries for sparse selection.
Minimum of a binary atrial arrhythmia flag in 25–36h provides little clinical interpretability and is redundant with max/mean. Unlikely to survive sparse screening versus the 25–36h max indicator.

Count/frequency of atrial arrhythmia measurements in 0–12h is mainly a monitoring-intensity/clinician-behavior proxy. Early post-op measurement frequency is especially routine and unlikely to add unique predictive value.

Measurement frequency for atrial arrhythmia in 13–24h reflects surveillance intensity rather than physiology. Typically redundant with actual arrhythmia indicators and low priority for a sparse linear model.

Measurement frequency in 25–36h may weakly proxy ongoing instability prompting closer rhythm monitoring. Still primarily a behavior/acuity proxy and dominated by the actual arrhythmia presence variable in the same window.

Proportion of time paced in 0–12h reflects conduction issues and post-op support needs, indirectly related to low output. Early pacing is common and may be transient, so it is a weak sparse-model candidate.

Proportion of time paced in 13–24h may indicate persistent conduction dysfunction and potentially worse hemodynamics. Still an indirect renal signal and likely redundant with other hemodynamic severity markers, so low-to-moderate priority.

Proportion of time paced in 25–36h captures more persistent need for pacing closer to the outcome window, which can correlate with low cardiac output and renal hypoperfusion. Moderate value, but still indirect and may be replaceable by direct perfusion/vasopressor features.

Binary indicator of any pacing in 0–12h, often routine immediately post-cardiac surgery. Early presence is less specific for later AKI risk and provides limited unique information versus later-window pacing.

Binary indicator of any pacing in 13–24h suggests ongoing conduction/hemodynamic support needs beyond the immediate post-op period. More specific than 0–12h and may add some unique severity signal, though still indirect for creatinine trajectory.

Indicates any paced rhythm occurrence in the 25–36h window, a proxy for conduction disease/low-output risk. It is only indirectly related to AKI and largely redundant with paced_rhythm_min in the same epoch, so low sparse-model priority.
Early (0–12h) paced rhythm presence is common post-op and often transient. Weak, indirect renal signal and dominated by later-epoch pacing indicators for Hour-60 creatinine.
Paced rhythm in 13–24h may reflect persistent conduction issues and possible hemodynamic compromise. Still an indirect proxy and likely redundant with the closer 25–36h pacing feature.

Paced rhythm in the closest window (25–36h) can mark ongoing conduction disease and potential low cardiac output affecting renal perfusion. Among pacing features this is the most temporally relevant, but remains an indirect predictor versus direct renal/hemodynamic variables.

Count/frequency of paced rhythm measurements in 0–12h is primarily a monitoring/EMR documentation intensity proxy. Adds little unique physiologic information and is unlikely to survive sparse selection.

Measurement frequency of paced rhythm in 13–24h reflects clinician attention/telemetry documentation rather than renal physiology. Low unique predictive value and redundant with actual paced rhythm presence indicators.

Measurement frequency in 25–36h may weakly proxy ongoing instability or ICU complexity closer to the target time. Still largely a behavior/monitoring signal and inferior to rhythm presence variables for sparse modeling.
Average heart block burden in 0–12h is often influenced by immediate post-op transient conduction changes. Mean aggregation blunts episodic events and is an indirect renal predictor, so low priority.

Mean heart block burden in 13–24h may reflect persistent conduction abnormality with potential hemodynamic impact. However, mean is less informative than an extreme/any-occurrence indicator and is redundant with max/min heart block flags.

Mean heart block burden in 25–36h is the closest window and may capture sustained conduction-related low-output risk affecting kidneys. Still indirect and likely dominated by a simpler any-heart-block indicator (max) in the same epoch.

Any heart block occurrence in 0–12h can indicate early conduction complications and possible hemodynamic instability. Early timing and frequent transient blocks post-op limit specificity for Hour-60 creatinine.

Any heart block in 13–24h suggests more persistent conduction disease and potential low cardiac output, which can contribute to AKI. More informative than mean/min and less noisy than 0–12h, but still an indirect proxy.

Any heart block in 25–36h is a strong marker of ongoing conduction abnormality close to the prediction horizon, plausibly reflecting sustained hemodynamic compromise relevant to renal perfusion. Within this batch it is the most informative heart-block aggregation and likely to survive sparse screening.

Heart block ‘min’ (essentially persistent presence across the window) in 0–12h is rare and confounded by immediate post-op pacing/temporary conduction changes. Low yield and dominated by later-epoch indicators.

Persistent heart block across 13–24h could indicate significant conduction pathology with hemodynamic consequences. However it is a stricter, lower-prevalence version of the signal and often adds less than the any-occurrence (max) feature.

Persistent heart block across 25–36h may indicate sustained conduction-related instability near the target time. Useful but likely redundant with heart_block_max in the same epoch, which captures the broader clinically relevant insult.

Count/frequency of heart block documentation/monitoring in the first 12h is mainly a clinician-monitoring proxy. It is indirect for AKI risk and likely redundant with hemodynamic/vasopressor features not shown here, so low sparse-selection priority.

Monitoring frequency for heart block in 13–24h may reflect ongoing conduction issues and illness complexity. Still largely a measurement-intensity proxy rather than a physiologic renal signal, so only weak incremental value.

Later (25–36h) heart block monitoring frequency could correlate with persistent instability and low-output states that predispose to AKI. However it remains indirect and behavior-driven, so unlikely to survive sparse selection unless few better predictors exist.

Average presence of ischemic ST changes in the first 12h suggests perioperative myocardial ischemia. Early post-op ECG changes can be transient/noisy and mean is dominated by any-event indicators, so limited unique value for creatinine at 60h.

Mean ischemic ST change in 13–24h reflects more sustained myocardial ischemia, which can drive low cardiac output and renal hypoperfusion. Still somewhat redundant with the corresponding max (any ischemia) and indirect for kidney injury.

Sustained ischemic ST changes closer to the target window may indicate ongoing myocardial ischemia/low output affecting renal perfusion. Mean adds some duration information but is still indirect and likely correlated with the max indicator.

Any ischemic ST change in 0–12h captures an acute ischemic insult, but this period is prone to transient post-bypass artifacts and rapid correction. As an indirect AKI precursor, it is a weaker sparse-model keeper than later-window ischemia.

Any ischemic ST change in 13–24h is a strong marker of clinically meaningful postoperative myocardial ischemia, which can precipitate renal hypoperfusion and AKI. As an extreme/any-event summary, it is the most efficient representative of this signal in this batch.

Any ischemic ST change in 25–36h is the closest-window indicator of ongoing ischemia/low-output physiology, a potent driver of worsening creatinine by 60h. It is the highest-yield, least diluted aggregation among the ST-change features here.

The minimum of a binary ST-change indicator is typically uninformative (often just reflects whether there was any time without ST change). It is dominated by the max/mean versions and adds essentially no unique predictive value.

Minimum of a binary ischemic ST-change flag is largely redundant and usually equals 0 unless ST change is present continuously. It provides little incremental information beyond max/mean for sparse selection.

Minimum of a binary ST-change variable is not a meaningful ‘extreme’ for injury (it captures absence rather than insult). It is dominated by the max indicator in the same window and should be excluded in a sparse model.

Measurement count for ST-change assessment in 0–12h reflects monitoring intensity/telemetry documentation rather than physiology. It is a weak, behavior-driven proxy and unlikely to add unique value for creatinine prediction.
Frequency of ST-change measurement in 13–24h is primarily a clinician-suspicion/acuity proxy. It is redundant with the actual ST-change indicators and not a direct renal-risk signal.

More frequent ST-change measurement in 25–36h may indicate ongoing concern for ischemia and instability near the prediction horizon. Still largely a monitoring proxy and typically inferior to the actual ST-change max/mean features.

Mean burden of ‘other’ rhythms in the first 12h is heterogeneous and hard to interpret mechanistically for renal outcomes. It is likely noisy, non-specific, and dominated by more defined ischemia/hemodynamic predictors.

Represents proportion of time in a non-standard/other rhythm during 13–24h. Rhythm category is an indirect severity/hemodynamic proxy and the mean is likely redundant with a simpler any-occurrence indicator, so low sparse-model priority.

Represents proportion of time in a non-standard/other rhythm during 25–36h (closest window). Still an indirect proxy for instability and likely redundant with binary presence of other rhythm, so only weak incremental value for creatinine_ratio.

Indicates whether any 'other' rhythm occurred in the immediate 0–12h post-op period. Early rhythm abnormalities are often transient/noisy after surgery and are indirect for AKI risk, so limited sparse-model value.

Indicates any occurrence of an 'other' rhythm in 13–24h, which may reflect clinically meaningful hemodynamic instability affecting renal perfusion. As a binary extreme it is the most useful within this rhythm family, but remains indirect and likely dominated by direct perfusion/vasopressor variables elsewhere.

Indicates any occurrence of an 'other' rhythm in 25–36h, the closest available window to the 60h creatinine outcome. Potentially captures ongoing instability, but still a nonspecific proxy and not a top sparse-model keeper.

Binary minimum for an 'other rhythm' flag over 0–12h is typically uninformative (often just reflects whether it was ever absent). Redundant with max/mean and adds essentially no unique predictive signal.
Binary minimum for 'other rhythm' over 13–24h is not clinically interpretable as an insult measure and is dominated by the corresponding max indicator. Very low priority for sparse selection.

Binary minimum for 'other rhythm' over 25–36h is largely redundant and usually constant (0 unless always in other rhythm). Provides little unique information beyond max/mean, so exclude in sparse models.

Count/frequency of documenting 'other rhythm' in 0–12h mainly reflects monitoring/annotation behavior rather than physiology. As a measurement-intensity proxy and early window, it is weak for predicting creatinine_ratio.
Measurement frequency of 'other rhythm' in 13–24h is primarily a clinician/monitoring proxy. Likely redundant with the binary occurrence feature and not a direct renal-risk signal.

Measurement frequency of 'other rhythm' in 25–36h reflects documentation intensity and care complexity more than renal physiology. Even though temporally closer, it is still a weak proxy and low sparse-model priority.

Represents proportion of time on ASV ventilator mode during 0–12h. Vent mode choice is largely practice/patient-management driven and early post-op, making it a weak and indirect predictor of AKI trajectory.

Represents proportion of time on ASV mode during 13–24h. Still mostly a clinician-behavior/ventilation-strategy marker and unlikely to add unique predictive value for creatinine_ratio in a sparse model.
Indicates any use of ASV mode in 0–12h, which may weakly proxy respiratory stability/sedation needs. However it is management-driven and early/noisy, so only minimal sparse-model value.

Indicates any ASV use in 13–24h and may reflect ongoing ventilatory support needs (general severity). Still indirect and likely dominated by more direct oxygenation/ventilation/hemodynamic variables, so low priority.
Binary minimum for ASV use over 0–12h is generally non-informative and redundant with max/mean. Provides little interpretable signal for renal outcome prediction.
Whether ASV mode was used at all in 13–24h. Vent mode choice is an indirect care-pattern/respiratory severity proxy and is unlikely to add unique renal predictive value in a sparse model.
Count/frequency of ASV documentation in 0–12h. This is primarily monitoring/documentation intensity and early post-op noise, offering little direct signal for Hour-60 creatinine ratio.
Frequency of ASV documentation in 13–24h. Mostly a clinician-behavior proxy and redundant with simpler “mode used” indicators; weak for sparse selection.
Frequency of ASV documentation in 25–36h (closest window). Still largely a care-pattern proxy, but later timing may weakly reflect ongoing critical illness that correlates with AKI risk.
Average use of controlled ventilation in 0–12h. Controlled ventilation can reflect immediate post-op severity/sedation but is early and indirect for renal injury, so low sparse priority.
Average use of controlled ventilation in 13–24h. Somewhat more informative than 0–12h as a severity proxy, but still indirect and likely dominated by hemodynamics/renal markers not in this batch.
Indicator that controlled ventilation occurred at any point in 0–12h. Binary presence is less redundant than mean/measured, but early post-op ventilation is common and not kidney-specific.

Indicator that controlled ventilation occurred at any point in 13–24h. Persistent need for controlled ventilation beyond the immediate post-op period can mark ongoing shock/illness severity and may add modest unique predictive value within this vent-mode-only batch.

Indicator that controlled ventilation was continuously present across 0–12h (min). This is largely redundant with controlled_max/mean and heavily influenced by routine immediate post-op management rather than renal physiology.

Indicator that controlled ventilation was continuously present across 13–24h. More suggestive of sustained severity than max, but still an indirect proxy and likely redundant with controlled_max in sparse selection.
Frequency of controlled-mode documentation in 0–12h. Measurement frequency is a monitoring proxy and early post-op documentation patterns add little unique value for predicting creatinine ratio.
Frequency of controlled-mode documentation in 13–24h. Mostly reflects charting/monitoring intensity and is redundant with simpler binary indicators of mode use.
Frequency of controlled-mode documentation in 25–36h. Later timing could weakly capture ongoing critical illness, but it remains a documentation proxy and not a direct renal signal.
Average use of SIMV in 0–12h. Vent mode selection early after surgery is common and non-specific, making it a weak and redundant predictor for AKI trajectory.
Average use of SIMV in 13–24h. SIMV use is an indirect respiratory-management marker and unlikely to survive sparse selection versus more direct renal/hemodynamic features.
Indicator that SIMV occurred at any point in 0–12h. This is a non-specific ventilation strategy marker and early timing further reduces its unique predictive value for Hour-60 creatinine ratio.

Whether SIMV was used at any point in 13–24h reflects ventilator dependence/illness severity rather than a direct renal signal. Likely redundant with other ventilation-mode indicators and weaker than hemodynamic/renal-specific predictors for sparse selection.

SIMV presence throughout 0–12h is heavily influenced by immediate post-op practice patterns and transient instability. Early timing and redundancy make it a low-priority proxy feature for predicting 60h creatinine ratio.

Persistent SIMV use in 13–24h suggests ongoing respiratory/cardiac support needs, an indirect marker of severity. Still largely a clinician-behavior proxy and redundant with other vent-mode summaries, so only weakly useful.

Count/frequency of SIMV documentation in 0–12h mainly captures monitoring/documentation intensity rather than physiology. As a measured/proxy variable in the noisiest epoch, it is unlikely to survive sparse selection.

Measurement frequency of SIMV in 13–24h is a documentation/attention proxy with limited direct renal relevance. Redundant with the binary SIMV indicators and generally low value for a sparse linear model.

Later (25–36h) documentation frequency may reflect persistent critical illness closer to the 60h target. Still primarily a monitoring proxy and dominated by direct hemodynamic/renal markers, so modest priority at best.

Average spontaneous mode use in 0–12h reflects early extubation trajectory but is confounded by immediate post-op management. Indirect and redundant with other spontaneous-mode aggregations, so low sparse-model value.

Sustained spontaneous ventilation in 13–24h suggests recovery and less severe illness, which can correlate with lower AKI risk. However it remains an indirect proxy and is redundant with max/min indicators.
Any spontaneous mode in 0–12h is largely driven by early extubation practices and transient post-op factors. Weak, indirect renal relevance and high redundancy make it low priority.

Any spontaneous mode in 13–24h can indicate readiness to wean and lower overall severity, indirectly relating to AKI risk. Still a proxy feature and not as uniquely informative as direct perfusion/renal measures.

Spontaneous mode throughout 0–12h is uncommon and heavily influenced by immediate post-op workflow and sedation strategy. Early epoch noise and redundancy limit its usefulness for 60h creatinine prediction.

Persistent spontaneous ventilation in 13–24h suggests stable respiratory status and less critical illness, indirectly associated with lower AKI risk. Nonetheless it is a clinician-management proxy and likely replaceable in a sparse model.
Frequency of documenting spontaneous mode in 0–12h is primarily a monitoring/documentation artifact. As a measured proxy in the earliest window, it adds little unique predictive value.

Documentation frequency for spontaneous mode in 13–24h reflects care intensity rather than a physiologic renal insult. Redundant with the binary spontaneous-mode indicators and low priority for sparse selection.
Later measurement frequency (25–36h) may weakly track ongoing ICU complexity near the prediction horizon. Still a proxy variable and generally dominated by direct hemodynamic/renal features.

Mean arterial pressure in the first 12h reflects systemic perfusion pressure, a key driver of renal hypoperfusion and AKI risk after cardiac surgery. Although a mean (not capturing hypotensive nadirs) and early-window noise exists, it is still a strong, relatively direct physiologic predictor compared with ventilator-mode proxies in this batch.
Average MAP over 13–24h reflects overall perfusion but smooths over short hypotensive insults most relevant to AKI. Likely redundant with MAP_min (and later-window MAP features) for sparse selection.

Average MAP over 25–36h is closer to the Hour-60 creatinine outcome and captures sustained perfusion adequacy. Still somewhat redundant with MAP_min in the same window, but may add signal if hypotension is prolonged rather than episodic.

Peak MAP in the immediate 0–12h post-op period often reflects transient vasopressor-driven hypertension rather than renal hypoperfusion risk. Low unique value for AKI prediction and dominated by hypotension-focused metrics.
Peak MAP in 13–24h is not a direct precursor of AKI and is typically less informative than hypotension extremes. Likely redundant/noisy for sparse modeling compared with MAP_min.

Peak MAP in 25–36h mainly captures hypertension/pressor effect and is weakly linked to creatinine rise compared with low-perfusion signals. Unlikely to survive sparse selection given stronger MAP_min alternatives.

Minimum MAP in 0–12h captures early hypotensive episodes that can contribute to renal ischemic injury. However, immediate post-op instability is noisy and less predictive than later hypotension closer to Hour 60.

Minimum MAP in 13–24h captures clinically meaningful hypotension after initial post-op noise, a strong precursor to renal hypoperfusion and evolving AKI. High-priority extreme metric, though still somewhat superseded by the later 25–36h window.

Minimum MAP in 25–36h is the closest hypotension extreme to the Hour-60 creatinine and directly reflects renal perfusion risk during the evolving AKI window. This is the dominant MAP aggregation in this batch and a top sparse-model candidate.

Count/frequency of MAP measurements in 0–12h is primarily a monitoring-intensity/acuity proxy rather than physiology. Early post-op measurement density is routine and adds little unique predictive value.
MAP measurement frequency in 13–24h reflects clinician behavior and ICU workflow more than renal pathophysiology. Unlikely to add independent signal beyond actual MAP values.

MAP measurement frequency in 25–36h may weakly proxy ongoing instability prompting closer monitoring. Still indirect and generally dominated by the actual hypotension extremes (MAP_min) for sparse selection.
Proportion of time NPO in 0–12h is largely routine post-cardiac surgery care and not a direct renal signal. Mostly a care-process proxy with minimal unique predictive value.
NPO status in 13–24h reflects feeding progression and clinician preference rather than kidney injury biology. Likely weak and confounded, so low priority for a sparse model.
Persistent NPO in 25–36h can proxy ongoing critical illness or delayed recovery, which may correlate with AKI risk. Still indirect and likely redundant with more direct hemodynamic/renal markers.
Any NPO during 0–12h is nearly universal after surgery and provides little discrimination. Redundant with NPO_mean and low relevance to creatinine trajectory.
Any NPO during 13–24h remains common and is a coarse care-process indicator. Adds little unique information compared with physiologic predictors of AKI.

Whether the patient was NPO at any point in the 25–36h window, reflecting delayed feeding/ongoing critical illness. It is an indirect severity/trajectory proxy and likely redundant with other acuity markers, but the late window makes it slightly more informative than earlier diet flags.

Whether the patient was NPO throughout the immediate 0–12h post-op period, which is routine after cardiac surgery. This is mostly standard care/early post-op noise and offers little unique signal for Hour-60 creatinine ratio.

Whether the patient remained continuously NPO during 13–24h, reflecting feeding delay. Still an indirect clinician-behavior/illness proxy and generally dominated by more direct renal perfusion/AKI features.

Whether the patient was continuously NPO during 25–36h, suggesting persistent instability or inability to feed. Later timing may correlate with ongoing organ dysfunction, but it remains nonspecific and redundant with stronger severity signals.

Count/frequency of NPO documentation in 0–12h, primarily reflecting charting intensity rather than physiology. As a monitoring/behavior proxy in an early noisy window, it is very unlikely to survive sparse selection.
Frequency of NPO documentation in 13–24h, mainly a documentation/monitoring proxy. Provides little unique predictive value for AKI compared with direct hemodynamics, urine output, and labs.

Frequency of NPO documentation in 25–36h, reflecting charting practices and care complexity more than renal pathophysiology. Even with closer timing, it is a weak proxy and redundant with better acuity indicators.

Average diet status/level in 0–12h, which largely captures routine post-op feeding delay. It is indirect and early-window noise, offering minimal unique information for predicting creatinine rise by Hour 60.
Average diet status in 13–24h, reflecting progression of feeding. This is a nonspecific recovery proxy and is unlikely to add value beyond direct renal and perfusion variables in a sparse model.

Average diet status in 25–36h, capturing whether feeding has resumed closer to the outcome time. It may weakly track recovery vs persistent critical illness, but remains indirect and redundant with stronger physiologic predictors.
Whether any diet (non-NPO) occurred in 0–12h, which is uncommon and heavily protocol-dependent. As an early clinician-behavior marker, it has low and noisy relevance to later creatinine ratio.

Whether any diet was started in 13–24h, indicating earlier recovery and potentially less severe course. Still indirect and likely dominated by direct renal/perfusion measures, but could add small unique trajectory signal.
Whether any diet was started in 25–36h, reflecting recovery status closer to Hour 60. It is a weak, nonspecific proxy and largely redundant with other markers of stability, so only low priority.
Whether the patient was on a diet throughout 0–12h, which is rare and protocol-driven. This is not a direct renal signal and is too early/noisy to be useful for sparse selection.

Whether the patient was continuously on a diet during 13–24h, suggesting faster recovery. It may weakly correlate with lower AKI risk but is indirect and redundant with stronger physiologic predictors.

Whether the patient was continuously on a diet during 25–36h, indicating sustained stability closer to the outcome. Still a nonspecific recovery proxy and unlikely to be retained when direct renal/hemodynamic features are available.
Count/frequency of diet-type documentation in the first 12h is mainly a monitoring/workflow proxy. It has no direct renal physiology signal and is unlikely to survive sparse selection.
Diet-type measurement frequency in 13–24h reflects documentation intensity rather than kidney function. Any association with AKI would be indirect and dominated by hemodynamics/labs.
Diet-type measurement frequency closest to the target window is still a clinician-behavior proxy. It adds little unique predictive value for creatinine_ratio versus true renal/perfusion features.
Mean value of being on an 'other' diet category early post-op is not a direct marker of renal injury. Likely noisy and redundant with overall illness severity and feeding status.
Average 'other diet' status in 13–24h is an indirect care-pattern variable. It is unlikely to add unique signal for Hour-60 creatinine beyond stronger physiologic predictors.
Sustained 'other diet' status at 25–36h may weakly proxy delayed recovery/ongoing critical illness. Still indirect and likely redundant, so low sparse-model priority.
Whether 'other diet' occurred at any point in 0–12h is a binary care-status indicator. Early post-op feeding orders are highly variable and not kidney-specific.
Any occurrence of 'other diet' in 13–24h is a weak proxy for clinical course and documentation. It provides minimal unique information about evolving AKI risk.
Any 'other diet' in 25–36h could reflect persistent NPO/atypical nutrition plans in sicker patients. Still a nonspecific proxy and likely dominated by direct renal/perfusion variables.
Minimum of a binary 'other diet' flag is essentially whether it was absent throughout 0–12h. This is not physiologically informative for renal outcomes and is redundant with max/mean encodings.
Minimum of the 'other diet' indicator in 13–24h mostly encodes persistent absence/presence and adds no renal-specific signal. Redundant with other aggregations in this family.
Minimum of a binary 'other diet' flag in 25–36h is a redundant encoding of diet category persistence. It is not a direct AKI precursor and unlikely to be selected sparsely.
Measurement frequency for the 'other diet' category is a documentation/monitoring proxy. It is not a biomarker and is dominated by more direct renal risk signals.
How often 'other diet' is recorded in 13–24h reflects charting patterns rather than kidney function. Low likelihood of adding unique predictive value in a sparse model.
Frequency of recording 'other diet' in 25–36h remains a clinician-behavior proxy. Even though temporally closer, it is still weak and redundant for predicting creatinine_ratio.

Average time in regular rhythm in the first 12h may weakly relate to cardiac output stability and renal perfusion. However it is early, indirect, and likely dominated by direct hemodynamic/vasopressor measures.

Represents proportion of time/rhythm assessments showing regular rhythm in 13–24h. Only indirectly related to AKI risk (via hemodynamic stability) and largely redundant with irregularity indicators and more proximal 25–36h data.

Represents sustained regular rhythm in the closest window (25–36h), which may reflect better cardiac output and renal perfusion. Still an indirect signal and partly redundant with irregular rhythm features, but timing makes it the best of the regular_mean variants.

Binary indicator that regular rhythm was observed at least once in 0–12h. Early post-op rhythm is noisy and this max is not informative (most patients will have some regular rhythm), so it is unlikely to survive sparse selection.

Binary indicator that regular rhythm occurred at least once in 13–24h. This is a weak, non-specific extreme (presence of any regular rhythm) and is dominated by measures capturing irregularity burden or later-window summaries.

Binary indicator that regular rhythm occurred at least once in 25–36h. Even in the closest window, this max is typically uninformative and redundant because it does not quantify arrhythmia burden relevant to renal hypoperfusion.

Binary indicator that regular rhythm was present at the minimum (i.e., consistently regular across the window). In 0–12h this is heavily confounded by immediate post-op factors and offers little unique predictive value for 60h creatinine ratio.

Binary indicator of consistently regular rhythm throughout 13–24h, which could reflect fewer arrhythmia-related hemodynamic swings. Still indirect and coarse (binary), and likely redundant with irregular rhythm burden measures.

Binary indicator of consistently regular rhythm in 25–36h, the most temporally relevant window. Could add some unique signal as a marker of stable rhythm/hemodynamics, but remains indirect and less informative than direct perfusion/renal markers.

Count/frequency of documenting regular rhythm in 0–12h, mainly reflecting monitoring intensity and charting practices. This is a clinician-behavior proxy and early-window noise, so low priority for sparse selection.

Measurement frequency of regular rhythm documentation in 13–24h, primarily a proxy for monitoring/acuity rather than physiology. Unlikely to add unique predictive value beyond actual rhythm/hemodynamic variables.

Measurement frequency of regular rhythm documentation in 25–36h, reflecting surveillance intensity rather than renal pathophysiology. Even though later, it is still a weak proxy and dominated by true physiologic features.

Represents burden of irregular rhythm (e.g., AF/ectopy) in 0–12h, which can relate to low output and renal hypoperfusion. However early post-op arrhythmias are common/transient and less predictive for 60h creatinine than later trajectory.

Represents sustained irregular rhythm burden in 13–24h, a more informative window for evolving instability than 0–12h. Still indirect for AKI and may be redundant with more proximal 25–36h irregularity if available.

Represents sustained irregular rhythm burden in 25–36h, closest to the 60h creatinine outcome and plausibly linked to reduced renal perfusion/need for vasoactive support. Among this batch, it is the strongest rhythm-derived predictor and likely to survive sparse screening.
Binary indicator that any irregular rhythm occurred in 0–12h. This is common and non-specific early after surgery and provides little incremental information beyond irregular_mean measures.

Binary indicator that any irregular rhythm occurred in 13–24h. Somewhat more meaningful than 0–12h, but still a coarse presence/absence metric dominated by irregular_mean (burden) and later-window rhythm summaries.

Represents whether any irregular cardiac rhythm (e.g., AF) occurred in the 25–36h window. Later arrhythmia can proxy hemodynamic instability/vasopressor need that may contribute to AKI risk, but it is indirect and largely redundant with other instability markers (not in this batch).

Indicates absence/presence of irregular rhythm early post-op (0–12h), where transient rhythm issues are common. Early timing and binary min aggregation add little unique signal for Hour-60 creatinine ratio.

Captures whether irregular rhythm was present at any point in 13–24h (depending on encoding of min). Mid-window arrhythmia may relate to renal hypoperfusion risk, but remains an indirect proxy and is dominated by the later 25–36h window.

Binary min for irregular rhythm in the closest window, but min is a weak aggregation for episodic arrhythmias compared with max/any-occurrence. Some relevance as a hemodynamic proxy, but redundant with irregular_max in the same epoch.
Number/frequency of documenting irregular rhythm in 0–12h is mainly a monitoring/clinician-behavior proxy. Early post-op measurement intensity is noisy and not a direct renal physiology signal.
Measurement frequency of irregular rhythm in 13–24h reflects surveillance intensity rather than arrhythmia burden itself. Low priority for sparse selection versus actual rhythm occurrence indicators.

Later measurement frequency may weakly proxy ongoing instability/arrhythmia management closer to the outcome. Still primarily a documentation/acuity proxy and typically dominated by occurrence (max) features.

Mean proportion/value of ‘other’ rhythm regularity category in 0–12h is hard to interpret clinically and likely reflects coding artifacts. Early timing and unclear physiologic mapping make it a poor sparse-model candidate.

Binary/mean indicator of ‘other’ rhythm category in 13–24h is nonspecific and likely heterogeneous (paced rhythms, artifact, etc.). Provides little unique renal-risk information compared with clearer instability markers.

‘Other’ rhythm category closer to Hour 60 could capture paced rhythms or atypical conduction that may correlate with illness severity. Still nonspecific and likely redundant/noisy, so only low-moderate priority.
Any occurrence of ‘other’ rhythm category in 0–12h is common post-op (pacing, artifact) and not directly tied to AKI. Early window and nonspecific definition limit predictive value.

Any ‘other’ rhythm occurrence in 13–24h may reflect pacing dependence or conduction issues that can correlate with hemodynamic complexity. Still indirect and heterogeneous, so not a top sparse feature.

Any ‘other’ rhythm occurrence in 25–36h is the most temporally proximate and may capture ongoing pacing/arrhythmia states linked to hemodynamic compromise. Among the ‘other’ family this is the best aggregation, but remains indirect for renal injury.
Binary min for ‘other’ rhythm in 0–12h is both early and an unhelpful aggregation for episodic states. Likely redundant with max/mean versions and mostly noise.
Binary min for ‘other’ rhythm in 13–24h is a weak encoding that adds little beyond max/mean. Nonspecific rhythm category makes it low value for sparse selection.

Binary min for ‘other’ rhythm in 25–36h is dominated by the corresponding max (any occurrence) feature for capturing clinically meaningful episodes. Adds minimal unique information for predicting creatinine ratio.

Count/frequency of documenting an 'other' cardiac rhythm/regularity in the first 12h, largely a monitoring/documentation proxy. Weak and nonspecific for AKI prediction and dominated by direct hemodynamic/renal features (not present here).

Measurement frequency of an 'other' rhythm category in 13–24h, reflecting clinician attention more than physiology. Low unique value for creatinine_ratio and likely redundant with more direct instability markers.

Later (25–36h) monitoring frequency for atypical rhythm documentation may weakly proxy ongoing instability closer to the outcome window. Still primarily a behavior/complexity signal, so low priority in a sparse model.
Mean heart rate from pulse-ox monitor in the immediate 0–12h post-op period. HR is an indirect illness/hemodynamic stress marker and early values are noisy; limited unique renal predictive value.

Mean HR in 13–24h may reflect sustained stress/low output states that can contribute to renal hypoperfusion. Still indirect and typically dominated by BP/vasopressors/urine output signals, so modest priority.

Mean HR in 25–36h is the closest sustained tachycardia/bradycardia burden available and may track ongoing low cardiac output or sepsis physiology relevant to AKI trajectory. More temporally aligned than earlier epochs, but remains indirect.

Peak HR in 0–12h captures acute sympathetic surges/arrhythmia episodes but this window is heavily influenced by immediate post-bypass/anesthesia effects. Extremes are better than means, yet early timing limits sparse-model value.

Peak HR in 13–24h can indicate clinically meaningful tachyarrhythmia, pain/agitation, or shock physiology that may precede AKI. As an extreme it can add signal beyond the mean, but remains nonspecific.

Peak HR in 25–36h is temporally closest and may capture ongoing instability (e.g., AF with RVR, shock) relevant to later creatinine rise. Useful as an extreme, though still an indirect proxy for renal injury.

Minimum HR in 0–12h may reflect pacing, sedation, or transient conduction issues common immediately post-op. Extreme bradycardia could relate to low output, but early noise reduces selection priority.

Minimum HR in 13–24h can capture clinically relevant bradycardia/heart block or over-sedation with potential low cardiac output implications for renal perfusion. Adds some unique extreme-value information versus mean HR.

Minimum HR in 25–36h is closest to the outcome and may reflect persistent bradyarrhythmia/pacing dependence associated with low perfusion risk. Still indirect for AKI, but the extreme and timing make it a reasonable sparse candidate.

Frequency of HR measurements from pulse-ox in 0–12h is essentially a monitoring intensity proxy in a period when nearly everyone is closely monitored. Very low unique predictive value for creatinine_ratio.
Measurement count of pulse-ox HR in 13–24h reflects workflow/acuity rather than a physiologic renal signal. Unlikely to survive sparse selection given redundancy with actual HR summaries.

Later measurement frequency may weakly indicate ongoing ICU-level instability or continued monitoring needs closer to Hour 60. Still a proxy variable and generally dominated by the HR min/max/mean themselves.

Mean apical HR in 0–12h is an alternative measurement source that is largely redundant with monitor-derived HR and subject to early post-op noise. Low incremental value for a sparse model compared with later/ extreme HR features.

Mean apical heart rate in 13–24h reflects overall sympathetic tone/illness severity rather than a direct renal signal. Likely redundant with other hemodynamic variables and less informative than extremes or later-window measures for sparse selection.

Mean apical heart rate in 25–36h is closer to the Hour-60 creatinine outcome and may capture sustained stress/low-output states affecting renal perfusion. Still indirect and typically dominated by BP/vasopressor/urine-output signals, so moderate priority.

Indicator of a high apical heart-rate extreme in the immediate 0–12h post-op period, which is often driven by transient pain/anesthesia/bypass effects. Early and nonspecific, so low unique value for predicting Hour-60 creatinine ratio.

Indicator of tachycardic extreme in 13–24h may reflect evolving shock, arrhythmia, or fever that can contribute to AKI risk. However it is an indirect severity marker and likely redundant with stronger perfusion/vasopressor features.

Indicator of tachycardic extreme in 25–36h is temporally closer and may capture ongoing hemodynamic stress relevant to renal hypoperfusion. Still indirect and often collinear with vasopressor use and hypotension, so not top-tier.

Indicator of low apical heart-rate extreme in 0–12h is commonly influenced by sedation, pacing, or immediate post-op management. Early and behavior-driven, making it a weak sparse-model candidate for AKI prediction.

Indicator of bradycardic extreme in 13–24h could reflect conduction issues/low cardiac output that may affect renal perfusion. Nonetheless it is nonspecific and likely redundant with direct cardiac output/BP/vasopressor signals.

Indicator of bradycardic extreme in 25–36h is closer to outcome and may mark persistent low-output physiology. Still an indirect proxy and often confounded by pacing/medication, so limited unique value.

Count/frequency of apical HR measurements in 0–12h mainly reflects monitoring intensity and workflow rather than physiology. As a clinician-behavior proxy and early window, it has minimal sparse-model value.

Apical HR measurement frequency in 13–24h is primarily a proxy for nursing/monitoring practices or perceived instability. Weak direct linkage to renal dysfunction and unlikely to survive sparse selection.
Apical HR measurement frequency in 25–36h may weakly indicate ongoing instability prompting closer monitoring. Still largely a behavior proxy and inferior to physiologic extremes, so low priority.
Mean peripheral heart rate in 0–12h is an early, nonspecific severity marker and often noisy post-bypass. Also redundant with apical HR summaries, so very low sparse-model priority.
Indicator related to peripheral HR mean in 13–24h adds little beyond apical HR and other hemodynamic variables. As a redundant and indirect signal, it is unlikely to be selected in a sparse model.
Indicator related to peripheral HR mean in 25–36h is closer to outcome and could reflect sustained stress. However it is redundant with apical HR and remains an indirect proxy for renal risk.

Indicator of peripheral tachycardic extreme in 0–12h is heavily influenced by immediate post-op factors and measurement artifacts. Early and redundant with apical HR extremes, so minimal unique predictive value.

Indicator of peripheral tachycardic extreme in 13–24h may capture instability relevant to AKI risk. Still indirect and likely dominated by apical HR/vasopressor/BP features, so low-to-moderate priority.

Indicates whether the heart rate measurement source was peripheral (not the HR value). This is a documentation/monitoring artifact with little direct renal signal and is unlikely to survive sparse selection.

Binary indicator of peripheral source for HR in the immediate post-op window. Early and largely reflects measurement modality rather than physiology, so it adds minimal unique value for predicting creatinine rise.
Peripheral HR source indicator during 13–24h. Still a monitoring/line-status proxy and redundant with other epochs; not a direct marker of renal hypoperfusion or AKI trajectory.

Peripheral HR source indicator closest to the outcome window. Despite better timing, it remains a documentation proxy (arterial line vs peripheral) and is unlikely to add predictive signal beyond true hemodynamics.
Count/frequency of peripheral-source HR measurements in 0–12h. Measurement frequency is mainly clinician-behavior/monitoring intensity and early post-op noise, so low sparse-model priority.
Frequency of peripheral-source HR measurements in 13–24h. Primarily reflects monitoring patterns and line availability rather than renal physiology; likely redundant and weakly informative.

Frequency of peripheral-source HR measurements in 25–36h. Closest timing may weakly proxy ongoing instability/ICU complexity, but it is still indirect and dominated by actual vitals/vasopressor variables.
Proportion/average time on mechanical ventilation in 0–12h. Early ventilation is common after cardiac surgery and is a nonspecific severity marker, so limited unique value for AKI prediction.
Average exposure to mechanical ventilation in 13–24h. Persistent ventilation beyond the immediate post-op period can reflect cardiopulmonary dysfunction and higher AKI risk, but remains indirect.

Average exposure to mechanical ventilation in 25–36h. Ongoing ventilation close to the prediction horizon is a strong proxy for sustained critical illness/hemodynamic compromise and may add unique predictive value in a sparse model.
Whether mechanical ventilation occurred at any point in 0–12h. Nearly ubiquitous post-cardiac surgery and thus low-discrimination; also redundant with mean/min indicators.

Whether mechanical ventilation occurred at any point in 13–24h. Captures failure to extubate and correlates with severity/low cardiac output states, but is less informative than sustained exposure near 25–36h.

Whether mechanical ventilation occurred at any point in 25–36h. Useful as a simple marker of ongoing critical illness, but largely redundant with the 25–36h mechanical mean which better captures sustained exposure.
