Whether the minimum status indicates mechanical ventilation across 0–12h (i.e., mechanically ventilated throughout). Early continuous ventilation is expected and noisy, offering little unique renal prognostic value.
Indicator consistent with being mechanically ventilated throughout 13–24h. More informative than 0–12h but still an indirect severity proxy and somewhat redundant with mean/max ventilation features.

Indicator consistent with being mechanically ventilated throughout 25–36h. Continuous ventilation close to Hour 60 suggests persistent organ dysfunction and higher AKI risk, but is still dominated by the 25–36h mean as a smoother exposure measure.

Count/frequency of documentation of mechanical ventilation in 0–12h, mainly reflecting monitoring/EMR activity rather than physiology. Very indirect for AKI and dominated by more direct hemodynamic/renal signals; early window adds noise.

Measurement frequency for mechanical ventilation status in 13–24h is largely a clinician/monitoring proxy. Adds little unique value for creatinine trajectory compared with actual ventilator settings, gas exchange, or perfusion variables.

Later (25–36h) measurement frequency of mechanical ventilation may weakly proxy ongoing critical illness and delayed extubation, which can correlate with AKI risk. Still mostly a documentation-intensity proxy and likely redundant with direct severity/perfusion features.

Mean time/proportion on no oxygen device in 0–12h reflects early extubation/low support but is heavily confounded by immediate post-op practice patterns. Weak and indirect for renal prediction and redundant with later oxygen/ventilation status.

Average time on no oxygen device in 13–24h suggests recovery vs ongoing respiratory support, a general severity marker. Indirect for AKI and likely redundant with a simpler indicator of any/no support in the later epoch.

By 25–36h, sustained absence of oxygen device better reflects overall physiologic recovery and lower illness severity, which can track with lower AKI risk. Still indirect, but this timing makes it the most potentially useful of the mean summaries.
Binary indicator that the patient was on no oxygen device at least once in 0–12h. Early post-op transitions make this noisy and it provides little unique renal signal.
Indicates any period without oxygen device in 13–24h, a coarse marker of respiratory recovery. Some association with lower severity, but indirect and dominated by the closer 25–36h status.

Any time on no oxygen device in 25–36h is a simple, less redundant summary of recovery close to the prediction horizon. Useful as a sparse proxy for overall stability, though still not kidney-specific.

Binary indicator that the patient was never on no oxygen device (i.e., always had some device) in 0–12h, which is expected for many post-cardiac surgery patients. Too nonspecific and early to add sparse-model value for creatinine ratio.

Never being off oxygen device throughout 13–24h suggests ongoing support and higher illness severity. Indirect for AKI and likely redundant with mechanical ventilation/oxygen support indicators in the later window.

Persistent need for some oxygen device across 25–36h is a closer-in marker of delayed recovery and higher systemic stress, which can correlate with AKI. Still a general severity proxy, but more selection-worthy than earlier epochs.
Measurement frequency for 'no oxygen device' status in 0–12h is primarily documentation behavior. Provides minimal unique predictive information for renal outcomes.
Counts of recorded 'no oxygen device' entries in 13–24h reflect charting intensity and workflow. Weak, indirect, and unlikely to survive sparse selection.
Later measurement frequency may weakly track clinical attention/instability, but remains a proxy rather than a physiologic exposure. Slightly higher only due to proximity to Hour 60.

Mean time/proportion on low-flow oxygen in 0–12h reflects early respiratory support needs after surgery. Indirect for AKI and early-window noise limits value, but it can weakly proxy overall severity if no better respiratory variables are retained.

Represents typical use of low-flow oxygen during hours 13–24, a marker of respiratory support needs. It is an indirect illness-severity/trajectory proxy and likely redundant with the closer 25–36h oxygen-support indicators.

Represents typical low-flow oxygen use in the closest window (25–36h), reflecting ongoing respiratory status and overall recovery. While still indirect for AKI, its temporal proximity may add some unique prognostic value versus earlier epochs.

Indicates whether low-flow oxygen was used at any point in the immediate 0–12h post-op period. Early post-op oxygen device choice is noisy and largely clinician/practice-driven, with weak specific linkage to later creatinine rise.

Flags any low-flow oxygen use during 13–24h, reflecting some ongoing need for supplemental oxygen. This is a coarse, indirect severity marker and is dominated by the more proximal 25–36h oxygen-support features.

Flags any low-flow oxygen use during 25–36h, capturing persistent oxygen requirement close to the prediction horizon. Still indirect for renal injury, but may contribute unique signal about delayed recovery/complications affecting renal perfusion.

Indicates whether low-flow oxygen was used throughout/at the minimum level in 0–12h (effectively a weak proxy for early respiratory support pattern). Highly susceptible to immediate post-op workflow and redundancy with other oxygen-device indicators.

Represents the minimum/consistent presence of low-flow oxygen in 13–24h, suggesting sustained need. It remains an indirect proxy and offers limited incremental value beyond the closer 25–36h measures.

Represents sustained low-flow oxygen requirement in 25–36h, closer to hour-60 creatinine and potentially reflecting ongoing cardiopulmonary compromise. Indirect but more likely to survive sparse selection than earlier low-flow variants due to timing.

Counts/documentation frequency for low-flow oxygen device in 0–12h, mainly reflecting monitoring/documentation intensity. This is a clinician-behavior proxy with little direct physiologic specificity for AKI prediction.

Measurement frequency of low-flow oxygen device in 13–24h, largely a proxy for charting/acuity rather than a renal mechanism. Unlikely to add unique value in a sparse model compared with actual support level indicators.

Measurement frequency of low-flow oxygen device in 25–36h, reflecting monitoring intensity near the prediction horizon. Still mostly a proxy, but proximity may weakly correlate with instability/complexity and thus earns a slightly higher score than earlier epochs.

Indicates high-flow oxygen use in 0–12h, a marker of significant respiratory support needs. Early post-op use can be transient and practice-driven, making it a weaker and noisier predictor of hour-60 creatinine ratio.

Indicates high-flow oxygen use in 13–24h, suggesting more persistent respiratory failure/complications. This is still indirect for AKI but may capture a higher-severity phenotype than low-flow oxygen and thus has moderate sparse-model value.

Indicates high-flow oxygen use in 25–36h, a strong marker of ongoing cardiopulmonary compromise close to the outcome window. While not kidney-specific, it can uniquely capture severe illness/low reserve likely associated with renal hypoperfusion and AKI risk.

Flags any high-flow oxygen use during 0–12h, capturing an early episode of significant respiratory support. However, early transient needs are common post-cardiac surgery and this is less informative than later high-flow indicators.

Flags any high-flow oxygen use during 13–24h, capturing intermittent or evolving respiratory failure. Moderately useful as a severity/trajectory marker but somewhat redundant with the more proximal 25–36h high-flow feature.

Indicates use of high-flow oxygen in the 25–36h window (respiratory failure/illness severity). Closest epoch makes it the best candidate among the high-flow variants, but it is still an indirect proxy for AKI risk and likely redundant with stronger hemodynamic/renal features.

Early (0–12h) presence/absence of high-flow oxygen use, reflecting immediate post-op respiratory support. Early window is noisy and this is dominated by later high-flow indicators; low unique value for creatinine ratio.

High-flow oxygen use in 13–24h suggests ongoing respiratory compromise. Some prognostic signal via overall severity, but indirect for renal dysfunction and largely redundant with the later 25–36h high-flow feature.

High-flow oxygen use in 25–36h captures persistent respiratory failure closer to the 60h creatinine outcome. Useful as a severity/low-cardiac-output proxy, but still indirect and likely secondary to direct renal/hemodynamic predictors.

Count/frequency of documentation of high-flow oxygen in 0–12h is mainly a monitoring/documentation intensity proxy. Weak physiologic content and early post-op noise make it unlikely to survive sparse selection.

Measurement frequency of high-flow oxygen in 13–24h reflects clinician attention and charting patterns more than physiology. Adds little beyond the binary presence of high-flow and is not a direct renal signal.

Measurement frequency of high-flow oxygen in 25–36h may weakly proxy persistent respiratory instability and ICU intensity. Still largely clinician-behavior/monitoring driven and redundant with the binary high-flow indicators.
Documentation frequency of first-degree AV block in 0–12h is a monitoring/ECG-labeling proxy. Minimal direct linkage to renal hypoperfusion and early timing further reduces predictive value.
Measurement frequency of first-degree AV block in 13–24h reflects charting rather than a strong physiologic driver of AKI. Unlikely to add unique value in a sparse model.

Measurement frequency of first-degree AV block in 25–36h is still primarily documentation intensity. Even though later timing is closer, the signal is indirect and weak for predicting creatinine rise.

Documentation frequency of higher-grade AV block (2nd/3rd degree) in 0–12h may correlate with low cardiac output/bradyarrhythmia risk. However it is a measurement proxy and early post-op conduction issues can be transient, limiting sparse-model priority.

Measurement frequency of 2nd/3rd degree AV block in 13–24h can indicate clinically significant conduction disease with potential hemodynamic impact. Still indirect for AKI and dominated by direct hemodynamic/vasopressor variables if available.

Measurement frequency of 2nd/3rd degree AV block in 25–36h suggests persistent significant conduction abnormality that could impair perfusion near the outcome window. Best of the heart-block measurement features by timing/severity, but remains an indirect monitoring proxy.

Measurement frequency of bundle branch block in 0–12h is largely a baseline ECG finding and documentation proxy. Weak and nonspecific for renal injury prediction, especially in the immediate post-op period.
Documentation frequency of bundle branch block in 13–24h provides little incremental physiologic information beyond comorbidity/ECG status. Low unique value for creatinine ratio in a sparse model.

Measurement frequency of bundle branch block in 25–36h remains a charting/ECG-label proxy with limited causal connection to AKI. Even with later timing, it is unlikely to compete with direct renal or perfusion features.

Average ionized calcium in the immediate post-op window, heavily influenced by transfusion/citrate and supplementation. Weak, indirect link to AKI and likely redundant with later values and other severity/hemodynamic signals.

Mean ionized calcium during early ICU course; may reflect ongoing metabolic derangements or replacement needs. Still an indirect renal signal and usually dominated by more kidney-proximal predictors, but more stable than 0–12h.

Mean ionized calcium closest to the prediction horizon, reflecting more stabilized physiology. Remains a nonspecific marker and likely redundant with extremes or other illness-severity variables, so only modest sparse-model priority.

Peak ionized calcium immediately post-op, often driven by calcium administration rather than intrinsic physiology. Low unique value for predicting creatinine trajectory and prone to treatment confounding.
Maximum ionized calcium in 13–24h may capture aggressive replacement or shifts in acid-base/protein binding. Still largely clinician-driven and indirect for AKI, so limited value in a sparse model.

Maximum ionized calcium in the closest window could reflect ongoing supplementation or metabolic instability. Not a direct renal marker and likely redundant with other severity indicators, so moderate-low priority.

Nadir ionized calcium early post-op can reflect citrate load, hemodilution, or shock physiology. Extremes are more informative than means, but 0–12h is noisy and often transient, limiting sparse-model value.

Lowest ionized calcium in 13–24h may better capture persistent hypocalcemia associated with ongoing critical illness and transfusion burden. As an extreme it can add some unique signal, though still indirect for renal dysfunction.

Nadir ionized calcium in 25–36h is the most temporally relevant extreme and may reflect sustained metabolic/critical illness that tracks with AKI risk. Still not kidney-specific, but among calcium summaries this is the most plausible keeper.
Count/frequency of ionized calcium measurements in 0–12h is mainly a monitoring/intensity proxy. High redundancy with general acuity and clinician behavior; not a physiologic renal signal.

Measurement frequency of ionized calcium in 13–24h reflects care patterns (e.g., ongoing transfusion/instability) more than kidney function. Typically weak and dominated by direct physiologic variables.

Measurement frequency in 25–36h may indicate persistent instability prompting repeated labs, which can correlate with evolving AKI. Still a proxy variable, so only slight priority increase due to temporal proximity.

Average magnesium early post-op is influenced by routine replacement and dilutional effects. Weak, nonspecific association with AKI and likely redundant with later magnesium or other severity markers.

Mean magnesium in 13–24h may reflect ongoing replacement needs or renal handling, but is still indirect. Some potential signal (reduced clearance vs supplementation), yet usually not sparse-model competitive.

Mean magnesium closest to Hour 60 can reflect impaired renal excretion (if elevated) or persistent replacement needs, making it more informative than earlier windows. Still confounded by supplementation, but may add modest unique value.
Peak magnesium in 0–12h is commonly driven by perioperative administration and hemodilution rather than renal clearance. Early timing and treatment confounding make it a poor sparse-model candidate.

Peak magnesium in 13–24h reflects supplementation/renal handling but is usually clinician-driven and weakly tied to evolving AKI. Likely redundant with later (25–36h) magnesium extremes and not a primary renal signal for sparse selection.

Peak magnesium in 25–36h can rise with reduced renal clearance and is closer to the Hour-60 outcome window. Still often influenced by replacement practices, so it’s a moderate, not top-tier, sparse-model candidate.
Early nadir magnesium is commonly driven by bypass-related shifts and routine repletion immediately post-op. High noise and timing far from Hour 60 make it a weak sparse-selection feature.
Low magnesium in 13–24h may reflect ongoing losses or under-repletion rather than kidney dysfunction. Some signal but largely treatment-dependent and dominated by later-window values.

Low magnesium in 25–36h is closer to outcome but typically reflects replacement strategy/diuresis more than impaired filtration. Provides limited unique AKI prediction compared with stronger perfusion/acid-base markers.
Count/frequency of magnesium checks in 0–12h is mainly a monitoring-intensity proxy in the immediate post-op period. Not a direct physiologic marker and unlikely to survive sparse screening.
Measurement frequency in 13–24h reflects clinician concern and protocolized lab schedules more than renal physiology. Low unique predictive value for creatinine ratio.
More frequent magnesium checks in 25–36h can proxy ongoing instability/renal concern closer to the outcome window. Still a behavior/monitoring signal, so only modest priority.

Average base excess in 0–12h summarizes early metabolic acidosis/alkalosis after surgery, which is often transient and confounded by perfusion and ventilation changes. Mean also smooths short insults, limiting sparse-model value.
Mean base excess in 13–24h reflects sustained metabolic derangement and can correlate with ongoing hypoperfusion and AKI risk. Useful but somewhat redundant with extreme (min) base excess measures.

Mean base excess in 25–36h captures persistent metabolic acidosis/alkalosis close to Hour 60, aligning with ongoing shock/renal hypoperfusion physiology. Stronger timing makes it a good sparse-model keeper, though still less specific than extremes.
Maximum base excess (most alkalemic) in 0–12h often reflects ventilation/bicarbonate administration rather than renal injury. Early timing and directionality make it low-yield for sparse selection.
Most alkalemic base excess in 13–24h is typically treatment/ventilation related and less directly tied to AKI than acidosis. Likely dominated by base excess minima/means for renal-risk signal.

Peak base excess in 25–36h may indicate overcorrection/diuresis effects but is not a strong mechanistic precursor of creatinine rise. Kept low because acidosis extremes are usually more informative for hypoperfusion-related AKI.

Minimum base excess in 0–12h captures worst early metabolic acidosis, which can reflect low cardiac output and hypoperfusion. However, immediate post-op noise reduces reliability for Hour-60 prediction.

Minimum base excess in 13–24h captures significant metabolic acidosis after initial stabilization, a strong marker of ongoing hypoperfusion/illness severity linked to AKI. As an extreme, it preserves insult signal and is a high-priority sparse feature (though slightly less proximal than 25–36h measures).

Minimum base excess in the 25–36h window captures worst metabolic acidosis, a marker of hypoperfusion/shock that can precede AKI. Among base-excess variants here, this extreme and closest window is the most likely to add unique predictive value for Hour-60 creatinine ratio.

Count/frequency of base excess measurements in 0–12h mainly reflects monitoring intensity and early post-op instability rather than renal physiology. Likely redundant with actual base-excess values and weak for sparse selection.
Measurement frequency of base excess in 13–24h is a clinician-behavior/acuity proxy, not a direct biomarker. Unlikely to survive sparse selection versus keeping an extreme base-excess value.

More frequent base-excess checks in 25–36h may indicate ongoing instability and concern for perfusion problems. Still largely a monitoring proxy and typically dominated by the actual base-excess extreme (e.g., min) for sparse modeling.

Frequency of direct bilirubin measurement early post-op reflects ordering/monitoring patterns rather than kidney-specific physiology. Early window and proxy nature make it low priority for a sparse model.

Direct bilirubin measurement frequency in 13–24h is primarily a clinician-suspicion/complexity signal (hepatic dysfunction workup) and indirect for AKI. Low unique value for predicting creatinine ratio.

More frequent direct bilirubin checks in 25–36h may correlate with evolving multi-organ dysfunction, which can co-occur with AKI. Still an indirect monitoring proxy and unlikely to beat direct renal/perfusion markers in sparse selection.

Mean WBC in 0–12h reflects inflammatory response/stress and possible infection but is nonspecific and noisy immediately post-bypass. Likely redundant with later WBC summaries and weakly linked to creatinine rise.

Mean WBC in 13–24h captures sustained inflammation but remains a generic severity marker rather than a direct renal signal. Some predictive value, but usually dominated by later timing or extremes if kept at all.

Mean WBC in 25–36h is closer to the outcome and may reflect ongoing systemic inflammation/infection contributing to AKI risk. Among WBC means, this timing is most useful, though still indirect and potentially redundant with WBC extremes.

Peak WBC in 0–12h may reflect acute stress response and early inflammation but is often transient and nonspecific post-cardiac surgery. Early timing limits its sparse-model priority versus later peaks.
Peak WBC in 13–24h can indicate evolving infection/inflammatory burden, which can contribute to AKI via sepsis physiology. More informative than early peaks, but still an indirect severity marker.

Peak WBC in 25–36h is the closest window and captures extreme inflammatory response that may accompany hemodynamic instability and AKI risk. Useful but not top-tier because it is nonspecific and may overlap with WBC mean/min information.
Minimum WBC in 0–12h is rarely a direct driver of AKI and may reflect dilutional effects or lab variability early post-op. Low unique predictive value and likely noise in sparse selection.
Minimum WBC in 13–24h is generally less informative than peaks/means for inflammatory burden and is not kidney-specific. Likely redundant and low priority for a sparse model.

Minimum WBC in 25–36h could weakly reflect immunosuppression/consumption in severe illness, but it is still nonspecific for renal dysfunction. Lower priority than WBC max/mean and perfusion/acidosis markers.
Count of WBC checks in the first 12h is mainly a monitoring-intensity/clinician-behavior proxy. Weak, indirect link to AKI and likely dominated by physiologic/hemodynamic and renal markers.
Frequency of WBC measurement reflects concern/complexity rather than kidney physiology. Unlikely to add unique predictive value for 60h creatinine ratio in a sparse model.

Later measurement frequency may better capture evolving complications/infection workup closer to the outcome window. Still largely a proxy variable and typically redundant with actual lab values and severity signals.

Average PT in the immediate post-op period reflects coagulopathy/hemodilution and liver function, which can correlate with illness severity. Early mean is noisy and less informative than later/extreme PT summaries for sparse selection.

Sustained PT elevation at 25–36h can indicate ongoing hepatic dysfunction/consumption and overall physiologic stress associated with AKI risk. Mean is still somewhat redundant with max, but timing makes it a moderate candidate.
Peak PT early may capture transient post-bypass coagulopathy or bleeding-related dilution. Useful as a severity marker but early timing and non-renal specificity limit sparse-model priority.

Peak PT at 25–36h captures the worst ongoing coagulopathy/possible hepatic hypoperfusion closer to the 60h creatinine outcome. Among PT variants, this extreme in the latest window is most likely to add unique predictive value.
Minimum PT is generally not pathophysiologically informative for AKI compared with elevations. Early minima are likely noise and dominated by max/mean PT features.
Low/normal PT at 25–36h provides little incremental information about renal hypoperfusion or evolving AKI. Redundant once max/mean PT are available.
Number of PT checks early reflects monitoring/bleeding concern rather than a direct renal signal. Low unique value and likely redundant with actual PT levels.
PT measurement frequency is a clinician-behavior proxy and not a direct biomarker of kidney injury. Unlikely to survive sparse selection when physiologic summaries are included.

Later frequent PT checks may indicate ongoing bleeding/coagulopathy management, which can correlate with AKI risk via shock/transfusion. Still indirect and typically weaker than PT max/mean themselves.
NRBCs reflect marrow stress/hypoxemia/inflammation and correlate with critical illness severity. Early post-op values can be transient and are indirect for AKI, so lower sparse priority.
Persistently elevated NRBCs by 13–24h suggest ongoing hypoxia/systemic stress that can precede AKI. Moderate value but still a nonspecific severity marker.
NRBC elevation at 25–36h indicates sustained physiologic stress/hypoperfusion closer to the 60h creatinine outcome. Likely the most informative NRBC summary in this batch for sparse selection.
Peak NRBC early may capture acute perioperative stress but is often noisy and influenced by transfusion/CPB effects. Less useful than later NRBC summaries for predicting 60h creatinine ratio.

Peak NRBCs in 13–24h reflects systemic stress/inflammation/hypoxemia rather than a direct renal signal. Likely a weak, nonspecific severity proxy and dominated by closer (25–36h) values if any signal exists.

Peak NRBCs in 25–36h may capture ongoing physiologic stress closer to the creatinine outcome. Still indirect for AKI and typically unlikely to survive sparse selection versus hemodynamics/renal markers.
Lowest NRBCs in 0–12h is largely uninformative (often near zero) and reflects early post-op noise. Minimal unique value for predicting Hour-60 creatinine ratio.
Minimum NRBCs in 13–24h is not a meaningful insult marker and is usually redundant with presence/absence. Very low priority for a sparse model.
Minimum NRBCs in 25–36h provides little physiologic information compared with maxima or other severity markers. Unlikely to add unique predictive value for AKI trajectory.
Count of NRBC measurements in 0–12h is mainly a monitoring-intensity/clinician-behavior proxy. Early window and indirectness make it a poor sparse-model candidate.
NRBC measurement frequency in 13–24h reflects lab ordering patterns and illness complexity more than kidney physiology. Low unique value and likely redundant with other acuity indicators.
NRBC measurement frequency in 25–36h may weakly proxy ongoing concern/instability closer to outcome. Still a behavior/monitoring signal, so only modest priority.
Average glucose in 0–12h reflects stress hyperglycemia and perioperative management but is early and often protocol-driven. Likely redundant with later glucose summaries and general severity.

Mean glucose in 13–24h captures sustained dysglycemia after initial post-op noise and can correlate with illness severity and AKI risk. Moderate value, but less informative than later window and extremes.
Mean glucose in 25–36h reflects ongoing metabolic stress closest to the Hour-60 creatinine outcome. Useful but still indirect and potentially redundant with glucose extremes.
Peak glucose in 0–12h captures acute stress hyperglycemia but is heavily influenced by intra/post-op dextrose/insulin protocols. Early timing reduces reliability for Hour-60 renal prediction.

Peak glucose in 13–24h may better reflect true stress response and is an extreme that can outperform means for capturing insults. Still an indirect AKI predictor and may be redundant with 25–36h peak.

Peak glucose in 25–36h is the closest extreme measure of ongoing stress/catecholamine exposure and correlates with severity that can precede AKI. Among glucose features, this is the strongest sparse-model candidate due to timing and extreme aggregation.
Minimum glucose in 0–12h may indicate hypoglycemia from insulin therapy, which can mark instability but is often iatrogenic. Early window and protocol effects limit unique predictive value.

Minimum glucose in 13–24h can capture clinically meaningful hypoglycemia/over-insulinization and physiologic fragility beyond averages. Moderate priority, though still indirect for renal dysfunction and potentially redundant with other instability markers.

Lowest glucose in the 25–36h window reflects hypoglycemia/stress-dysglycemia and overall physiologic instability. It can add some unique signal for AKI risk but is indirect and likely weaker than hemodynamic/renal-specific predictors, so mid-priority.

Count/frequency of glucose checks in the first 12h is mainly a monitoring-intensity/insulin-protocol proxy. Early post-op measurement frequency is noisy and not a direct renal signal, so low sparse-selection value.

Frequency of glucose measurements in 13–24h reflects clinician concern/ICU workflow more than physiology. As a proxy variable it is unlikely to survive sparse selection when actual glucose values or organ-perfusion markers are available.

Glucose measurement frequency at 25–36h may capture ongoing instability or insulin infusion needs closer to the outcome time. Still largely a clinician-behavior proxy and dominated by actual glucose extremes, so only weak-to-modest priority.

Average platelet count in the immediate 0–12h post-op period reflects CPB-related consumption/hemodilution and bleeding risk. It is an indirect AKI correlate and early values are noisy, so lower priority versus later/extreme platelet signals.

Mean platelets at 13–24h reflects evolving thrombocytopenia/inflammation. Still an averaged (smoothed) signal and indirect for renal injury, making it a weaker sparse-model candidate than platelet minima later.

Mean platelet count at 25–36h captures sustained thrombocytopenia closer to Hour 60, which can track systemic inflammation/complications associated with AKI. More temporally relevant than earlier means but still somewhat redundant with platelet minima.

Maximum platelet count in 0–12h is rarely pathophysiologically informative for AKI and is influenced by transfusion/hemodilution artifacts. It is dominated by minima/trajectory measures, so very low priority.
Peak platelets in 13–24h is not a typical driver or strong marker of renal dysfunction and is often clinically uninformative. Redundant/noisy compared with platelet minima, so low importance.
Maximum platelets at 25–36h provides little unique information about impending AKI compared with thrombocytopenia measures. Likely redundant and low yield for sparse selection.

Lowest platelet count in 0–12h captures early thrombocytopenia from CPB/bleeding/transfusion needs, which can correlate with complications and AKI risk. However early post-op noise limits reliability for Hour 60, so moderate priority.

Platelet nadir in 13–24h reflects evolving thrombocytopenia/inflammation and is less dominated by immediate post-bypass dilution. As an extreme value it preserves the clinically relevant signal and is a strong sparse-model candidate.

Platelet nadir in 25–36h is the closest and most informative extreme for ongoing thrombocytopenia, often tracking systemic complications (sepsis/bleeding/low-flow states) linked to AKI by Hour 60. High unique predictive value within the platelet family, so top priority.

Frequency of platelet measurements in 0–12h is primarily a monitoring/bleeding-workup proxy rather than a physiologic renal marker. Early post-op practice variation makes it unlikely to add stable predictive value.

Platelet measurement frequency in 13–24h reflects clinician concern and lab-ordering patterns more than kidney physiology. Typically redundant once platelet values themselves are included, so low priority.

More frequent platelet checks at 25–36h may indicate ongoing bleeding/instability closer to the outcome window. Still a proxy variable and generally dominated by platelet nadir/level features, so only modest importance.

Mean corpuscular hemoglobin (MCH) reflects RBC indices/anemia phenotype rather than acute renal physiology. Early post-op mean values are unlikely to add unique signal for Hour-60 creatinine ratio and are dominated by hemodynamics/renal markers.

MCH mean in 13–24h is a stable hematologic index with weak mechanistic linkage to evolving AKI. Low sparse-model priority because it is indirect and largely redundant with broader anemia/bleeding/transfusion signals not captured here.

MCH mean at 25–36h remains a chronic RBC characteristic and not a near-direct precursor of renal dysfunction. Even with closer timing, it is unlikely to survive sparse selection versus more proximal perfusion/renal biomarkers.

Maximum MCH in 0–12h mainly reflects lab variability or RBC population shifts, not kidney injury. The extreme aggregation does not capture a meaningful renal insult and is low-value for sparse selection.
Peak MCH in 13–24h is not a recognized driver or early marker of AKI after cardiac surgery. Likely noise/redundant with other hematology indices, so low priority.
Peak MCH at 25–36h is still a hematologic index with minimal direct relationship to creatinine rise. Unlikely to add unique predictive value in a sparse linear model.

Minimum MCH in the immediate post-op window is more reflective of baseline anemia/microcytosis or measurement variation than renal hypoperfusion. Early timing and weak causal link make it a poor sparse-model candidate.
Low MCH in 13–24h indicates RBC indices rather than acute kidney stress. It is indirect and unlikely to compete with more proximal physiologic predictors of AKI.
Low MCH at 25–36h remains a chronic hematologic feature with little near-term information about impending creatinine rise. Redundant/low signal for sparse selection.
Count of MCH measurements is primarily a monitoring-intensity/ordering behavior proxy. In 0–12h it is especially routine post-op care and unlikely to add unique predictive value.
Measurement frequency of MCH reflects lab ordering patterns rather than physiology. As a proxy feature it is low priority for a sparse model predicting creatinine ratio.
MCH measurement count at 25–36h may weakly reflect complexity but is still largely clinician-behavior driven. Default low score because it is not a direct renal signal and is noisy.

Mean sodium in 0–12h reflects fluid balance, dilution, and perioperative shifts that can correlate with illness severity. Early post-op sodium is often confounded by bypass/fluids, so it has limited unique value for Hour-60 creatinine ratio.

Mean sodium in 13–24h can capture evolving free-water balance/diuresis and neurohormonal stress that may track with AKI risk. Moderate sparse-model value as a systemic marker, though still indirect compared with direct renal/perfusion variables.

Mean sodium at 25–36h is closer to the outcome and may reflect ongoing fluid management, diuretic response, and renal concentrating ability. Useful but indirect, so a mid-tier keeper if feature budget allows.

Peak sodium in 0–12h may indicate hypernatremia from diuresis or sodium load, but in the immediate post-op period it is often transient and treatment-driven. The extreme is potentially more informative than the mean, yet still an indirect AKI predictor here.

Peak sodium in the 13–24h window reflects hypernatremia/free-water deficit or diuretic effect. It is an indirect, nonspecific renal-risk signal and is largely redundant with the closer 25–36h sodium extremes for sparse selection.

Peak sodium in the 25–36h window can capture evolving hypernatremia from diuresis, inadequate free water, or concentrating defects during early AKI. It’s the most temporally proximal sodium extreme here, but remains an indirect marker so not top-tier.

Early postoperative nadir sodium often reflects bypass/hemodilution and immediate fluid shifts. This window is noisy and the signal is indirect for Hour-60 creatinine ratio, so it is unlikely to survive sparse screening.

Nadir sodium in 13–24h may reflect ongoing dilutional hyponatremia or excess free water, sometimes tracking severity/volume management. Still indirect for AKI and dominated by the closer 25–36h sodium information.

Nadir sodium in 25–36h captures later dilutional states or impaired water handling closer to the outcome time. Among sodium features it has better temporal proximity, but sodium remains a weak-to-moderate proxy rather than a direct renal biomarker.
Count/indicator of sodium measurements in 0–12h is primarily a monitoring-intensity/clinical workflow proxy. It adds little unique physiologic information and is especially noisy immediately post-op.

Sodium measurement frequency in 13–24h mainly reflects clinician concern, lab ordering patterns, or complexity of care. As a proxy variable it is unlikely to add stable, unique predictive value in a sparse linear model.
More frequent sodium checks in 25–36h can indicate evolving electrolyte/volume issues and higher acuity closer to the outcome. Still largely a behavior/monitoring proxy, so only modest priority.

Whether TEG platelet aggregation was measured early post-op reflects bleeding/coagulopathy evaluation rather than renal physiology. It is a clinician-behavior proxy and unlikely to contribute uniquely to creatinine ratio prediction.

TEG platelet aggregation measurement in 13–24h indicates ongoing hemostasis assessment (e.g., bleeding/transfusion management). This is indirect for AKI and mostly a care-process marker, so low sparse-model value.

Late TEG platelet aggregation measurement suggests continued bleeding/coagulopathy workup. It does not directly encode renal perfusion/function and is primarily a workflow proxy, so minimal selection priority.

Mean MCHC in 0–12h reflects red cell indices influenced by hemodilution/transfusion and lab variability. It has no direct mechanistic link to AKI and is dominated by more relevant hemodynamic/renal markers (not present here).

Mean MCHC in 13–24h is a stable hematologic index with limited sensitivity to acute renal hypoperfusion or evolving AKI. It is unlikely to add unique predictive value for creatinine ratio in a sparse model.

Mean MCHC in 25–36h remains a nonspecific RBC index and is not a meaningful precursor of AKI compared with physiologic/renal variables. Temporal proximity does not overcome its weak mechanistic relevance.
Maximum MCHC in 0–12h may reflect lab artifact, hemoconcentration, or transfusion effects immediately post-op. It is a weak, noisy signal for renal outcomes and redundant with other MCHC summaries.

Maximum MCHC in 13–24h is still a nonspecific hematology extreme with limited connection to kidney injury trajectory. It is unlikely to survive sparse feature selection given low unique renal predictive content.

Peak MCHC in the 25–36h window; mainly reflects RBC indices/hemoconcentration or lab artifact rather than renal physiology. Low unique value for AKI prediction and dominated by more direct perfusion/renal markers (and even hematocrit for concentration).
Lowest MCHC immediately post-op, a period with hemodilution/transfusion noise. Weak, indirect link to kidney injury and unlikely to survive sparse selection.
Lowest MCHC in 13–24h; reflects anemia/hemoconcentration patterns but is not a direct renal signal. Redundant with hematocrit/hemoglobin-type information and low incremental predictive value.

Lowest MCHC closest to the outcome window; could weakly proxy ongoing dilution/bleeding/transfusion burden. Still indirect and largely redundant with hematocrit extremes, so only a marginal candidate.
Count/frequency of MCHC measurements in 0–12h, primarily a monitoring-intensity/clinician-behavior proxy. Early post-op measurement frequency is noisy and not a direct kidney dysfunction marker.
Measurement frequency of MCHC in 13–24h reflects lab ordering intensity rather than physiology. Low priority for sparse modeling versus physiologic extremes.
Measurement frequency in 25–36h may weakly indicate clinical concern/instability closer to Hour 60. Still a proxy variable and generally dominated by actual hemodynamic/lab values.

Average hematocrit in the immediate post-op window, heavily influenced by bypass-related hemodilution and transfusions. Some relation to oxygen delivery but early mean is noisy and less informative than later extremes.
Mean hematocrit in 13–24h reflects anemia/hemodilution which can affect renal oxygen delivery. However, the mean smooths important nadirs and is redundant with min/max hematocrit measures.

Mean hematocrit closest to the prediction horizon may capture sustained anemia/hemodilution affecting renal oxygen delivery. Still less sensitive than the nadir and partly redundant with hematocrit_min.
Highest hematocrit in 0–12h is often driven by hemoconcentration/transfusion and early fluid shifts. Weak and noisy for predicting later creatinine rise compared with later-window nadirs.
Peak hematocrit in 13–24h mainly reflects concentration/transfusion rather than renal hypoperfusion risk. Provides little unique AKI signal relative to hematocrit_min and later timing.

Peak hematocrit in 25–36h may indicate hemoconcentration from diuresis/negative balance, which can correlate with perfusion status. Still indirect and generally less informative than hematocrit_min for oxygen delivery risk.

Lowest hematocrit early post-op can reflect significant hemodilution/bleeding/transfusion needs impacting oxygen delivery. Despite early noise, the nadir is the most informative aggregation within this epoch.

Nadir hematocrit in 13–24h captures clinically meaningful anemia after initial stabilization, affecting renal oxygen delivery and AKI risk. Stronger and less noisy than 0–12h and more signal-preserving than means/max.

Nadir hematocrit in 25–36h is closest to Hour 60 and best captures ongoing anemia/hemodilution that can impair renal oxygen delivery. Among this batch it is the most pathophysiologically relevant extreme and likely to survive sparse selection.

Count of hematocrit checks in the first 12h is mainly a monitoring/intensity proxy rather than renal physiology. Early post-op labs are frequent and noisy, so this is unlikely to add unique value in a sparse model.
Measurement frequency reflects clinician concern/complexity more than kidney injury risk. Still indirect and dominated by actual hemodynamics/renal markers, so low sparse-selection priority.

Later (25–36h) monitoring intensity may weakly track evolving instability/bleeding that can contribute to AKI risk. However it remains a behavior proxy and is unlikely to survive sparse selection over physiologic extremes.

Mean basophil count is a nonspecific CBC differential component with little direct linkage to renal hypoperfusion/AKI. Early post-op values are noisy and this signal is unlikely to be selected sparsely.

Basophil mean at 13–24h is still a weak, nonspecific inflammatory marker and typically not predictive of creatinine trajectory. Redundant with broader WBC/inflammation signals not shown here, so low priority.
Peak basophil count in 0–12h is unlikely to capture a meaningful renal insult and is prone to lab noise/outliers. Not a plausible sparse-model keeper for Hour-60 creatinine ratio.
Max basophil count at 13–24h remains nonspecific and rarely drives AKI prediction compared with hemodynamics, lactate, urine output, or creatinine trends. Adds minimal unique value.
Minimum basophil count early post-op is largely measurement variability and not mechanistically tied to renal dysfunction. Very unlikely to survive feature selection.
Minimum basophil count at 13–24h is still a weak, indirect marker with high redundancy/noise. Low expected incremental predictive value for creatinine_ratio.
Number of basophil measurements is essentially CBC draw frequency, a clinician-behavior proxy. Early frequent labs are routine after cardiac surgery, so it provides little discriminative signal.
CBC measurement frequency at 13–24h reflects monitoring intensity rather than kidney physiology. Unlikely to add unique predictive value in a sparse linear model.

More frequent CBCs at 25–36h may weakly indicate ongoing complications (bleeding/infection) that correlate with AKI risk. Still indirect and dominated by direct renal/hemodynamic features, so only modest priority.

Mean lymphocyte percentage reflects stress/inflammation (often lymphopenia) and overall illness severity rather than direct renal injury. Early window is noisy, so it’s a weak-to-moderate sparse candidate.

At 13–24h, sustained lymphopenia can better reflect systemic inflammatory response/severity that correlates with AKI risk. Still indirect, but more stable than 0–12h and may add some unique prognostic value.

Maximum lymphocyte percentage in the immediate post-op period is not a clear renal-risk exposure and is sensitive to transient shifts/dilution. Likely redundant with mean and low value for sparse selection.

Max lymphocyte percentage at 13–24h is less interpretable than sustained lymphopenia (mean) and may reflect variability rather than a pathologic extreme. Some weak severity signal but generally dominated by the mean.

Early postoperative lymphopenia reflects stress/inflammation rather than a direct renal signal. In a sparse model it is an indirect severity marker and the 0–12h window is noisy for predicting Hour-60 creatinine.

Minimum lymphocyte percent in 13–24h may track ongoing inflammatory response and frailty/illness severity. Still indirect for AKI and likely dominated by more kidney-proximal hemodynamic/lab features, so low sparse-selection priority.
Measurement frequency is mainly a monitoring-intensity/ordering behavior proxy rather than physiology. In 0–12h it is especially routine and unlikely to add unique predictive value.
Counts how often lymphocyte percent was checked, reflecting clinician behavior and care complexity. Weak and non-specific for renal dysfunction, so unlikely to survive sparse selection.
Later measurement frequency can proxy evolving concern/infection workup closer to the outcome window. Still a behavior proxy (not a biomarker), so only modest value even though timing is closer.

Having a random vancomycin level drawn suggests vancomycin exposure and/or concern for altered clearance. However it is a clinician-behavior proxy and early timing is less informative for Hour-60 creatinine ratio.

Random vancomycin level measurement in 13–24h may indicate nephrotoxin exposure or suspected impaired clearance. Still indirect and redundant with stronger renal signals (urine output/creatinine trends), so low-moderate priority.

Random vancomycin level measurement at 25–36h is closer to Hour 60 and may capture emerging reduced clearance prompting monitoring. As a proxy it’s not top-tier, but timing gives it more unique predictive value than earlier epochs.

Trough measurement implies vancomycin dosing/monitoring and potential nephrotoxin exposure. Early postoperative trough checks are often protocolized and behavior-driven, limiting unique predictive value.
Trough monitoring in 13–24h may reflect ongoing vancomycin therapy and concern for accumulation. Still largely an ordering proxy and likely redundant with direct renal function markers.

Trough measured at 25–36h can signal suspected accumulation/reduced clearance nearer the prediction horizon. While still a proxy, it may add incremental information about evolving AKI risk compared with earlier trough indicators.

Average oxygen saturation reflects respiratory status and global illness severity, which can contribute indirectly to renal hypoxia. Mean SpO2 in 0–12h is often stable/ceilinged and noisy post-op, so weak sparse-model value.
Mean SpO2 in 13–24h captures sustained hypoxemia better than immediate post-op values. Still an indirect, non-specific severity marker and often redundant with hemodynamics/ventilator variables.

Mean SpO2 at 25–36h is the closest window and sustained hypoxemia can contribute to renal injury risk. It remains indirect and ceiling-limited, but timing makes it a more plausible sparse-model keeper than earlier means.
Maximum SpO2 is typically near 100% and reflects supplemental oxygen rather than pathologic insult. As an extreme it does not capture hypoxemia and is largely uninformative for AKI prediction.

Max SpO2 in 13–24h is usually a ceiling effect and dominated by mean/min saturation if available. Provides little unique information for creatinine ratio and is unlikely to be selected in a sparse model.
Represents best oxygenation achieved in the 25–36h window. Max SpO2 is rarely informative for AKI risk and is dominated by hypoxemia extremes and hemodynamic/renal markers in sparse selection.

Captures early postoperative hypoxemia episodes. Hypoxemia can contribute to renal injury but 0–12h is noisy after surgery and is less proximal than later minima, so it’s a weaker sparse-model candidate.
Represents hypoxemia in the 13–24h period, reflecting evolving respiratory/cardiac dysfunction. Still an indirect AKI signal and likely redundant with the closer 25–36h minimum if included.

Captures the worst hypoxemia closest to the prediction horizon, which can reflect ongoing cardiopulmonary failure and renal hypoperfusion risk. Among SpO2 features, this extreme and timing make it the most defensible sparse inclusion, though still indirect.
Number of SpO2 measurements in 0–12h is mainly a monitoring-intensity/acuity proxy. It adds little unique physiologic information and is unlikely to survive sparse selection.

Measurement frequency of SpO2 in 13–24h reflects clinician behavior and care intensity more than renal pathophysiology. Typically redundant with other severity markers and low value for a sparse linear model.

SpO2 measurement count in 25–36h is a proxy for ongoing instability/monitoring rather than a direct biomarker. Even with better timing, it’s weak and likely dominated by actual SpO2 minima/maxima and renal labs.

Average ALT in the immediate postoperative window reflects hepatic injury/ischemia or baseline liver disease. It is an indirect, early, and smoothed signal that is unlikely to add unique predictive value for creatinine ratio.

Peak ALT early post-op can indicate ischemic hepatitis/low-flow states that may co-occur with AKI. Still indirect and early (0–12h), but the extreme is more informative than mean/min, giving modest sparse-model value.
Minimum ALT is not clinically meaningful for organ injury and mostly reflects baseline/assay noise. It is dominated by ALT max (if any ALT feature is kept) and offers little unique AKI prediction.
ALT measurement count in 0–12h is primarily a clinician-suspicion/monitoring proxy. It does not directly encode renal physiology and is low priority for sparse selection.
Frequency of ALT checks in 13–24h reflects care patterns and concern for liver injury rather than kidney function. Adds minimal unique signal and is unlikely to be selected in a sparse model.

ALT measurement frequency in 25–36h remains a monitoring-intensity proxy. Even with closer timing, it is weakly related to creatinine trajectory and dominated by direct renal markers like BUN/creatinine/urine output (if available).

Mean BUN early post-op reflects baseline renal reserve, catabolism, and volume status, which relate to AKI risk. However, 0–12h is less reliable and less proximal than later BUN, so it’s moderate priority.

Mean BUN in 13–24h captures early renal dysfunction/azotemia trajectory beyond immediate post-op noise. It is a relatively direct renal-related marker and a strong sparse-model candidate unless dominated by an even closer BUN measure.

Mean BUN in 25–36h is the closest available azotemia signal to Hour 60 and often tracks evolving AKI and perfusion/volume status. Within this batch it is the most direct, proximal renal marker and highest priority for sparse selection.

Early peak BUN reflects azotemia/renal hypoperfusion but is confounded by immediate post-bypass hemodilution and catabolic shifts. Useful signal, yet likely dominated by later (25–36h) BUN for Hour-60 creatinine trajectory.
Peak BUN in 13–24h captures evolving azotemia after initial post-op noise settles. Strong renal-relevant predictor, though still somewhat redundant with the closer 25–36h peak.

Closest-window peak BUN is a direct marker of worsening azotemia and correlates with evolving AKI leading into Hour 60. Among BUN summaries, this extreme and timing best preserve the renal signal and is highest-priority for sparse selection.
Minimum BUN in the immediate post-op window mainly reflects dilution/fluids rather than renal dysfunction. Low unique value and likely noisy/redundant versus max BUN measures.
Minimum BUN is not a pathophysiologic AKI signal and is heavily influenced by volume status and sampling. Unlikely to survive sparse selection given stronger max/late BUN features.
Later minimum BUN may weakly reflect ongoing dilution or nutrition status but is not a direct renal injury marker. Still dominated by 25–36h BUN max for predicting creatinine rise.
Count/frequency of BUN measurements is primarily a monitoring-intensity/clinician-behavior proxy. Adds little physiologic information beyond the BUN values themselves, especially early post-op.
Measurement frequency reflects concern/complexity rather than kidney function directly. Likely redundant with actual BUN levels and other severity markers in a sparse model.
Later frequent BUN checks can proxy evolving renal concern closer to the outcome window. Still indirect and typically dominated by the actual 25–36h BUN max value.
Whether amylase was checked early is a clinician-suspicion proxy (e.g., pancreatitis/ischemia) rather than a renal biomarker. Very low expected unique value for creatinine_ratio.
Amylase measurement indicates diagnostic workup intensity and non-renal complications. Unlikely to add independent predictive signal for AKI beyond general severity.
Later amylase measurement remains a behavior/complication proxy and not a direct renal physiology feature. Low priority for sparse selection versus renal/hemodynamic markers.

Mean pH early post-op reflects acid-base status but is influenced by ventilation, transfusion, and bypass-related shifts. Indirect for AKI and likely less informative than later trajectory or lactate/hypotension features (if available).
Mean pH in 13–24h captures persistent acidosis/poor perfusion after initial stabilization, which can precede AKI. Moderately useful but still a nonspecific severity marker.

Closest-window sustained acidemia suggests ongoing hypoperfusion/shock physiology that can drive creatinine rise by Hour 60. Mean is appropriate here because persistence (not a brief dip) is clinically meaningful, giving it strong sparse-model value.
Early maximum pH mainly reflects transient alkalosis from hyperventilation/bicarbonate and is not a renal injury signal. Low unique predictive value and likely noise for Hour-60 creatinine_ratio.
Represents the highest alkalemia in the 13–24h window. Max pH is usually less informative for AKI risk than acidemia/hypoperfusion signals and is largely redundant with pH_min/other severity markers.

Represents the highest alkalemia closest to the prediction time. Still typically weaker/less specific for evolving AKI than low pH (acidemia) and likely redundant with other physiologic severity features.

Represents worst acidemia immediately post-op, which can reflect hypoperfusion/lactate burden associated with AKI. Scored moderate because 0–12h is noisy after bypass/anesthesia and may not persist to Hour 60.

Represents worst acidemia in the 13–24h window, a stronger indicator of ongoing hypoperfusion/shock physiology linked to AKI. This extreme aggregation is more likely to add unique predictive value than pH_max or measurement counts.

Represents worst acidemia in the 25–36h window, the closest available physiologic snapshot to Hour 60 and a strong marker of ongoing hypoperfusion/critical illness driving AKI. Among pH features, this extreme and timing make it the top sparse-model candidate.

Counts/indicates frequency of pH measurement in 0–12h, mainly reflecting monitoring intensity and clinician concern. This is a proxy variable and early post-op practice-driven, so low priority for sparse selection.
Measurement frequency of pH in 13–24h is primarily a clinician-behavior/acuity proxy rather than a physiologic renal signal. Likely redundant with actual pH extremes and other severity indicators.
Measurement frequency of pH closest to Hour 60 may weakly capture ongoing instability prompting repeated ABGs. Still largely a monitoring-intensity proxy and dominated by the actual pH_min value.
Whether cholesterol was checked in 0–12h is almost entirely practice-pattern driven and not mechanistically tied to short-term creatinine rise. Very unlikely to survive sparse feature selection.
Whether cholesterol was checked in 13–24h reflects ordering behavior rather than renal physiology. Adds little unique predictive value for Hour 60 creatinine ratio.
Whether cholesterol was checked in 25–36h is a weak, non-specific proxy for care patterns and not a direct AKI precursor. Low priority for a sparse linear model.
Mean lymphocyte count in 0–12h reflects stress response/inflammation and overall illness severity. Early post-op values are noisy and this is indirect for AKI, so low-to-moderate sparse value.

Mean lymphocyte count in 13–24h is an indirect marker of stress/inflammation that may correlate with complications. Likely redundant with stronger hemodynamic/acid-base signals and not kidney-specific.
Maximum lymphocyte count in 0–12h is not a typical AKI-relevant extreme and is heavily influenced by perioperative shifts. Adds minimal unique information for creatinine trajectory.
Maximum lymphocyte count in 13–24h is a weak and non-specific signal for AKI risk. Unlikely to be selected over more direct perfusion/acid-base or renal markers.

Minimum lymphocyte count in 0–12h captures lymphopenia from stress/inflammation, which can track with postoperative complications and AKI risk. Scored moderate because it is indirect and early, but the extreme (min) is more informative than mean/max within this family.

Minimum absolute lymphocyte count reflects stress/inflammation and possible infection risk rather than renal physiology. It is an indirect illness-severity marker and unlikely to add unique value for creatinine_ratio versus more kidney/perfusion-specific features.
Whether ALC was measured is mainly a monitoring/clinician-behavior proxy in the immediate post-op window. Low direct renal signal and high noise/redundancy make it a poor sparse-model candidate.
ALC measurement frequency indicates care intensity rather than kidney function. Even at 13–24h it is a weak, indirect proxy and unlikely to survive sparse selection.
ALC measured at 25–36h still reflects monitoring patterns more than physiology. Despite closer timing, it provides little unique predictive information for AKI compared with direct renal markers.

Glucose measurement is a routine monitoring indicator and not a direct biomarker of renal injury. In the 0–12h window it is especially dominated by standard post-op protocols and adds minimal unique signal.

Glucose being checked is largely protocol-driven and a proxy for ICU intensity/diabetes management. It is unlikely to contribute uniquely to predicting Hour-60 creatinine ratio in a sparse linear model.
Glucose measurement at 25–36h remains a clinician-behavior proxy with weak mechanistic linkage to AKI. Even with better temporal proximity, it is not competitive with direct renal function features.

CK measurement indicates concern for muscle injury/rhabdomyolysis but is mostly a testing-intensity proxy here. Early post-op ordering is nonspecific and provides little unique renal predictive value without CK values.

CK being measured may capture suspicion for rhabdomyolysis/ischemia, which can contribute to AKI, but it is still indirect and behavior-driven. Modest score because it could add limited signal in select cases yet is usually dominated by direct renal markers.

Later CK measurement may reflect evolving muscle injury or ongoing concern that could relate to AKI risk. Still primarily a proxy (no magnitude), so only a weak-to-moderate sparse-selection candidate.

Mean eGFR in the first 12h reflects early postoperative renal function derived from creatinine. It is relevant but can be noisy immediately post-op and is somewhat redundant with later eGFR summaries closer to Hour 60.

Mean eGFR at 13–24h captures early trajectory of renal function after initial post-op noise settles. Strong direct renal signal and likely to add predictive value for subsequent creatinine_ratio, though still somewhat dominated by the closest window.

Mean eGFR at 25–36h is the closest direct renal function summary to the Hour-60 target and reflects evolving AKI. High priority for sparse selection because it is a near-direct precursor of the outcome.

Maximum eGFR mainly reflects the best (often dilutional) creatinine value early after surgery rather than renal insult. It is less informative for AKI prediction than mean/low-function summaries and is redundant with later eGFR features.

Peak eGFR in 13–24h tends to capture transient improvement/hemodilution rather than kidney injury. It is a weaker, less pathophysiologic aggregation for predicting creatinine rise and is dominated by mean eGFR.

Maximum eGFR at 25–36h reflects the best renal function in that window and is less aligned with risk of creatinine increase than sustained/low-function measures. Likely redundant and lower-yield for sparse modeling than 25–36h eGFR_mean.
Lowest eGFR in the immediate post-op window reflects early renal dysfunction/low clearance. Strong renal signal but somewhat noisier early after bypass and less proximal than later epochs.
Minimum eGFR at 13–24h captures early trajectory toward AKI beyond immediate post-op noise. Likely predictive, though still dominated by the closest (25–36h) renal signal.

Minimum eGFR in the 25–36h window is a direct, proximal marker of evolving kidney dysfunction before the Hour-60 creatinine. High unique value and the best-timed/most informative aggregation in this batch.
Count/frequency of eGFR measurements is mainly a monitoring-intensity/clinician concern proxy rather than physiology. Adds little beyond actual eGFR values and is early-window biased.
Measurement frequency reflects care intensity and suspicion for complications more than renal function itself. Likely redundant once eGFR_min is included.
Later measurement frequency may weakly proxy evolving instability/AKI workup closer to Hour 60. Still largely a clinician-behavior signal and dominated by eGFR_min.

LDH measurement frequency is a nonspecific monitoring proxy (hemolysis/ischemia workup) rather than a direct renal marker. Early post-op ordering patterns are noisy and unlikely to survive sparse selection.
Represents how often LDH was checked, not the LDH level. Weak, indirect signal and likely redundant with stronger physiologic predictors.

Later LDH checking may indicate concern for hemolysis/ischemia or complicated course, which can correlate with AKI risk. Still a proxy variable and typically lower priority than direct renal/hemodynamic features.

Average INR early post-op reflects coagulopathy/hepatic dysfunction/warfarin effect and overall illness severity. Mean smooths extremes and early timing makes it a weaker, indirect predictor of later creatinine rise.

Sustained INR elevation at 25–36h can reflect ongoing hepatic dysfunction, congestion, or critical illness associated with AKI risk. Useful but indirect and generally dominated by more kidney-specific variables.
Peak INR in the first 12h captures extreme coagulopathy/bleeding risk but is often transient after surgery/CPB. Indirect for renal outcome and less reliable than later-window extremes.

Maximum INR at 25–36h captures significant ongoing coagulopathy/organ dysfunction closer to the prediction horizon. Among INR features, this extreme is more informative than mean/min, but remains a nonspecific severity marker.
Minimum INR is rarely the clinically meaningful direction for post-op risk and may reflect normal values. Low unique predictive value and dominated by INR_max/mean.
Minimum INR in this window is unlikely to add information beyond whether INR ever became elevated. Redundant and not a strong mechanistic link to AKI.
INR measurement frequency is primarily a monitoring/bleeding-management proxy. Adds little once INR values are available and is early-window noisy.

Count of INR checks in hours 13–24, mainly reflecting monitoring intensity/bleeding or anticoagulation management. Weak, indirect proxy for AKI risk and likely redundant with more direct hemodynamic/renal signals.

Count of INR checks in hours 25–36, a clinician-behavior/acuity marker closer to the outcome window. Still not a physiologic renal marker and unlikely to survive sparse selection unless few other severity features exist.
Average eosinophil percentage in the immediate post-op window, often influenced by stress response and dilution. Low direct linkage to AKI and early timing makes it noisy and low priority.
Average eosinophil percentage in hours 13–24. Eosinophils are not a strong or specific predictor of post-cardiac surgery AKI, and the mean is unlikely to add unique value.
Peak eosinophil percentage in hours 0–12. Even as an extreme, it is nonspecific and early post-op variability dominates, so it is unlikely to help a sparse model.

Peak eosinophil percentage in hours 13–24, which could weakly reflect drug reaction/inflammation. Still a nonspecific signal for AKI and would be dominated by direct renal perfusion/renal function features.
Nadir eosinophil percentage in hours 0–12, largely reflecting stress leukogram and hemodilution. Minimal mechanistic connection to creatinine rise and low sparse-selection value.
Nadir eosinophil percentage in hours 13–24. Low specificity and limited incremental information versus other inflammatory/acuity markers, so low priority.
Whether/how often eosinophils were measured in hours 0–12, primarily a monitoring proxy (CBC ordering). Not a physiologic renal signal and unlikely to add unique predictive value.
Measurement frequency of eosinophils in hours 13–24, reflecting lab-ordering patterns rather than kidney physiology. Very weak candidate for sparse inclusion.
Measurement frequency of eosinophils in hours 25–36, a late monitoring-intensity proxy. Even with better timing, it remains indirect and low yield for predicting creatinine ratio.
Whether LDL was checked in hours 0–12, which is typically unrelated to acute ICU kidney trajectory and reflects non-urgent ordering. No direct renal signal and very low sparse-model priority.
Whether LDL was checked in hours 13–24, a clinician-ordering artifact rather than AKI physiology. Unlikely to contribute unique predictive information.
Whether LDL was checked in hours 25–36, still largely irrelevant to acute postoperative renal dysfunction. Low value and likely noise in sparse selection.

Mean MCV in hours 0–12, reflecting baseline hematologic status and perioperative dilution/transfusion effects. At best an indirect frailty/nutrition/transfusion proxy and early timing limits usefulness.

Mean MCV in hours 13–24, a relatively stable hematologic marker that may weakly proxy transfusion burden or chronic disease. Indirect for AKI and unlikely to beat direct perfusion/renal markers in a sparse model.

Mean corpuscular volume (MCV) average in the 25–36h window reflects RBC size/anemia/nutrition rather than renal physiology. Likely weak and indirect for AKI prediction and largely redundant with other nonspecific hematology signals.
Peak MCV in the immediate 0–12h post-op period is unlikely to change meaningfully or track renal hypoperfusion/AKI. Early window plus low mechanistic link makes it a poor sparse-model candidate.
Peak MCV at 13–24h is still a nonspecific hematologic index with minimal direct relationship to creatinine rise. Adds little unique predictive value versus other physiologic/renal features.

Peak MCV at 25–36h is the closest window but remains a weak, indirect marker (anemia/transfusion effects) rather than kidney injury. Could capture hemodilution/transfusion context but unlikely to survive sparse selection.
Minimum MCV in 0–12h is dominated by perioperative dilution/transfusion noise and is not a direct renal signal. Very low priority for a sparse model.
Minimum MCV at 13–24h remains a nonspecific hematology metric with weak linkage to evolving AKI. Redundant/low-yield compared with hemodynamics, urine output, and renal labs (if available elsewhere).

Minimum MCV at 25–36h may reflect ongoing hemodilution or transfusion burden, which can correlate with illness severity but not specifically renal dysfunction. Some timing advantage but still indirect and likely redundant.
Count of MCV measurements in 0–12h is primarily a monitoring-intensity/ordering proxy rather than physiology. Early post-op labs are routine, so this adds little unique information.
MCV measurement frequency at 13–24h reflects clinician behavior and lab cadence more than kidney injury. Low priority and likely noisy/redundant with overall lab frequency signals.
MCV measurement frequency at 25–36h could weakly proxy ongoing instability/complications prompting repeat CBCs. Still a behavior proxy and not kidney-specific, so only modest value.

Mean platelet volume (MPV) average in 0–12h can reflect platelet activation/inflammation but is nonspecific for renal hypoperfusion. Early post-op variability and indirectness make it a weak sparse-model feature.
Average MPV at 13–24h may correlate with systemic inflammation/severity, which can relate to AKI risk but indirectly. Likely replaceable by stronger hemodynamic/renal markers.

Average MPV at 25–36h is closer to the Hour-60 target and may capture sustained inflammatory/consumptive physiology associated with AKI risk. Still indirect, but among this batch it has comparatively better timing and potential signal.

Peak MPV in 0–12h may capture acute platelet activation but is heavily influenced by immediate post-bypass/post-op noise. Indirect for creatinine rise and unlikely to be selected when feature budget is tight.
Peak MPV at 13–24h is a nonspecific severity/inflammation marker with limited kidney specificity. Provides little unique value relative to later-window MPV and stronger renal-perfusion variables.

Peak MPV at 25–36h preserves an extreme (potentially more informative than mean) and is temporally closest to the outcome window. Still indirect for AKI, but within this batch it is one of the more plausible keepers.

Minimum mean platelet volume (MPV) in the immediate post-op window; MPV is a nonspecific inflammation/platelet activation marker. Weak and indirect for AKI prediction and likely dominated by hemodynamics/lactate/renal labs, so low sparse-selection priority.
MPV minimum during 13–24h reflects nonspecific platelet/inflammatory changes. Still far from a direct renal signal and unlikely to add unique value in a sparse model.

MPV minimum closer to the outcome window may weakly track ongoing systemic stress/inflammation. However it remains indirect and typically redundant with stronger perfusion/illness markers, so only a modest score.
Count/frequency of MPV measurements in 0–12h is primarily a monitoring-intensity/clinician-behavior proxy. Not a physiologic kidney injury marker and unlikely to survive sparse selection.
MPV measurement frequency in 13–24h reflects lab ordering/acuity rather than renal physiology. Generally low unique predictive value for creatinine ratio.

MPV measurement frequency in 25–36h may proxy evolving complications prompting closer monitoring. Still a proxy feature and typically dominated by actual physiologic variables, so low-to-modest priority.
Average lactate in 0–12h reflects global hypoperfusion/low cardiac output after surgery, a risk factor for AKI. Mean smooths transient peaks and early post-op noise is common, so moderate priority.

Mean lactate in 13–24h captures sustained tissue hypoperfusion after initial post-op stabilization, which is strongly linked to subsequent AKI. More temporally informative than 0–12h and less noisy, making it a strong sparse-model candidate.

Peak lactate in 0–12h captures the worst early hypoperfusion/oxygen debt, which can precipitate renal injury. Despite early-window noise, the extreme value preserves insult signal better than the mean, so high priority within lactate features.

Peak lactate in 13–24h indicates ongoing or recurrent hypoperfusion after the immediate post-op period and is a strong precursor of evolving AKI. As an extreme and in the more informative epoch, it is the top lactate feature for sparse selection.
Minimum lactate in 0–12h mainly reflects periods of adequate perfusion and is less informative than peaks for AKI risk. Likely redundant and low yield for sparse selection.
Minimum lactate in 13–24h is not a strong marker of renal hypoperfusion compared with mean/max lactate. Adds little unique predictive value and is dominated by lactate_max/mean.
Number of lactate measurements in 0–12h is largely a proxy for clinician concern/instability rather than a direct biomarker. May add slight signal about acuity but generally low priority.
Lactate measurement frequency in 13–24h reflects ongoing monitoring for shock/low output. Still a proxy and usually inferior to actual lactate values, so modest importance.

Lactate measurement frequency in 25–36h may indicate persistent concern closer to the outcome time. However it remains a monitoring-intensity proxy and is typically dominated by lactate level features.

Mean alkaline phosphatase in 0–12h reflects hepatobiliary/bone signal and is not a direct renal injury marker. Early post-op values are unlikely to add unique predictive value for creatinine ratio in a sparse model.

Peak alkaline phosphatase early post-op reflects hepatobiliary/bone signal rather than renal perfusion or AKI. In a sparse AKI model it is low-yield and likely noise/redundant with broader illness markers.

Minimum alkaline phosphatase in the first 12h is not mechanistically linked to evolving AKI and is unlikely to add unique predictive value. Dominated by more direct renal/hemodynamic features (not present in this batch).
Count of ALP measurements is primarily a monitoring/clinician-behavior proxy rather than physiology. Early (0–12h) measurement frequency is especially nonspecific for Hour-60 creatinine trajectory.
ALP measurement frequency in 13–24h mainly reflects care intensity or workup for liver issues, not kidney injury. Unlikely to survive sparse selection for predicting creatinine_ratio.
Later ALP measurement frequency could weakly proxy ongoing complications/complexity closer to the outcome window. Still indirect and nonspecific for AKI, so low priority in a sparse model.

Mean RDW reflects baseline anisocytosis/inflammation and chronic illness burden, not an acute renal insult. Early post-op values add limited unique signal and are largely redundant with later RDW summaries.
RDW mean at 13–24h is a nonspecific severity/frailty marker with weak direct linkage to evolving AKI. Likely dominated by the closest (25–36h) RDW features if any are kept.

RDW mean at 25–36h is the closest sustained hematologic/inflammatory signal to Hour 60 and may correlate with overall physiologic stress affecting kidneys. Still indirect, but the best-timed RDW summary and a plausible sparse-model candidate.

Max RDW in 0–12h is unlikely to represent a true acute spike (RDW changes slowly) and may reflect lab variability/transfusion effects. Early timing and redundancy with later RDW reduce sparse-selection value.
Max RDW at 13–24h remains a nonspecific marker and RDW extremes are not clearly more informative than mean given slow kinetics. Likely redundant with 25–36h RDW if selecting sparsely.

Max RDW at 25–36h is the closest available RDW signal and may capture transfusion/hemolysis/inflammation burden associated with worse outcomes including AKI. Still indirect, but could add modest unique value versus the mean.
Minimum RDW in the first 12h is not physiologically meaningful for AKI prediction and is likely dominated by other RDW summaries. Very low priority for sparse selection.
RDW minimum at 13–24h provides little interpretable renal-relevant signal and is largely redundant with mean/max RDW. Unlikely to survive feature screening.
RDW minimum at 25–36h is not a plausible marker of renal hypoperfusion or evolving AKI and adds little beyond mean/max. Lowest-yield RDW aggregation for sparse modeling.
RDW measurement count is a monitoring-frequency proxy (CBC ordering) rather than a biomarker. Early post-op ordering is routine and not specific for predicting creatinine_ratio.
CBC/RDW measurement frequency at 13–24h mainly reflects clinician practice and overall complexity, not kidney physiology. Low incremental value for a sparse linear AKI model.
Counts whether RDW was checked in 25–36h, reflecting monitoring intensity rather than renal physiology. As a clinician-behavior proxy it is unlikely to add unique value in a sparse AKI model.
Average chloride in the immediate 0–12h window, influenced by bypass/fluids and early shifts. Likely noisy and dominated by later chloride extremes/levels closer to Hour 60.
Mean chloride 13–24h reflects ongoing fluid/acid-base management but is an indirect AKI signal. Redundant with 25–36h chloride summaries and less informative than extremes.
Mean chloride 25–36h captures sustained hyperchloremia from resuscitation which can correlate with AKI risk. Closest window to outcome, but still an indirect marker and often weaker than max/min.
Peak chloride 0–12h may reflect large chloride load early post-op. Early peaks are common and transient, so this is a weaker, more noise-prone predictor than later peaks.
Peak chloride 13–24h better reflects persistent hyperchloremia and ongoing chloride-rich fluids. More likely to carry AKI-relevant signal than means, but still indirect and less proximal than 25–36h.

Peak chloride 25–36h is the most proximal extreme and best captures sustained/ongoing hyperchloremia and acid-base stress from resuscitation. Among chloride features, this is the strongest sparse-model candidate.
Lowest chloride 0–12h is often driven by dilutional effects and peri-bypass shifts. It is early, less mechanistically tied to AKI than hyperchloremia, and dominated by later windows.
Minimum chloride 13–24h is an indirect and typically less informative extreme for AKI than maximum chloride. Likely redundant and low yield for sparse selection.

Minimum chloride 25–36h may reflect dilution/over-resuscitation but is not a strong direct renal injury signal. Some proximity helps, yet it is generally dominated by chloride_max/mean for AKI prediction.
Number of chloride measurements 0–12h mainly reflects monitoring intensity and early instability. As a proxy variable it is unlikely to survive sparse selection.

Measurement frequency 13–24h is a clinician-behavior/acuity proxy rather than a physiologic renal marker. Adds little unique predictive value beyond actual chloride values and other severity features.
More frequent chloride checks 25–36h may indicate ongoing metabolic/renal concern and ICU complexity. Still a proxy and generally secondary to the chloride level extremes themselves.
Whether HDL was measured 0–12h reflects ordering patterns (nutrition/lipid panel) rather than kidney physiology. Very unlikely to add unique predictive signal for Hour 60 creatinine ratio.
HDL measurement in 13–24h is a care-pattern proxy with minimal mechanistic link to evolving AKI. Low priority for sparse linear selection.
HDL measurement in 25–36h remains a clinician-behavior indicator and not a direct renal or perfusion marker. Even with better timing, it is not expected to contribute unique predictive value.

Average neutrophil percentage early post-op reflects inflammation/stress response. It is an indirect, nonspecific illness-severity signal and likely redundant with later values or other inflammatory markers, so low sparse-model priority.
