Mean neutrophil percentage in 13–24h captures ongoing inflammatory response. Still indirect for AKI and typically dominated by more renal-proximal hemodynamic/renal markers, so unlikely to survive sparse selection.

Peak neutrophil percentage in the immediate 0–12h window reflects acute stress/inflammation. Extremes can matter, but this is early and nonspecific for renal dysfunction, so only weak incremental value.

Peak neutrophil percentage at 13–24h may better reflect sustained inflammatory activation (e.g., infection/SIRS) that can contribute to AKI risk. Among neutrophil summaries, the max is the most plausible keeper, but it remains indirect.

Minimum neutrophil percentage early post-op is rarely a meaningful renal-risk signal and may reflect dilutional effects or lab variability. It is dominated by other aggregations and has minimal sparse-model value.
Minimum neutrophil percentage in 13–24h is not a typical driver or marker of evolving AKI. Likely noise/redundant and not competitive for a sparse feature budget.
Count/frequency of neutrophil measurements is mainly a monitoring-intensity/clinician-behavior proxy. It does not directly encode renal physiology and is low priority for sparse selection.
Measurement frequency of neutrophil percentage in 13–24h reflects care intensity rather than kidney injury. Generally redundant with overall lab-draw patterns and weak for prediction.
Later (25–36h) measurement frequency may proxy evolving concern for complications closer to the outcome time. Still a behavior proxy rather than physiology, so only modest value at best.

Mean albumin early post-op reflects baseline nutrition/chronic illness and acute dilution/capillary leak, which correlate with AKI risk. Useful but early and partly confounded by fluid shifts, so moderate priority.
Maximum albumin in 0–12h is less informative than low values for capillary leak/dilution and is sensitive to timing of draws/fluids. Likely redundant with mean/min and lower sparse-model value.

Minimum albumin in 0–12h captures the worst dilution/capillary leak/inflammatory state, which is associated with postoperative AKI and renal hypoperfusion risk. Within the albumin family, the min best preserves the pathologic signal and is the strongest candidate.
Albumin measurement frequency is primarily a clinician-ordering/monitoring proxy (e.g., nutrition/oncotic management) rather than a direct renal signal. Low likelihood of surviving sparse selection.
Frequency of albumin checks in 13–24h reflects practice patterns and perceived acuity more than kidney physiology. Typically redundant with other monitoring proxies and low priority.
Later albumin measurement frequency may indicate ongoing instability or fluid/oncotic management closer to Hour 60. Still largely a behavior proxy, so only modest incremental value.

Whether triglycerides were measured early post-op is a weak proxy for nutrition/propofol monitoring rather than renal injury. Very low direct relevance and unlikely to add unique predictive value in a sparse model.

Whether triglycerides were checked in 13–24h is mainly a monitoring/TPN/pancreatitis workup proxy rather than renal physiology. Low unique value for AKI prediction and likely dominated by hemodynamics/renal labs.

Measurement of triglycerides in 25–36h reflects clinician behavior/complexity (e.g., nutrition or propofol monitoring) more than kidney injury. Even though closer to Hour 60, it is still a weak, indirect proxy for creatinine rise.

Average hemoglobin early post-op reflects anemia/hemodilution and bleeding/transfusion context, which can relate indirectly to renal hypoperfusion. In 0–12h it is noisy and redundant with later hemoglobin summaries, so low sparse-model priority.

Mean hemoglobin in 13–24h captures sustained anemia/hemodilution that may contribute to renal stress. Still an indirect severity marker and likely less informative than the corresponding minimum and later (25–36h) window.

Mean hemoglobin in 25–36h is closer to the outcome window and may reflect ongoing anemia/hemodilution affecting renal oxygen delivery. Moderately useful but still indirect and often dominated by the hemoglobin minimum for capturing worst exposure.
Peak hemoglobin in 0–12h is usually driven by transfusion/hemoconcentration and is not a direct AKI signal. Early timing and weak mechanistic link make it unlikely to survive sparse selection.
Maximum hemoglobin in 13–24h mostly reflects transfusion/volume status rather than renal injury risk. Provides little incremental information beyond minima/means and other perfusion variables.
Maximum hemoglobin in 25–36h is a poor marker for renal hypoperfusion compared with low hemoglobin or hemodynamics. Likely redundant/noisy and low priority for a sparse model.
Lowest hemoglobin in 0–12h captures nadir anemia from bleeding/hemodilution, which can contribute to renal ischemic risk. However early post-op variability is high, so it’s useful but not top-tier.

Hemoglobin nadir in 13–24h reflects ongoing anemia after initial stabilization and can signal continued bleeding/transfusion needs linked to AKI risk. Moderately predictive but still indirect and somewhat redundant with the later nadir.

Hemoglobin nadir in 25–36h is the best hemoglobin aggregation here for capturing clinically important anemia closest to Hour 60. Stronger sparse-model candidate than mean/max because extremes better reflect renal oxygen delivery stress.
Frequency of hemoglobin checks in 0–12h is primarily a monitoring intensity/bleeding concern proxy. It is not a physiologic biomarker and is likely confounded by care patterns.
Number of hemoglobin measurements in 13–24h reflects clinician concern and ICU workflow more than kidney function. Low unique predictive value relative to actual hemoglobin levels.

Hemoglobin measurement frequency in 25–36h may weakly proxy ongoing instability/bleeding management closer to the outcome window. Still largely a behavior/acuity surrogate and inferior to hemoglobin nadir itself.

Whether TEG fibrinogen activity was measured early suggests bleeding/coagulopathy evaluation rather than renal physiology. Mostly a clinician-behavior proxy and early post-op noise limits value for Hour 60 creatinine ratio.

TEG fibrinogen activity measurement in 13–24h indicates ongoing coagulation/bleeding assessment and care complexity. Indirect and unlikely to add unique predictive signal for AKI beyond hemodynamics and renal labs.

Indicates whether TEG fibrinogen activity was checked (monitoring/bleeding-workup intensity) rather than a renal physiology signal. Likely a clinician-behavior proxy with weak, indirect linkage to AKI, so low sparse-model priority.

Average ACT in the immediate post-op window reflects anticoagulation/heparin reversal management. It is early, non-renal, and the mean is unlikely to add unique predictive value for Hour-60 creatinine beyond overall illness/severity proxies.

Peak ACT may capture excessive anticoagulation or delayed reversal, which can correlate with bleeding/transfusion and downstream renal risk. Still indirect and early (0–12h), so only a weak-to-modest candidate.
Minimum ACT mainly reflects periods of lower anticoagulation and is not a direct kidney injury or perfusion marker. Redundant within the ACT family and unlikely to survive sparse selection.
Number/frequency of ACT measurements is primarily a monitoring intensity/procedural management proxy. It is not a physiologic biomarker and is likely confounded by workflow, so low priority.

ACT measurement frequency in 13–24h reflects ongoing monitoring/concern rather than renal function. Adds little unique signal for AKI prediction compared with hemodynamics/renal labs, so low priority.

Late ACT measurement frequency (25–36h) may indicate ongoing bleeding/anticoagulation issues closer to the outcome window. Still a clinician-behavior proxy and indirect for AKI, but timing makes it slightly more informative than earlier epochs.

Mean AST reflects early hepatic/ischemic injury burden and systemic hypoperfusion, which can co-occur with AKI. However it is early and the mean smooths peaks, making it a weaker sparse-model candidate than an extreme.

Peak AST in 0–12h can capture ischemic hepatitis/low-flow states after surgery, a plausible precursor of renal hypoperfusion and AKI. Among AST aggregations this extreme is most informative, but timing is still early so not top-tier.
Minimum AST is generally uninformative (low values rarely indicate injury) and adds little beyond other AST summaries. Highly redundant and unlikely to help a sparse model.
AST measurement frequency is mainly a proxy for clinician concern/monitoring rather than organ injury magnitude. Weak, indirect, and confounded, so low sparse-selection priority.
Frequency of AST checks in 13–24h reflects monitoring intensity and care patterns more than physiology. Unlikely to add unique predictive value for creatinine_ratio.
Late AST measurement frequency may indicate evolving organ dysfunction closer to Hour 60. Still a monitoring proxy rather than a biomarker, so only modest value.

Mean total protein reflects hemodilution/nutrition/inflammation and can correlate with fluid balance, which relates indirectly to renal outcomes. It is nonspecific and early, so a weak-to-moderate sparse-model candidate.
Maximum total protein is rarely a meaningful renal-risk signal post-op and is influenced by lab/volume status noise. Likely redundant with mean/min and low priority.

Minimum total protein may better capture hemodilution/capillary leak and aggressive fluid resuscitation, which can track with AKI risk. Within this family the extreme (min) is most plausible, though still indirect and early.
Count/frequency of total protein checks in the first 12h is mainly a monitoring-intensity proxy. It has little direct renal signal and is unlikely to survive sparse selection.
Measurement frequency of total protein reflects clinician behavior/acuity rather than kidney physiology. Redundant as a proxy and weakly linked to Hour-60 creatinine ratio.
Later measurement frequency may weakly indicate evolving complications prompting labs, but it remains a proxy rather than a biomarker. Slightly higher only due to closer timing to the outcome.
Whether HbA1c was measured post-op is a workflow/comorbidity-documentation proxy, not an acute renal insult marker. Early timing and binary nature make it low-value for sparse prediction.
HbA1c measurement indicates diabetes evaluation rather than perioperative kidney trajectory. Adds little unique information beyond known comorbidities and is unlikely to be selected.
Late HbA1c measurement still reflects clinician behavior and baseline risk assessment, not evolving AKI physiology. Minimal incremental value for predicting Hour-60 creatinine ratio.

Mean serum bicarbonate/total CO2 reflects acid-base status but is often confounded early post-op by ventilation and fluid shifts. The mean smooths extremes and is likely dominated by later/min values.
Average CO2 in 13–24h may capture developing metabolic acidosis from hypoperfusion/AKI but remains indirect. Likely redundant with CO2 minima and later-window summaries.
Sustained lower bicarbonate closer to Hour 60 can reflect ongoing renal dysfunction or systemic hypoperfusion. Still indirect and typically less informative than the corresponding CO2 minimum.

High CO2 (metabolic alkalosis/compensation) is not a typical direct precursor of AKI and is noisy early post-op. Likely provides little unique predictive value versus minima or other severity markers.
CO2 maxima are generally less tied to renal hypoperfusion than low CO2 values. Redundant/low-yield for sparse selection.
Later CO2 maxima still weakly related to AKI compared with acidosis signals. Unlikely to add unique value in a sparse linear model.
Low CO2 captures metabolic acidosis, which can reflect hypoperfusion and early kidney injury, but the immediate post-op window is noisy. Useful but not top-tier given timing and confounding.
A low CO2 minimum in 13–24h is a stronger signal of evolving metabolic acidosis from shock/AKI than means or maxima. Likely to survive sparse screening as an extreme-value insult marker.

Low CO2 minimum in 25–36h is the closest and most pathophysiologically aligned extreme marker for ongoing acidosis from renal dysfunction or persistent hypoperfusion. High unique predictive value relative to other CO2 aggregations in this batch.

Frequency of CO2 measurements reflects monitoring intensity/instability rather than a direct biomarker. Slightly informative as an acuity proxy but dominated by actual CO2 values (especially minima) for sparse selection.
Counts how often CO2 was checked (monitoring intensity) in 13–24h. This is mainly a clinician-behavior/acuity proxy and is dominated by physiologic variables, so low sparse-model priority.

Frequency of CO2 measurement in the closest window (25–36h) may weakly reflect evolving instability/ventilation issues. Still largely a monitoring proxy rather than a renal signal, so only modest value.

Whether BNP was measured early post-op (0–12h), reflecting clinician concern for heart failure/volume status. Mostly a testing/acuity proxy and early timing is noisy for Hour-60 creatinine, so low priority.
BNP measurement in 13–24h suggests concern for cardiac dysfunction/volume overload that can contribute to renal hypoperfusion. Still a proxy for suspicion rather than BNP level, so weak candidate.

BNP measurement in 25–36h is temporally closer and may indicate persistent hemodynamic/volume issues relevant to AKI risk. However it remains a clinician-ordering proxy without magnitude, so limited unique predictive value.

Average arterial/venous PCO2 in 0–12h reflects ventilation/perfusion immediately after surgery. Early means are often dominated by transient post-op management and are indirect for kidney injury, so low priority.

Mean PCO2 in 13–24h captures sustained ventilatory status and may correlate with severity of illness. It is indirect for renal dysfunction and likely redundant with more informative extremes, so low-moderate priority.

Mean PCO2 in 25–36h is closest to the outcome window and may reflect ongoing respiratory failure/acidemia affecting renal perfusion. Still indirect and potentially redundant with max/min, but could survive sparse screening as a general physiologic severity marker.
Peak PCO2 in 0–12h captures episodes of hypoventilation/hypercapnia early after surgery. Extremes are better than means, but early timing is noisy and renal linkage is indirect, so modest value.

Peak PCO2 in 13–24h reflects significant hypercapnic episodes that may indicate respiratory failure and systemic stress. As an extreme it is more informative than the mean and may add unique severity signal, though still indirect for AKI.

Peak PCO2 in 25–36h captures late hypercapnic insults closest to Hour 60, which can track with ongoing critical illness and acid-base stress relevant to renal dysfunction. Among PCO2 summaries, this extreme in the latest window is the strongest sparse-model candidate.
Lowest PCO2 in 0–12h reflects hyperventilation or ventilator settings immediately post-op. Early minima are often management-driven and weakly related to kidney outcomes, so low priority.

Lowest PCO2 in 13–24h may indicate aggressive ventilation or metabolic compensation. It is less plausibly linked to renal hypoperfusion than hypercapnia and is likely redundant with other respiratory markers, so weak value.
Lowest PCO2 in 25–36h is closer in time but still often reflects ventilator strategy rather than pathology driving AKI. Provides limited unique predictive value compared with late PCO2 max/mean.
Number of PCO2 measurements in 0–12h is primarily a monitoring/acuity proxy in the immediate post-op period. High redundancy with other measurement-frequency features and little direct renal signal.
Frequency of PCO2 checks in 13–24h reflects clinician monitoring intensity rather than physiology. Generally dominated by actual PCO2 values/extremes for sparse selection.
Count/frequency of PaCO2 measurements in 25–36h is mainly a monitoring-intensity/ventilation-management proxy. It is indirect for AKI risk and unlikely to add unique value in a sparse model.
Whether indirect bilirubin was checked in 0–12h reflects clinician behavior/monitoring rather than renal physiology. Early post-op ordering patterns are noisy and weak for Hour-60 creatinine ratio.

Indirect bilirubin measurement in 13–24h is a proxy for concern about hemolysis/hepatic dysfunction rather than a direct renal signal. Adds little unique predictive value versus true hemodynamic/renal markers.

Indirect bilirubin measurement in 25–36h may indicate evolving hemolysis/hepatic congestion that can co-travel with AKI risk. Still largely a clinician-intensity proxy and weaker than actual bilirubin values or renal/hemodynamic features.

Mean calcium in 0–12h is influenced by transfusion/citrate, albumin shifts, and supplementation immediately post-bypass. It is an indirect and early, smoothed signal that is unlikely to survive sparse selection.
Mean calcium in 13–24h may reflect ongoing critical illness and replacement needs, but remains indirect for kidney injury. The mean is also redundant with more informative extremes and later windows.

Mean calcium in 25–36h is closer to the outcome window and may capture sustained derangements, but calcium is still not a direct AKI precursor. Likely redundant with calcium extremes and other severity/hemodynamic variables.
Peak calcium in 0–12h is often driven by supplementation and peri-bypass management rather than renal pathophysiology. Early timing and treatment confounding make it a low-priority sparse feature.

Peak calcium in 13–24h can reflect aggressive replacement or metabolic shifts, but is not a strong mechanistic predictor of AKI. Some signal may exist, yet it is likely dominated by later calcium minima or direct renal/hemodynamic markers.

Peak calcium in 25–36h is temporally closer but still heavily influenced by clinician supplementation and not directly tied to creatinine rise. Provides limited unique value compared with calcium minima or true renal perfusion indicators.

Minimum calcium in 0–12h may capture severe hypocalcemia from citrate load/transfusion and physiologic stress, which can correlate with illness severity. However, early post-op noise and treatment effects reduce sparse-model priority.

Minimum calcium in 13–24h can reflect persistent hypocalcemia and higher acuity (transfusion burden, inflammation), which may track with AKI risk. Among calcium summaries, the minimum is more insult-sensitive and less smoothed than the mean.

Minimum calcium in 25–36h captures late/persistent hypocalcemia, which can be a strong marker of ongoing critical illness and transfusion/citrate burden near the prediction horizon. Within this batch, it is the most informative calcium aggregation and timing for sparse selection.
Frequency of calcium checks in 0–12h is largely routine post-cardiac surgery monitoring and clinician behavior. It is not a direct physiologic predictor and is dominated by actual calcium values.
Calcium measurement frequency in 13–24h mainly reflects monitoring intensity and care complexity. It adds little unique predictive information beyond calcium minima/maxima/means.
More frequent calcium checks in 25–36h may indicate ongoing instability or replacement needs closer to Hour 60. Still a proxy variable and generally weaker than the calcium minimum itself.

Mean PTT in the immediate post-op window reflects anticoagulation/heparin effect and bleeding risk more than renal physiology. Early averages are noisy and largely redundant with later/extreme PTT summaries for sparse AKI prediction.

Mean PTT at 25–36h may reflect ongoing heparin/anticoagulation or hepatic dysfunction rather than direct kidney injury. In a sparse model it is usually dominated by more informative extremes (e.g., max) and by direct renal/hemodynamic features not in this batch.

Peak PTT early post-op captures excessive anticoagulation or coagulopathy, which is an indirect severity/management signal. It is temporally distant and less specific for Hour-60 creatinine trajectory than later peaks, so low sparse-selection priority.

Peak PTT at 25–36h can indicate ongoing coagulopathy/anticoagulation and overall physiologic derangement closer to the outcome window. Among PTT variants, this extreme and timing best preserves signal, though it remains indirect for AKI.

Minimum PTT early post-op is rarely a meaningful renal-risk signal and may reflect lab/measurement variability or brief normalization. It is dominated by later and/or maximal PTT measures for capturing clinically relevant derangements.

Minimum PTT at 25–36h mainly reflects periods of normal coagulation rather than pathologic insult. It adds little unique predictive value versus max/mean PTT and is unlikely to survive sparse selection.

Count/frequency of PTT measurements in 0–12h is primarily a monitoring/clinician-behavior proxy (e.g., heparin titration, bleeding concern). Such intensity signals are weak and non-specific for predicting creatinine ratio.
PTT measurement frequency at 13–24h reflects care intensity and anticoagulation management rather than kidney function. It is a proxy feature with limited unique value for sparse AKI prediction.

More frequent PTT checks at 25–36h may indicate ongoing instability (e.g., anticoagulation titration, bleeding/ECMO concern) closer to the outcome window. Still largely a clinician-behavior proxy, so only modest priority.

Mean monocyte count early post-op reflects inflammatory response/stress but is non-specific and noisy immediately after surgery. It is unlikely to add unique predictive value for Hour-60 creatinine ratio in a sparse model.

Mean monocyte count at 13–24h may capture evolving inflammation/infection risk, which can correlate with AKI indirectly. However it is a weak, non-specific signal and typically dominated by more direct renal/hemodynamic predictors.

Peak monocyte count in 0–12h is largely driven by perioperative inflammatory shifts and lab variability. It is temporally distant and non-specific for subsequent creatinine rise, so low sparse-selection priority.

Peak monocyte count at 13–24h could reflect heightened inflammatory activation, which may associate with AKI risk. Still indirect and likely redundant with other inflammatory markers (not shown), so only a weak-to-moderate candidate.
Minimum monocyte count early post-op is not a clear pathophysiologic driver of AKI and may reflect dilutional effects or measurement noise. It provides little unique predictive information.
Minimum monocyte count at 13–24h is generally not informative for renal hypoperfusion or evolving AKI. It is unlikely to survive sparse feature selection given weak specificity.
Frequency of monocyte measurements is a monitoring proxy (CBC ordering patterns) rather than a physiologic biomarker. It is non-specific and low value for sparse prediction of creatinine ratio.

Count of monocyte labs drawn (monitoring intensity) rather than the monocyte value. Mostly a clinician-behavior proxy and unlikely to add unique signal for AKI beyond actual renal/hemodynamic markers.

Frequency of monocyte measurement in the closest window; reflects surveillance/complexity more than physiology. Low priority for sparse selection compared with direct kidney perfusion/injury indicators.

Average phosphorus early post-op; can be influenced by shifts, refeeding, and supplementation and is noisy immediately after surgery. Likely redundant with later phosphorus extremes that better track evolving renal dysfunction.
Mean phosphorus in the 13–24h window; mild indirect marker of renal handling/catabolism. Lower priority because mean smooths peaks and is dominated by later (25–36h) and/or max values for AKI signal.

Mean phosphorus closest to the target; sustained hyperphosphatemia can reflect reduced renal clearance and correlate with evolving AKI. Still somewhat indirect and potentially redundant with phosphorus_max in the same window.

Peak phosphorus in the immediate post-op period; extremes can capture early renal clearance impairment or tissue breakdown. Scored below later peaks because 0–12h is noisier and less temporally proximal to Hour 60.

Peak phosphorus at 13–24h; an extreme is more informative than the mean for detecting evolving impaired clearance. Moderate priority but still dominated by the closer 25–36h peak for sparse selection.

Peak phosphorus in the closest window; hyperphosphatemia is a relatively direct consequence of reduced GFR and can precede/track creatinine rise. Among phosphorus features, this extreme and timing make it the strongest sparse-model candidate.
Lowest phosphorus early post-op often reflects dilution, insulin/alkalosis shifts, or replacement rather than renal dysfunction. Weak and potentially misleading for predicting later creatinine ratio.
Minimum phosphorus in 13–24h is more related to supplementation/redistribution than impaired clearance. Low unique value for AKI prediction compared with phosphorus maxima.
Minimum phosphorus even in the closest window is not a typical AKI signal and is heavily confounded by repletion practices. Unlikely to survive sparse selection versus phosphorus_max/mean.
Number of phosphorus checks in 0–12h reflects monitoring intensity and protocols rather than kidney physiology. Low priority proxy feature.
Measurement frequency of phosphorus in 13–24h is mainly clinician behavior/acuity proxy. Adds little unique predictive value in a sparse linear model.
More frequent phosphorus checks at 25–36h may indicate clinician concern for metabolic/renal issues. Still a proxy and generally dominated by the actual phosphorus values, so only modest priority.
Average neutrophil count early post-op reflects inflammation/stress response and possible infection risk. Indirect for AKI and early window is noisy, so low-to-moderate sparse value.

Mean neutrophil count at 13–24h captures ongoing inflammatory burden which can correlate with organ dysfunction. Still nonspecific and likely secondary to stronger hemodynamic/renal markers, so limited sparse-model priority.

Peak ANC in the immediate post-op window reflects inflammatory/stress response or infection risk. It is an indirect AKI signal and early post-bypass leukocytosis is common, so it’s unlikely to survive sparse selection.

Peak ANC at 13–24h may better reflect evolving systemic inflammation/infection than the first 12h. Still indirect for renal dysfunction and likely dominated by more direct perfusion/renal markers, so moderate priority.
Lowest ANC early post-op is usually not a meaningful driver of AKI risk and can reflect lab variability/hemodilution. Redundant with other inflammatory markers and low yield for sparse modeling.
Minimum ANC in 13–24h is rarely a specific precursor of AKI compared with hemodynamics/renal labs. Adds little unique predictive value versus ANC max or other severity features.
Count of ANC measurements is primarily a monitoring-intensity/clinician-behavior proxy. It is not a physiologic renal signal and is unlikely to add unique value in a sparse model.
Measurement frequency reflects care intensity rather than kidney physiology. Low priority and likely redundant with overall acuity indicators.
Later measurement frequency may capture ongoing concern for infection/instability closer to the outcome window. Still a proxy variable, so only weak-to-modest sparse-model value.

Troponin measurement count reflects post-op surveillance patterns rather than myocardial injury magnitude. As a proxy and early window, it is unlikely to contribute uniquely to creatinine ratio prediction.
More frequent troponins at 13–24h can indicate concern for perioperative MI/low output, which can secondarily affect renal perfusion. Still primarily clinician behavior and not a direct renal marker.

Troponin measurement frequency closer to 60h may proxy ongoing hemodynamic/cardiac concerns relevant to renal hypoperfusion. However it remains a monitoring proxy and is weaker than direct hemodynamic/renal features.
Average PaO2 in the first 12h is heavily influenced by ventilator settings and perioperative management. Mean values smooth extremes and are weak/indirect for AKI risk, so low sparse priority.
Sustained oxygenation status at 13–24h may reflect pulmonary dysfunction and overall severity that can correlate with AKI. Still indirect and mean PaO2 is often management-driven, so modest value.

Mean PaO2 at 25–36h is closer to the outcome and may capture persistent respiratory failure/ongoing critical illness linked to AKI risk. Indirect but potentially more stable than early values, giving moderate sparse-model priority.

Maximum PaO2 early post-op mainly reflects high FiO2/ventilator strategy rather than physiology relevant to kidney injury. As an extreme in the ‘high’ direction it is not a typical AKI insult marker and is low value.
Peak PaO2 at 13–24h is largely treatment-driven and not a direct indicator of renal hypoperfusion or evolving AKI. Likely redundant/noisy for sparse selection.

Later peak PaO2 may still mostly reflect ventilator management, but could weakly indicate ongoing high oxygen requirements/critical illness context. Remains indirect and less informative than hypoxemia metrics, so low-to-modest priority.

Lowest arterial oxygen tension in the immediate post-op window, reflecting hypoxemia/respiratory instability. Indirect AKI risk signal and early post-bypass noise; likely dominated by hemodynamics/renal markers in a sparse model.
Minimum PaO2 in the 13–24h window, capturing more sustained hypoxemia than 0–12h. Still an indirect severity marker with limited unique renal-specific value.

Minimum PaO2 closest to the prediction horizon, reflecting ongoing hypoxemia that can worsen renal oxygen delivery. More temporally relevant and extreme-based, but remains indirect and may be redundant with other illness-severity features.

Count/frequency of PaO2 measurements in 0–12h, mainly reflecting monitoring intensity and ventilator/ABG practice. Proxy for clinician behavior and early instability; low priority for sparse selection.
Frequency of PaO2 measurements in 13–24h, a monitoring/acuity proxy rather than physiology. Unlikely to add unique predictive value beyond actual oxygenation/hemodynamic variables.
Frequency of PaO2 measurements in 25–36h may reflect persistent respiratory failure/ICU complexity near the target time. Still largely a clinician-behavior proxy, so only modest sparse-model value.

Average serum potassium in 0–12h, influenced by transfusion, bypass, acidosis, and early shifts. Mean smooths extremes and early window is noisy, so it is a weaker AKI signal than later/peak potassium.
Average potassium in 13–24h, reflecting evolving renal handling and ongoing cell/acid-base issues. Moderately informative but likely redundant with potassium extremes (max/min) in the same epoch.
Average potassium in 25–36h, closer to Hour 60 and may reflect sustained impaired excretion. Useful but still less sensitive than peak hyperkalemia for sparse selection.

Peak potassium in 0–12h captures hyperkalemia episodes that can reflect early renal hypoperfusion or massive shifts. Extreme is more informative than mean, but early post-op confounding reduces priority.
Peak potassium in 13–24h is a strong marker of impaired renal excretion and evolving AKI trajectory. Extreme-based and mid-course timing makes it a strong sparse-model candidate within this batch.

Peak potassium in 25–36h is a near-term, extreme marker of reduced renal clearance and impending AKI, closest to the Hour 60 creatinine outcome. High unique predictive value relative to means/mins and likely to survive sparse selection.
Lowest potassium in 0–12h reflects hypokalemia from diuresis/insulin/alkalosis or dilution. Less directly tied to AKI than hyperkalemia and early window is noisy, so low sparse priority.
Minimum potassium in 13–24h may reflect treatment effects or diuretic exposure rather than renal failure. Generally weaker and more confounded than potassium_max for predicting creatinine rise.

Minimum potassium in 25–36h is closer in time but still more reflective of therapy (replacement/diuretics/insulin) than intrinsic renal dysfunction. Likely redundant and low yield versus potassium_max.
Frequency of potassium checks in 0–12h is primarily a monitoring/acuity and protocol-driven behavior proxy. Adds little unique physiologic information for a sparse linear model.

Count/frequency of potassium checks in 13–24h is mainly a monitoring-intensity/clinician-concern proxy rather than a renal physiology signal. Unlikely to add unique value in a sparse model versus actual potassium/creatinine/urine output features.

Frequency of potassium measurement in 25–36h may weakly reflect evolving AKI/arrhythmia risk prompting closer monitoring. Still largely a behavior proxy and typically dominated by direct lab values and hemodynamics, so low sparse-selection priority.

Average bilirubin in the immediate post-op window reflects hepatic dysfunction/hemolysis and overall illness severity more than kidney-specific injury. Mean in 0–12h is also noisy and likely redundant with later trajectory or extremes.

Peak bilirubin early post-op can capture acute hepatic hypoperfusion/hemolysis from bypass, which correlates with multi-organ dysfunction and AKI risk. Among bilirubin summaries, the max preserves insult signal best, but timing is early and indirect for creatinine at 60h.
Minimum bilirubin in 0–12h is not physiologically informative for AKI risk and mostly reflects baseline/measurement noise. Clearly dominated by bilirubin max or later values if available.
Bilirubin measurement frequency in 0–12h is primarily a clinician-behavior/monitoring proxy. Adds little unique predictive information for 60h creatinine ratio in a sparse model.
Number of bilirubin checks in 13–24h reflects concern for liver dysfunction/hemolysis rather than a direct renal signal. Typically redundant with actual bilirubin values and other severity markers.

Later (25–36h) bilirubin check frequency may indicate ongoing organ dysfunction prompting continued monitoring. Still a proxy variable and unlikely to survive sparse selection against direct physiologic/lab predictors.

Mean RBC count early post-op reflects anemia/hemodilution/transfusion effects, which can relate to renal hypoxia but is indirect. Early mean is also smoothed and likely redundant with nadir measures and later epoch values.

Average RBC count in 13–24h captures ongoing anemia/hemodilution but remains an indirect AKI predictor. Mean is generally dominated by the minimum (nadir) for capturing clinically relevant oxygen-delivery risk.

Mean RBC count closest to the target window may reflect sustained anemia affecting renal oxygen delivery and ongoing bleeding/hemodilution. Still indirect, but timing improves usefulness relative to earlier means.
Maximum RBC count in 0–12h is rarely the pathologic signal for AKI risk and may reflect hemoconcentration or transfusion timing. Dominated by nadir (min) and later trajectory for sparse prediction.
RBC max in 13–24h is not a strong mechanistic driver of AKI compared with anemia/nadir or perfusion variables. Likely redundant/noisy and low priority for sparse inclusion.
RBC max in 25–36h provides limited incremental information for AKI prediction versus nadir or mean and may reflect transfusion effects. Low unique value for sparse model selection.

Early nadir RBC count captures worst anemia/hemodilution/transfusion needs, which can contribute to renal hypoxia and correlate with surgical complexity. More informative than mean/max, but 0–12h timing is still relatively noisy.

Nadir RBC count in 13–24h captures clinically meaningful anemia after initial stabilization, reflecting oxygen-delivery limitation and ongoing bleeding/hemodilution that can precede AKI. Among RBC summaries in this batch, this is the strongest sparse-model candidate due to extremum and better timing.

Lowest RBC count in the closest (25–36h) window reflects anemia/bleeding/hemodilution and overall physiologic stress. It is only an indirect AKI signal and is likely dominated by more direct perfusion/renal markers, so low sparse-selection priority.

Whether RBC count was checked in 0–12h is mainly a monitoring/clinical workflow proxy. Early post-op measurement frequency is noisy and not a direct renal physiology signal, so it is unlikely to survive sparse selection.

RBC measurement occurrence in 13–24h reflects clinician concern/ICU intensity rather than kidney function. As a proxy variable with high confounding and redundancy with other “measured” flags, it has minimal unique predictive value.

RBC measurement occurrence in 25–36h may weakly indicate ongoing bleeding/anemia evaluation closer to the outcome time. Still primarily a clinician-behavior proxy and generally dominated by actual RBC values/transfusion/hemodynamics, so only a modest score.

Whether D-dimer was ordered in 0–12h is a test-ordering proxy (concern for thrombosis/DIC) rather than a physiologic value. Early post-op ordering is especially non-specific and unlikely to add unique predictive signal for creatinine ratio.
D-dimer measurement in 13–24h reflects clinician suspicion and complexity more than renal injury biology. As a binary “measured” feature it is weak and redundant, so low priority.

D-dimer measurement in 25–36h could mark evolving thrombotic/inflammatory complications closer to Hour 60. However it remains a monitoring proxy (not the level) and is too indirect for AKI prediction, so only slight value.

Whether TEG ACT was measured in 0–12h mainly captures perioperative coagulation management practices. It is a clinician-behavior proxy with high early post-op noise and little direct linkage to later creatinine rise.

TEG ACT measurement in 13–24h indicates ongoing coagulation evaluation rather than renal perfusion or injury. As a binary ordering flag it is unlikely to contribute unique predictive value in a sparse model.

TEG ACT measurement in 25–36h may signal persistent bleeding/coagulopathy management closer to the outcome window. Still a proxy variable and generally dominated by direct hemodynamics/transfusion/renal markers, so modest importance.

Mean immature granulocyte percentage in 0–12h is a nonspecific inflammation/stress marker immediately after surgery. Early values are often transient and indirect for AKI, and the mean smooths peaks, so low sparse-selection value.

Minimum immature granulocyte percentage in 13–24h provides little pathophysiologic information compared with peaks or sustained elevation. It is redundant and unlikely to survive sparse selection.

Frequency of checking immature granulocyte % is mainly a monitoring/clinician-behavior proxy rather than a renal physiology signal. Unlikely to add unique value for Hour-60 creatinine beyond core hemodynamics/renal markers.

Later monitoring frequency still primarily captures clinician concern/complexity rather than a mechanistic AKI precursor. Even with closer timing, it is a weak, indirect proxy for creatinine rise.

Whether TEG fibrinolysis was measured reflects bleeding/coagulopathy evaluation early post-op. This is largely unrelated to renal trajectory and is a clinician-action proxy, so low sparse-model value.

Late TEG measurement remains a proxy for bleeding/coagulopathy management and overall complexity. Despite temporal proximity, it is not a direct AKI signal and is unlikely to survive sparse selection.

Average early post-op creatinine ratio captures initial renal response but is susceptible to immediate post-bypass dilution/shift noise. Useful but dominated by later windows and by extreme/trajectory summaries.
Mean creatinine ratio in 13–24h reflects early AKI trajectory after immediate post-op noise settles. Strong predictor, though somewhat redundant with max in the same/later epoch.

Mean creatinine ratio at 25–36h is the closest available direct renal function marker to the 60h target and captures sustained elevation. Highest-priority sparse feature because it directly encodes evolving AKI trajectory.

Peak creatinine ratio in 0–12h can capture an early insult but is often confounded by perioperative fluid shifts and transient changes. Moderately useful, but later peaks are typically more predictive for 60h.
Peak creatinine ratio in 13–24h captures early worsening and is less confounded than the immediate post-op window. Strong candidate, though partially redundant with 25–36h creatinine features.

Peak creatinine ratio in 25–36h is a near-direct indicator of ongoing AKI close to the prediction horizon and preserves acute worsening better than means. Top sparse-model keeper and likely dominates earlier maxima.
Minimum early creatinine ratio mainly reflects dilution/hemodilution or measurement variability rather than kidney injury. Low unique value and likely noisy in the immediate post-op period.
Minimum creatinine ratio in 13–24h is generally less informative for predicting later elevation than mean/max trajectory measures. Likely redundant and not a strong AKI signal.

Minimum creatinine ratio in 25–36h may weakly indicate recovery vs persistent elevation, but it is usually dominated by mean/max which better capture AKI burden. Low-to-moderate priority due to redundancy.

Number of creatinine measurements in 0–12h reflects monitoring intensity and clinical concern rather than physiology. Could add slight signal about instability/AKI suspicion, but is a weak proxy and early-window biased.

Counts how often creatinine ratio was measured in 13–24h, reflecting monitoring intensity/clinician concern rather than renal physiology. Weak, indirect proxy and likely dominated by actual creatinine values/trajectory if available.

Measurement frequency of creatinine ratio in the closest window (25–36h) may weakly proxy evolving AKI prompting repeat labs. Still a behavior/acuity marker and usually redundant with direct renal biomarkers, so low sparse-model priority.

Indicator of hyperoncotic (25%) albumin use early post-op, often reflecting hypotension/third-spacing or resuscitation strategy. Early window is noisy and this is largely a treatment/behavior proxy with confounding by indication.

25% albumin use in 13–24h can signal ongoing capillary leak, hemodynamic instability, or attempts to mobilize fluid—contexts associated with AKI risk. Still a clinician-action proxy, but more temporally informative than 0–12h.

25% albumin use in 25–36h suggests persistent volume management issues or hemodynamic fragility closer to the outcome window. Moderate value as a proxy for severity/renal perfusion concerns, but not a direct kidney signal.

Amount of 5% albumin given in 0–12h reflects early resuscitation/volume expansion after surgery. High confounding by indication and early post-bypass fluid shifts reduce unique predictive value for Hour-60 creatinine ratio.

5% albumin dose in 13–24h may capture ongoing intravascular volume needs and illness severity that correlate with AKI risk. Moderate sparse-model candidate but likely redundant with direct hemodynamics/vasopressors/urine output if present.

5% albumin dose in 25–36h is closer to the outcome and may reflect persistent hemodynamic/volume issues relevant to renal perfusion. Still primarily a treatment proxy and may be dominated by more direct perfusion/renal markers.

Analgesic dosing (acetaminophen with codeine) mainly reflects pain control/sedation practices rather than renal pathophysiology. Minimal expected unique signal for AKI prediction and likely noise in sparse selection.
Continued acetaminophen-codeine dosing reflects analgesia needs and prescribing patterns, not kidney function. Weak, indirect, and unlikely to survive sparse feature selection.
Analgesic dose in 25–36h is still largely unrelated to renal injury mechanisms and heavily practice-dependent. Very low priority versus direct renal/perfusion variables.
Acetaminophen dose early post-op is a routine analgesic/antipyretic exposure with little direct linkage to AKI at typical doses. Mostly reflects care patterns and adds little unique predictive value.
Acetaminophen dosing in 13–24h is common and not a meaningful renal insult marker in this context. Unlikely to contribute beyond noise in a sparse linear model.
Acetaminophen dose in 25–36h remains a weak, non-specific treatment variable with minimal mechanistic connection to creatinine rise. Very low sparse-model selection priority.
Allopurinol dosing reflects chronic gout/hyperuricemia management rather than acute perioperative renal physiology. Likely rare and confounded, offering little incremental predictive value.
Allopurinol administration in 13–24h is a continuation medication and not a near-term AKI precursor. Low prevalence and weak mechanistic tie make it unlikely to survive sparse selection.

Allopurinol exposure in this window mainly reflects chronic gout/hyperuricemia management rather than acute renal hypoperfusion or evolving AKI. Likely low prevalence and weak, indirect linkage to Hour-60 creatinine ratio, so poor sparse-model priority.

Early postoperative aminocaproic acid use is largely a surgical/bleeding-management practice pattern. It is an indirect proxy with substantial confounding and early (0–12h) noise, so unlikely to add unique predictive value for creatinine_ratio.

Aminocaproic acid use in 13–24h still primarily reflects bleeding/antifibrinolytic strategy rather than kidney physiology. Any association with AKI is indirect and likely dominated by hemodynamics/transfusion variables not in this batch.

Late (25–36h) aminocaproic acid suggests ongoing bleeding risk or re-intervention, which can correlate with renal injury via shock/transfusion burden. Still a clinician-behavior proxy and not kidney-specific, but timing is closer to Hour 60 so slightly higher sparse value than earlier epochs.

Amiodarone early post-op indicates atrial/ventricular arrhythmia prophylaxis or treatment, which can correlate with low cardiac output states. However 0–12h is noisy and the signal is indirect and likely redundant with direct hemodynamic measures, so low priority.

Amiodarone in 13–24h may reflect clinically significant arrhythmias and instability that can contribute to renal hypoperfusion. Still an indirect treatment proxy and not kidney-specific, so unlikely to survive sparse selection if stronger perfusion/vasopressor features are available.

Amiodarone use at 25–36h suggests persistent arrhythmia/low-output physiology closer to the creatinine outcome window. It can add some unique prognostic information about ongoing instability, though it remains an indirect proxy rather than a direct renal marker.

Amlodipine administration is typically chronic antihypertensive continuation and not an acute ICU titratable marker of perfusion. In 0–12h it is especially unlikely to reflect evolving AKI risk, so minimal sparse-model value.

Amlodipine in 13–24h mainly reflects medication reconciliation/continuation rather than renal physiology. Any association with creatinine_ratio would be weak and confounded, making it a poor sparse selection candidate.

Late amlodipine use still primarily indicates chronic BP management and clinician preference, not kidney injury trajectory. It is unlikely to add unique predictive signal beyond direct hemodynamics/renal labs.

Ampicillin exposure early post-op is usually prophylaxis or empiric infection coverage and is not a direct renal injury marker. It is a treatment/behavior proxy with weak specificity for AKI risk, so low sparse priority.
Ampicillin in 13–24h may indicate suspected infection/sepsis, which can contribute to AKI risk. Still indirect and confounded (antibiotic choice varies), so only modest value for sparse selection.

Continued ampicillin at 25–36h suggests ongoing infection concern closer to the outcome window, which can relate to inflammatory AKI. However it remains a nonspecific clinician-action proxy and likely dominated by physiologic severity markers.

Angiotensin II use indicates refractory vasodilatory shock requiring high-tier vasopressor support, strongly linked to renal hypoperfusion and AKI risk. Even in 0–12h it is a high-specificity severity signal that may add unique value in a sparse model.

Angiotensin II requirement in 13–24h reflects persistent severe shock, a strong precursor to worsening kidney function. It is a relatively specific marker of extreme vasopressor need and likely to survive sparse screening versus many medication proxies.

Angiotensin II dose at 25–36h captures ongoing refractory shock closest to Hour 60, tightly linked to sustained renal hypoperfusion and evolving AKI. Within this batch it is the most kidney-relevant, high-specificity severity marker and a top sparse-model keeper.

Early postoperative apixaban exposure mainly reflects anticoagulation strategy/bleeding risk rather than renal physiology. Likely weak and behavior-driven, and dominated by later-window dosing if any signal exists.
Apixaban dosing in this window is a clinician-choice proxy (AF/VTE prophylaxis) with limited direct linkage to evolving AKI. Any association is likely confounded and not a sparse-model keeper.

Later apixaban dosing may indirectly reflect stability and renal function (dose holding in AKI/bleeding), making it slightly more informative than earlier epochs. Still largely a treatment/selection proxy and not a direct renal signal.

Immediate postoperative aspirin use reflects routine post-cardiac surgery antiplatelet practice and bleeding considerations. It is unlikely to add unique predictive value for Hour-60 creatinine ratio.
Aspirin dosing here is mostly protocolized and only weakly tied to renal hypoperfusion/AKI. Sparse selection would favor hemodynamics/renal markers over this medication proxy.

Aspirin continuation by 25–36h may indicate clinical stability but remains a nonspecific care-pattern variable. Low unique value for predicting creatinine trajectory compared with direct renal/volume/hemodynamic features.
Early statin administration reflects chronic therapy resumption and institutional practice, not acute kidney injury physiology. Any effect on creatinine over 60h is likely minimal and indirect.
Statin dosing in this window is largely routine and confounded by ability to take enteral meds. It is a weak proxy and unlikely to survive sparse feature selection.

By 25–36h, receiving atorvastatin may weakly proxy recovery/enteral tolerance and overall stability, which can correlate with lower AKI risk. Still indirect and dominated by more proximal physiologic predictors.
Bisacodyl use is a bowel regimen marker and primarily reflects routine ICU care rather than renal function. Very low expected unique predictive value for creatinine ratio.
Bowel regimen administration is a nonspecific care-pattern variable and may only weakly proxy extubation/enteral status. Not a priority feature for sparse modeling of AKI.

Later bisacodyl use can proxy clinical stability/enteral feeding and less severe illness, which may correlate with better renal outcomes. Still indirect and likely redundant with stronger severity/hemodynamic features.

Bivalirudin use early post-op suggests heparin avoidance/HIT concern or complex anticoagulation, which can correlate with higher acuity and renal risk. However it is largely a treatment-selection proxy and early timing is noisy.

Ongoing bivalirudin at 13–24h may better capture sustained complexity/complications (e.g., HIT suspicion, ECMO/VAD contexts) associated with AKI risk. Still indirect, but more informative than 0–12h and may add unique acuity signal within this batch.

Bivalirudin use at 25–36h is a strong proxy for persistent high-acuity postoperative course and complex anticoagulation contexts that are tightly linked to AKI risk. Among these medication features, this later-window indicator is most likely to survive sparse selection.

Early loop diuretic dosing reflects attempts to manage volume overload/oliguria and can indicate early renal stress or congestion after cardiac surgery. Despite early-window noise, it is a more kidney-adjacent signal than the other medications in this batch and may add unique predictive value.

Loop diuretic requirement in the 13–24h window reflects evolving volume overload/oliguria and clinician response to early renal dysfunction. Strong AKI/renal congestion proxy, though somewhat behavior-mediated and dominated by the later 25–36h dose if both are available.

Loop diuretic requirement closest to the outcome window captures persistent diuretic resistance/volume overload and evolving AKI physiology. High sparse-model value due to temporal proximity and strong linkage to renal trajectory, despite some clinician-behavior component.

Calcium acetate use is mainly a chronic CKD/hyperphosphatemia medication signal rather than an acute renal injury marker. In the immediate 0–12h post-op period it is noisy and largely redundant with baseline comorbidity information.

Ongoing calcium acetate suggests underlying CKD/mineral bone disease and possibly reduced renal reserve. Still largely a chronic-condition proxy and likely redundant with baseline creatinine/comorbidity features, so low sparse priority.

Later calcium acetate administration continues to reflect pre-existing CKD management more than acute AKI evolution. Slightly more informative by timing, but still a weak, behavior-driven proxy unlikely to survive sparse selection.

Calcium carbonate is commonly given for supplementation/antacid or chronic CKD-related binding and is not a direct AKI signal. Early post-op dosing is highly practice-driven and unlikely to add unique predictive value.

Later calcium carbonate may weakly correlate with CKD/mineral management or electrolyte replacement patterns. Still indirect and behavior-mediated, so only modest priority despite better timing.

IV calcium chloride often reflects hemodynamic instability, transfusion/citrate load, or post-bypass electrolyte management. It is an indirect severity/procedure proxy and early timing is noisy, limiting sparse-model value.

Continued need for calcium chloride suggests ongoing instability or significant electrolyte derangements, which can track with AKI risk. Still largely a nonspecific severity/clinician-response marker and not kidney-specific.

Late calcium chloride use may indicate persistent critical illness (e.g., ongoing transfusions/vasopressors) and thus higher AKI risk closer to the outcome window. Moderately useful as a severity correlate, but indirect and likely dominated by direct renal/hemodynamic features.

IV calcium gluconate is typically given for hypocalcemia, hyperkalemia stabilization, or post-op electrolyte replacement. Early dosing is common and practice-driven, making it a weak, indirect predictor of later creatinine rise.

Ongoing calcium gluconate may reflect persistent electrolyte abnormalities or severity of illness that correlates with AKI risk. However it is nonspecific and likely redundant with stronger physiologic signals (hemodynamics, labs) in a sparse model.

Later calcium gluconate use can mark ongoing metabolic derangements (e.g., hyperkalemia treatment) closer to Hour 60, which may co-occur with evolving AKI. Moderately informative by timing but still an indirect clinician-action proxy.

Carvedilol administration mainly reflects baseline beta-blocker therapy and clinician comfort restarting it, not acute renal physiology. Immediate post-op dosing is highly variable and unlikely to add unique predictive value for creatinine ratio.

Restarting carvedilol by 13–24h may indicate hemodynamic stability and lower illness severity, indirectly relating to AKI risk. Still a treatment/behavior proxy and likely redundant with direct hemodynamic measures in sparse selection.

Beta-blocker exposure in the 25–36h window, reflecting rate/BP control and clinician preference. Only an indirect renal signal (hemodynamic management proxy) and likely redundant with direct BP/vasopressor features, so low sparse-model priority.

Early postoperative cefepime dosing, mainly indicating empiric broad-spectrum antibiotic use. Weak and behavior-driven with limited direct linkage to AKI at 60h, and the 0–12h window is especially noisy.
Cefepime dosing in 13–24h suggests ongoing concern for infection/sepsis (an AKI risk context). Still largely a clinician-behavior proxy and not a direct renal physiology marker, so only modest value.

Cefepime dosing closest to the outcome window may capture evolving infection/sepsis severity and renal-dose adjustment behavior. Among cefepime epochs this is the most informative temporally, but remains indirect and confounded, so moderate priority.

Binary indicator of ceftriaxone use in the immediate 0–12h period. Mostly reflects prophylaxis/empiric coverage patterns rather than kidney injury trajectory, and is dominated by later-window signals if any.

Ceftriaxone use in 13–24h may indicate infection workup/therapy and overall illness context. Still a weak, indirect proxy with substantial practice-pattern confounding, so low-to-moderate sparse value.
