Early clonidine dosing reflects sedation/analgesia adjunct and BP control in the immediate post-op period. Highly practice-driven and early-window noise makes it unlikely to add unique predictive value for 60h creatinine ratio.
Clonidine dosing in 13–24h may proxy ongoing sedation needs or hypertension management. Indirect and likely redundant with direct hemodynamic/ventilation severity measures, so low priority.

Clonidine dosing in 25–36h is closer to the outcome and may reflect persistent sympathetic control needs. Still primarily a treatment-choice proxy with weak mechanistic linkage to AKI, so modest importance.

Early clopidogrel exposure reflects antiplatelet management rather than renal physiology. Any association with creatinine is indirect (bleeding/transfusion pathways) and likely dominated by direct anemia/hemodynamic features.
Clopidogrel dosing in 13–24h indicates antiplatelet strategy post-surgery. It is not a direct AKI precursor and is unlikely to survive sparse selection versus stronger perfusion/inflammation markers.
Later clopidogrel use may correlate with procedural context (e.g., CABG/PCI) and bleeding risk management closer to 60h. Still indirect and confounded, so only modest incremental value.
Early colchicine use typically reflects pericarditis prophylaxis/treatment rather than kidney injury. Minimal direct renal signal and early timing makes it a weak sparse-model candidate.
Colchicine dosing in 13–24h is mainly an anti-inflammatory treatment choice. Any renal association is indirect and likely redundant with direct inflammatory/lab markers, so very low priority.

Colchicine use in 25–36h may indicate ongoing inflammatory complications (e.g., pericarditis) closer to the outcome window. Still a clinician-behavior proxy with weak mechanistic linkage to AKI, so modest importance.

D10 in lactated Ringer’s given in the immediate post-op window, mainly reflecting routine glucose/volume management. Weak, clinician-driven proxy and early timing; unlikely to add unique AKI signal in a sparse model.

D10 LR use in 13–24h may indicate ongoing hypoglycemia management or continued fluid administration. Still largely treatment-pattern/volume proxy and likely redundant with broader fluid balance/hemodynamics features.

D10 LR use closer to the outcome window could reflect persistent instability or nutrition/glucose issues. However it remains a nonspecific clinician-behavior/fluids proxy with limited unique renal predictive value.

D10W administration in the first 12h is common perioperative practice for glucose control and carrier fluids. Early and highly protocol-driven, so it is unlikely to survive sparse selection for predicting creatinine ratio.

Total D10W dose in 13–24h may capture ongoing dextrose needs or fluid exposure. Some signal for illness/feeding trajectory, but indirect for AKI and typically dominated by direct renal perfusion/urine output/lab features.

D10W use at 25–36h could reflect continued metabolic support or fluid administration closer to Hour 60. Still nonspecific and likely redundant with total fluids and severity markers, so low sparse-model priority.

D5 0.45% NS volume in 0–12h reflects hypotonic maintenance fluid exposure. Fluid type/amount can influence creatinine via dilution and perfusion, but early timing and indirectness limit unique predictive value.
Use of D5 0.45% NS in 13–24h suggests ongoing maintenance fluids and clinician preference. Indirect AKI signal and likely redundant with overall fluid balance and hemodynamic variables.

D5 0.45% NS use in 25–36h is closer to the creatinine outcome and may reflect ongoing fluid strategy affecting renal perfusion/dilution. Still indirect, but among this batch it has relatively more temporal relevance.
D5 LR in the first 12h is largely routine perioperative fluid/carrier choice. Early and treatment-driven, offering minimal unique information about evolving AKI.
D5 LR use in 13–24h may reflect continued fluid administration and metabolic support. Indirect and likely redundant with total crystalloid/volume status features, so low priority.

Total D5 LR dose in 25–36h captures later fluid exposure that could relate to renal perfusion and creatinine dilution. Still a proxy for management rather than kidney injury itself, but the numeric dose and timing make it one of the more informative in this batch.
D5 NS in 0–12h is common post-op maintenance/carrier fluid and reflects clinician practice more than renal physiology. Early window and high protocol dependence make it weak for sparse selection.
D5 NS use in 13–24h indicates ongoing maintenance fluids and possibly continued instability. Indirect AKI relevance and redundancy with overall fluid balance/hemodynamics keep it low.

D5 NS exposure in 25–36h is temporally closer and may correlate with ongoing fluid needs that track with renal hypoperfusion or dilutional effects. Still nonspecific, but comparatively more useful than earlier epochs in this batch.

D50W bolus use suggests treatment of significant hypoglycemia or insulin-related management early post-op. It can mark metabolic instability/severity, but it is episodic, early, and an indirect proxy for AKI risk.

D50W use suggests treatment of hypoglycemia or insulin-driven glucose management, which is only indirectly related to AKI risk. Likely a clinician-behavior/metabolic proxy and not a top sparse-model keeper for creatinine trajectory.

Later D50W use may reflect ongoing glycemic instability or nutrition/insulin titration rather than renal physiology. Despite closer timing, it remains an indirect care-pattern signal with limited unique value for Hour-60 creatinine ratio.

D5W volume reflects maintenance fluids or hypernatremia management and is weakly tied to renal injury mechanisms. Early post-op fluid choices are noisy and largely redundant with better hemodynamic/renal perfusion markers.

D5W in this window is still mainly a fluid-management/nutrition proxy rather than a direct kidney dysfunction signal. Unlikely to survive sparse selection versus stronger predictors like hypotension, vasopressors, lactate, or urine output.

Later D5W may capture ongoing fluid strategy and free-water needs, which can correlate with illness complexity. Closer timing helps slightly, but it remains indirect and likely redundant with net fluid balance and hemodynamics.

Dialysate infusion implies renal replacement therapy (or preparation for it), a near-direct indicator of severe renal dysfunction. Even early, this is a strong, non-redundant signal that strongly predicts elevated creatinine ratio.

Dialysate infusion in 13–24h strongly indicates evolving AKI requiring RRT, tightly linked to subsequent creatinine behavior. High unique predictive value for sparse selection because it encodes a major renal endpoint/trajectory.

Dialysate infusion closest to the target time window is an extremely strong marker of ongoing severe AKI/RRT dependence. This should be among the first retained features in a sparse linear model for Hour-60 creatinine ratio.

Hydromorphone PCA dosing reflects analgesia strategy and patient wakefulness rather than renal perfusion or injury. Early post-op dosing is highly practice-dependent and offers little unique predictive value for creatinine ratio.

PCA opioid dose is mainly a sedation/comfort and care-pattern proxy with weak mechanistic linkage to AKI. Likely redundant with overall severity/ventilation status and not competitive for sparse selection.

Later PCA dosing still primarily reflects analgesia needs and extubation/mental status rather than kidney injury. Minimal unique signal for creatinine ratio compared with direct renal/hemodynamic variables.

Total hydromorphone dose is an analgesia/sedation marker and may reflect clinician preference more than physiology. Early dosing is noisy and unlikely to add unique predictive information for AKI beyond severity markers.

Opioid dosing in this window is still an indirect proxy for pain control, ventilation status, and practice patterns. Low priority for sparse modeling because it is not a direct renal signal and is likely confounded.
Later hydromorphone dose remains a weak, indirect predictor of renal dysfunction and is dominated by direct kidney/hemodynamic features. Not expected to survive sparse feature selection.

Dobutamine use indicates low cardiac output/cardiogenic shock risk, which can drive renal hypoperfusion and AKI. However, the immediate post-op window is noisy and this may be partly redundant with MAP/vasopressor/lactate signals.

Dobutamine requirement at 13–24h suggests persistent low-output state, a strong precursor to renal hypoperfusion and worsening creatinine. More informative than 0–12h and likely to add unique value beyond simple vitals in a sparse model.

Total dobutamine requirement in the 25–36h window reflects ongoing low cardiac output/cardiogenic shock physiology that can drive renal hypoperfusion and evolving AKI. Late inotrope need is a relatively strong, nonredundant severity/hemodynamic signal likely to survive sparse selection.

Docusate is a bowel regimen medication and does not directly reflect renal physiology. In the immediate 0–12h window it is largely routine care/ordering behavior and adds little unique predictive value.
Docusate dosing mainly indicates bowel regimen use rather than kidney function or perfusion. It is a weak clinician-behavior proxy and unlikely to be selected in a sparse model.

Docusate use at 25–36h still reflects routine constipation prophylaxis more than renal risk. It is not a meaningful physiologic precursor of Hour-60 creatinine rise and is dominated by true hemodynamic/renal markers.

Early dopamine use can reflect hypotension/low output or older ‘renal-dose’ practice, both loosely related to AKI risk. However 0–12h is noisy post-op and dopamine is a treatment proxy with substantial practice-pattern confounding.

Dopamine requirement in 13–24h suggests persistent hemodynamic support needs beyond immediate post-op instability, which can precede AKI. Still partly redundant with other vasoactive/inotrope signals and influenced by clinician preference.

Late (25–36h) dopamine requirement indicates ongoing shock/low perfusion close to the Hour-60 outcome window, a strong precursor for creatinine rise. This timing makes it more informative than earlier epochs despite being a therapy proxy.

Enoxaparin dosing is primarily VTE prophylaxis/anticoagulation practice and not a direct renal biomarker. In 0–12h it is especially protocol-driven and offers minimal unique signal for creatinine_ratio.

Enoxaparin use may weakly proxy renal function (dose holding/reduction in AKI) and overall stability, but it is indirect and heavily confounded by protocols and bleeding risk. Likely redundant with more direct hemodynamic/renal indicators.

At 25–36h, enoxaparin administration/withholding can reflect clinician assessment of bleeding risk and sometimes renal dosing considerations. Still an indirect, behavior-driven proxy and unlikely to beat direct perfusion/AKI signals in sparse selection.

Ephedrine use in 0–12h typically reflects transient peri-anesthetic hypotension management rather than sustained shock. This early, episodic pressor signal is noisy and dominated by later vasoactive requirements.

Ephedrine use beyond the immediate post-op period may indicate intermittent hypotension, which can contribute to renal hypoperfusion. However it is a coarse binary treatment proxy and less informative than continuous inotrope/pressor dosing signals.

Ephedrine use at 25–36h suggests ongoing episodic hypotension close to the outcome window. Still, as a binary and often situational agent, it is weaker and more practice-dependent than sustained dopamine/dobutamine dosing.

Esmolol use early post-op reflects rate control for tachyarrhythmia/hypertension and is not a direct renal-risk marker. In 0–12h it is particularly confounded by transient post-bypass physiology and clinician preference.

Esmolol in 13–24h may proxy atrial fibrillation/tachycardia management and overall hemodynamic strategy, which can relate indirectly to renal perfusion. It remains an indirect treatment marker and likely redundant with direct hemodynamic variables.

Late esmolol use can indicate persistent tachyarrhythmia or hypertension management near the outcome window, with potential indirect links to renal perfusion. As a binary therapy indicator, it is still a weak, practice-driven predictor compared with inotrope requirements.

Etomidate exposure early post-op, mainly reflecting induction/sedation choice and hemodynamic instability risk. Weak, indirect AKI signal and early-window noise; may add a small proxy for shock/vasopressor need but unlikely to survive sparse selection.

Etomidate use in the 13–24h window suggests ongoing sedation needs/instability rather than a direct renal marker. Still largely clinician-behavior proxy and typically rare; limited unique value for creatinine_ratio.

Later etomidate use (25–36h) may indicate persistent critical illness or re-intubation/procedures closer to the outcome window. As a near-term severity proxy it can add some unique signal, but remains indirect and dominated by direct hemodynamics/renal markers (not in this batch).
Ezetimibe administration reflects chronic lipid management/resumption of home meds. No plausible short-term mechanistic link to AKI trajectory and likely pure confounding/noise for sparse modeling.
Continuation/resumption of ezetimibe in 13–24h is a routine medication-reconciliation signal. Minimal unique predictive value for Hour-60 creatinine ratio.
Ezetimibe given 25–36h post-op mainly indicates stability to take enteral meds. At best a weak proxy for recovery; too indirect and low-yield for sparse selection.
Famotidine use is stress-ulcer prophylaxis and common ICU routine care. It is not a direct renal signal and is heavily confounded by protocolized practice, so low priority.
Famotidine in 13–24h continues prophylaxis and reflects standard order sets more than physiology. Little unique information for predicting creatinine_ratio.

Famotidine at 25–36h remains routine prophylaxis; dose may be adjusted in renal dysfunction but that effect is inconsistent and redundant with direct renal labs. Overall weak and unlikely to survive sparse screening.

Fentanyl PCA dose reflects analgesia needs and ability to use PCA (often extubated/awake), indirectly tied to illness severity. Early post-op variability and strong confounding by practice make it a weak predictor.

PCA fentanyl in 13–24h may proxy extubation/neurologic status and overall recovery, which can correlate with lower AKI risk. Still indirect and likely redundant with more direct severity/hemodynamic features.

Later PCA fentanyl use (25–36h) is a closer-to-outcome proxy for being stable/awake and may inversely track with ongoing shock/ventilation that drives AKI. Moderately useful as a recovery marker, but still clinician-behavior mediated.
Total fentanyl dose early post-op reflects sedation/analgesia intensity and possibly hemodynamic tolerance. Indirect AKI signal and early-window noise; limited unique value in a sparse model.
Fentanyl dose in 13–24h can indicate ongoing ventilation/sedation needs and illness severity. Useful only as a proxy and likely redundant with other severity/hemodynamic variables.

Higher fentanyl requirements at 25–36h may mark persistent critical illness/ventilation closer to Hour 60, which correlates with AKI risk. Moderate sparse-model candidate as a near-term severity proxy, but not a direct renal biomarker.

Intranasal fluticasone is a chronic rhinitis medication and largely unrelated to perioperative renal physiology. Likely noise/med-reconciliation artifact with negligible predictive value for creatinine_ratio.

Intranasal steroid use reflects chronic rhinitis/allergy management rather than perioperative physiology. Very unlikely to add unique signal for AKI/creatinine trajectory and is mostly noise for sparse selection.

Intranasal fluticasone dosing in the closest window is still unrelated to renal perfusion or nephrotoxicity. Likely a routine home-med continuation and not a useful predictor of Hour-60 creatinine ratio.
Folic acid administration is a nutrition/supplement signal and not a direct renal biomarker. Early post-op timing plus low mechanistic link makes it a poor sparse-model candidate.
Folic acid use mainly reflects baseline supplementation or anemia management rather than evolving AKI. Adds little unique predictive value compared with hemodynamics/diuretics/fluids.
Later folic acid dosing remains a weak proxy for general care patterns, not kidney injury. Unlikely to survive sparse screening given minimal causal/associative link to creatinine rise.

Loop diuretic dosing reflects attempts to manage volume status/oliguria and can correlate with evolving renal dysfunction or congestion. However, the 0–12h window is noisy post-bypass and may be partly protocol-driven, so it’s less uniquely predictive than later dosing.

Furosemide dose in 13–24h captures early trajectory of fluid overload/oliguria and clinician response, often aligning with impending AKI. More informative than 0–12h and less redundant than non-renal meds, making it a strong sparse-model candidate.

Furosemide requirement closest to the target time is a strong near-term indicator of volume management difficulty/oliguria and evolving renal dysfunction. Likely to add high unique predictive value for Hour-60 creatinine ratio relative to other features in this batch.

Gabapentin dosing is primarily analgesia/sedation adjunct and not a direct renal physiology marker in this context. Early administration is often routine and unlikely to predict creatinine ratio beyond noise.

Gabapentin use reflects pain regimen and may be held/adjusted for delirium or renal function, but this is an indirect clinician-behavior proxy. Low priority for sparse selection compared with diuretics/fluids.

Later gabapentin dosing could weakly proxy renal impairment if dose is reduced/withheld, but this is indirect and confounded by sedation/delirium practices. At best a minor adjunct signal, not a core predictor.
Guaifenesin is an expectorant and mainly reflects respiratory secretion management. It has no meaningful mechanistic or predictive link to AKI/creatinine rise in a sparse model.
Continued guaifenesin use is a low-specificity marker of cough/secretions and routine care. Unlikely to contribute unique predictive value for renal outcomes.
Even in the closest window, guaifenesin dosing remains unrelated to renal perfusion or nephrotoxic exposure. Dominated by more kidney-relevant signals (e.g., diuretics/fluids) for sparse selection.

0.45% saline volume reflects early fluid administration and can relate to hemodilution/volume status, but 0–12h is heavily influenced by intra/post-op protocols. As a standalone predictor it’s indirect and likely redundant with better hemodynamic/urine output features (if available).

Half-normal saline volume in 13–24h better reflects ongoing fluid strategy and net volume burden, which can influence renal congestion and creatinine trajectory. Still an indirect treatment proxy, but more informative than the immediate 0–12h window.

Represents hypotonic maintenance fluid volume given in the closest (25–36h) window. Mostly a clinician-behavior/volume-management proxy and less directly tied to renal hypoperfusion/AKI than hemodynamics or urine output, so low sparse-model priority.

Early postoperative anticoagulation dose in the first 12h. Weak and indirect relationship to creatinine rise and heavily confounded by routine protocols; early window adds noise, so unlikely to survive sparse selection.
Anticoagulation dose during 13–24h post-op. Still largely protocol-driven and not a direct renal signal; provides little unique predictive value for AKI compared with physiologic markers.

Anticoagulation dose in the closest window (25–36h), potentially reflecting ongoing critical illness/ECMO/AF management. Remains an indirect care-pattern proxy with substantial confounding, but timing makes it slightly more informative than earlier epochs.
IV/PO afterload reduction/antihypertensive use in the immediate 0–12h period. Early post-op BP management is noisy and medication choice is clinician-driven; weak, indirect link to renal outcomes.

Hydralazine use in 13–24h may indicate persistent hypertension and attempts to avoid vasodilatory drips. Still an indirect proxy for BP control and not as informative as actual MAP minima, so low priority.

Hydralazine use in 25–36h reflects later BP management closer to the creatinine outcome window. Some signal via hemodynamic management, but remains medication-choice confounded and redundant with direct BP/hemodynamic features.
Opioid/acetaminophen analgesic dosing early after surgery. Primarily reflects pain control and extubation pathway rather than renal physiology; minimal unique value for predicting creatinine ratio.
Analgesic dosing in 13–24h. Mostly a proxy for recovery trajectory and care practices; weak and nonspecific for AKI prediction in a sparse model.
Analgesic dosing in 25–36h. Still largely unrelated to kidney injury mechanisms and likely redundant with more direct severity markers (ventilation, hemodynamics), so very low selection priority.

Hydrocortisone exposure in 0–12h often reflects vasoplegia/shock treatment or chronic steroid use. It is an indirect severity/vasopressor-resistance marker and early timing is noisy, so only modest value.

Hydrocortisone use in 13–24h suggests ongoing vasoplegia/shock physiology associated with renal hypoperfusion risk. Still a treatment proxy, but this window better captures persistent instability and may add unique signal.

Hydrocortisone use in 25–36h indicates persistent shock/vasoplegia close to the outcome horizon, which is strongly linked to evolving AKI risk. While treatment-confounded, its late persistence can add unique predictive value in a sparse model.

Hypertonic saline administration early post-op, typically for hyponatremia or intracranial concerns. Rare and highly indication-driven with little direct connection to AKI trajectory; early window further limits usefulness.
Hypertonic saline use in 13–24h. Still uncommon and dominated by specific indications rather than renal physiology; unlikely to contribute stable, generalizable predictive signal.

Hypertonic saline use in 25–36h may reflect ongoing dysnatremia management and complex critical illness closer to Hour 60. Remains a niche, indication-driven proxy, but timing gives slightly more potential signal than earlier epochs.

Early postoperative NSAID exposure can contribute to renal hypoperfusion/AKI risk, but in cardiac ICU it is often low/avoided and 0–12h is noisy. Likely weak and partly a clinician-behavior proxy; later dosing is more temporally relevant.

NSAID dosing in the 13–24h window may reflect ongoing analgesic strategy and can directly worsen renal perfusion in vulnerable elderly patients. More temporally informative than 0–12h and could add unique nephrotoxin signal if NSAIDs are used.

NSAID exposure closest to the outcome window is a direct, potentially causal nephrotoxin signal and may capture sustained use when AKI is evolving. Among the ibuprofen epochs, this is the strongest sparse-model candidate due to temporal proximity and mechanistic relevance.

Inhaled acetylcysteine is typically used for airway secretion management rather than renal protection. It is largely unrelated to kidney function and mainly reflects respiratory care intensity, so low sparse-selection value.

Continued inhaled acetylcysteine use indicates pulmonary toileting needs, not a direct renal physiology signal. Likely redundant with other severity/ventilation features and weakly linked to creatinine trajectory.

Late inhaled acetylcysteine remains a respiratory-therapy marker with minimal mechanistic connection to AKI. Even with better timing, it is unlikely to survive sparse screening versus direct hemodynamic/renal markers.

Inhaled LABA use reflects underlying COPD/asthma management and ventilatory course rather than renal perfusion or injury. Early post-op dosing is common protocolized care and offers little unique predictive value for creatinine ratio.

Arformoterol dosing in this window is still primarily a chronic lung disease/respiratory management proxy. Any association with AKI would be indirect and likely dominated by stronger illness-severity and hemodynamic variables.

Late LABA use may correlate with prolonged ventilation/respiratory morbidity but is not a direct renal signal. Low priority for sparse selection due to indirectness and expected redundancy with ventilation/hemodynamic features.

Inhaled corticosteroid dosing reflects airway inflammation treatment and chronic pulmonary disease rather than kidney injury. Early dosing is often routine and unlikely to add unique predictive information for AKI.
Budesonide use in 13–24h is a respiratory-care marker with minimal direct renal relevance. Any predictive signal would be indirect and typically redundant with more proximal physiologic predictors.

Late inhaled budesonide remains an indirect proxy for pulmonary issues and treatment patterns. Despite closer timing, it is unlikely to compete with direct renal/hemodynamic features in a sparse model.

Short-acting bronchodilator use indicates bronchospasm/airway management and is not a kidney biomarker. Early post-op administration is common and provides little unique information for creatinine ratio.
Levalbuterol dosing in this window is still primarily a respiratory status/treatment proxy. Indirect links to AKI (via severity) are likely dominated by direct perfusion and renal markers.

Late bronchodilator requirement may track prolonged respiratory morbidity but has weak mechanistic connection to renal dysfunction. Low sparse-model priority due to indirectness and redundancy with ventilation/oxygenation variables.

Inhaled saline is used for secretion clearance/humidification and does not directly affect renal function. It is mainly a care-pattern proxy and unlikely to add predictive value for Hour-60 creatinine ratio.

Nebulized saline is an airway secretion/bronchial hygiene therapy signal, not a renal physiology marker. Likely reflects respiratory care patterns and is weak/indirect for AKI prediction, so low sparse-model priority.

Nebulized saline dose in the closest window mainly captures respiratory secretion management rather than kidney perfusion or function. Minimal unique value for creatinine_ratio and mostly clinician-behavior proxy.
Inhaled levalbuterol indicates bronchospasm/airway reactivity treatment early post-op. It is not directly linked to renal dysfunction and is likely noisy in the immediate 0–12h period.

Levalbuterol use reflects pulmonary status and treatment intensity rather than renal injury mechanisms. Any association with AKI would be indirect via illness severity and unlikely to survive sparse selection.
Levalbuterol dose at 25–36h is still primarily a respiratory therapy marker. It offers little unique predictive signal for Hour-60 creatinine ratio compared with hemodynamics/renal markers.

Basal insulin dosing reflects diabetes status and glycemic management, which can correlate with AKI risk but indirectly. Early post-op dosing is often protocol-driven and noisy, so limited sparse-model value.

Glargine dose in 13–24h is a proxy for baseline diabetes/insulin needs and evolving nutrition status. Predictive value for creatinine_ratio is indirect and likely redundant with other diabetes/severity features.

Later basal insulin may better reflect sustained insulin requirement, but it remains an indirect marker (diabetes, stress hyperglycemia, feeding). Unlikely to add unique AKI signal in a sparse linear model.
Detemir is another basal insulin; dosing mainly captures diabetes management rather than renal physiology. Redundant with glargine as a basal-insulin signal and early window adds noise.
Detemir dose reflects basal insulin requirement and care pathway choices. It is largely redundant with other basal insulin variables and only indirectly related to AKI risk.
Basal detemir at 25–36h may track ongoing metabolic stress but remains a weak proxy for renal outcomes. Redundant with glargine/other insulin features, so low priority.

Rapid-acting insulin dose reflects hyperglycemia burden and feeding/TPN initiation, which can correlate with severity but not directly with kidney injury. In 0–12h it is often protocol-driven and unstable.

Lispro dose in 13–24h can capture stress hyperglycemia and nutritional exposure, which may track systemic stress relevant to AKI risk. Among insulin features it may add more dynamic information than basal insulin, but remains indirect.

Lispro dose at 25–36h is the closest window and may reflect ongoing stress hyperglycemia/feeding intensity, which can correlate with evolving organ dysfunction. Still an indirect proxy and potentially redundant with insulin infusion dose if present.

Regular insulin infusion dose reflects tight glycemic control needs and acute stress response, often used in sicker post-cardiac surgery patients. Early window is noisy, but infusion requirement can be a stronger severity/metabolic signal than basal insulin.

Insulin infusion dose in 13–24h captures sustained stress hyperglycemia and ICU intensity, which may correlate with subsequent AKI. It is still indirect for renal injury but likely more informative than inhaled meds or basal insulin in sparse selection.

Regular insulin infusion dose reflects stress hyperglycemia/diabetes management and overall illness severity. It is an indirect proxy for acuity and clinician behavior and is unlikely to add unique AKI signal beyond hemodynamics/labs in a sparse model despite being in the closest window.

Exposure to iodinated contrast can precipitate or worsen AKI, especially in very elderly post-cardiac surgery patients. Early (0–12h) timing is less proximal and may be noisy, but contrast is a relatively specific renal insult and could add unique value if present.

Iodinated contrast exposure in the 13–24h window is a specific nephrotoxic hit with plausible impact on creatinine trajectory by Hour 60. Compared with 0–12h it is more temporally aligned and may survive sparse selection as a distinct mechanism.

Recent (25–36h) iodinated contrast exposure is a strong, specific, and temporally proximal risk factor for rising creatinine by Hour 60. Within this batch it is the most direct kidney-injury signal and least redundant with other features.

Ipratropium dosing reflects bronchodilator use for COPD/bronchospasm rather than renal physiology. It is largely a comorbidity/treatment proxy and early timing further reduces usefulness for predicting creatinine ratio.
Ongoing ipratropium use mainly indicates respiratory disease management and care patterns. It has minimal direct linkage to AKI and is unlikely to be selected in a sparse renal model.

Late-window ipratropium dose still primarily captures respiratory treatment intensity rather than kidney injury. Even though temporally closer, it remains a weak, indirect proxy with low unique predictive value.

Combination bronchodilator dosing reflects airway/reactive disease treatment and ventilatory course. It is not a direct renal signal and is likely redundant with broader severity markers not in this batch.
Bronchodilator requirement in this window is a nonspecific marker of pulmonary issues and clinician practice. It offers little unique information for Hour 60 creatinine ratio in a sparse model.

Late bronchodilator dosing remains an indirect proxy for respiratory status and treatment intensity. It is unlikely to contribute independent predictive value for AKI compared with more kidney-specific exposures like contrast.

Iron administration typically reflects anemia management/transfusion strategy rather than renal perfusion or injury. In the immediate post-op window it is especially confounded by routine care and has low sparse-selection priority.

Iron dosing in this period is still mainly a treatment proxy for anemia and does not directly encode evolving kidney dysfunction. Any association with AKI would be indirect and likely dominated by stronger physiologic variables.

Later iron administration continues to represent anemia management and clinician behavior more than renal pathophysiology. Despite proximity, it is unlikely to add unique predictive signal for creatinine ratio.

Isosorbide mononitrate use indicates chronic CAD/angina therapy and may correlate with cardiovascular comorbidity and hemodynamic management. However it is an indirect marker and early post-op continuation/holding is practice-driven, limiting sparse-model value.

Nitrate administration suggests ongoing antianginal therapy and possibly blood pressure/afterload considerations. It is still a weak, indirect proxy for renal outcomes and likely redundant with direct hemodynamic features.

Late-window nitrate use may reflect stability and resumption of home meds rather than kidney injury. It could weakly track overall recovery/hemodynamics but is unlikely to survive sparse selection versus more specific renal insults like contrast.

Potassium phosphate repletion early post-op, often reflecting hypophosphatemia and/or clinician response to electrolyte shifts. Indirect and behavior-driven; 0–12h is noisy and this is unlikely to add unique AKI signal beyond core renal/hemodynamic features.

K-phos dosing in 13–24h may reflect ongoing electrolyte derangements from critical illness or nutrition/diuresis. Still largely a treatment proxy and weakly linked to creatinine trajectory, so low sparse-model priority.

K-phos dosing closest to the outcome window may capture persistent metabolic derangement or evolving renal dysfunction affecting phosphate handling. Among the k-phos epochs this is the most temporally informative, but remains indirect and clinician-driven.

Ketamine dose in the immediate post-op period reflects sedation/analgesia strategy and agitation/hemodynamic tolerance. It is not a direct renal signal and is likely dominated by stronger severity/hemodynamic variables.
Ketamine use in 13–24h is mainly a sedation/analgesia choice and proxy for ventilation/ICU course. Weak mechanistic link to AKI and unlikely to survive sparse selection.

Ketamine dose at 25–36h reflects ongoing sedation needs rather than kidney injury physiology. Even with better timing, it is a clinician-behavior proxy with minimal unique predictive value for creatinine ratio.
Volume of lactated Ringer’s given early post-op reflects resuscitation and fluid shifts. Early fluids are confounded by routine bypass/anesthesia effects and are a noisy proxy for perfusion/AKI risk.

LR volume in 13–24h may better reflect ongoing resuscitation needs, hemodynamic instability, or attempts to support renal perfusion. Moderately useful as a treatment-intensity proxy, but still indirect and confounded by clinician practice.

LR volume at 25–36h is closest to Hour 60 and may capture persistent shock/resuscitation or fluid management affecting renal congestion/perfusion. Useful but not top-tier because it is a therapy proxy and overlaps with net fluid balance/pressors if available.
Lactulose use early post-op typically reflects bowel regimen or hepatic encephalopathy management rather than renal physiology. Mostly unrelated to AKI and unlikely to add unique predictive signal.
Lactulose administration in 13–24h is primarily a constipation/encephalopathy treatment marker. It is a weak, indirect proxy with minimal expected contribution to creatinine ratio prediction.
Lactulose use at 25–36h remains a bowel regimen/encephalopathy proxy and not a renal biomarker. Even with closer timing, it is unlikely to survive sparse feature selection.
Levothyroxine dosing reflects chronic hypothyroidism replacement and medication reconciliation. Not an acute renal signal and largely unrelated to short-term postoperative creatinine changes.
Continuation of levothyroxine in 13–24h is a routine chronic medication marker. Minimal mechanistic or predictive linkage to evolving AKI over the next 36 hours.
Levothyroxine dosing at 25–36h remains a chronic therapy indicator rather than an acute physiologic marker. Very low unique value for sparse prediction of creatinine ratio.

Topical lidocaine patch use reflects pain-control strategy and mobilization comfort. It is a clinician-choice proxy with no direct renal relevance and is unlikely to contribute in a sparse linear model.

Topical lidocaine patch use is primarily an analgesia choice and does not directly reflect renal perfusion or AKI evolution. Likely a clinician-behavior proxy with minimal unique value for creatinine_ratio.
Topical lidocaine patch dosing in the closest window still has no plausible mechanistic link to AKI beyond general care patterns. Unlikely to survive sparse selection given weak/indirect signal.

Systemic lidocaine early post-op is usually for analgesia/antiarrhythmic protocols and is not a direct kidney injury marker. The 0–12h window is noisy and this is likely redundant with overall illness/arrhythmia management.

Systemic lidocaine may reflect arrhythmia burden or analgesia needs, which can correlate weakly with severity but not specifically renal dysfunction. Some potential proxy value, but low priority versus hemodynamics/renal markers.

Later systemic lidocaine could indicate ongoing arrhythmia management or ICU complexity closer to the outcome time. Still an indirect proxy and unlikely to add unique predictive value in a sparse model.

ACE inhibitor exposure can reduce intraglomerular pressure and is often held in AKI/hypotension, so administration/withholding may weakly signal renal/hemodynamic status. However 0–12h is early/noisy and dosing is heavily clinician-driven.

ACE inhibitor use in this window may reflect hemodynamic stability and clinician comfort (or conversely absence may reflect evolving AKI), giving some predictive signal. Still partly a treatment-policy proxy and likely correlated with ARB features.

ACE inhibitor dosing closest to Hour 60 can encode whether renal function and blood pressure have stabilized enough to resume RAAS blockade, which often tracks evolving AKI. Among RAAS-agent features, this timing is most likely to add unique predictive value.

ARB exposure/withholding can relate to renal perfusion and clinician concern for AKI, but immediate post-op dosing is uncommon and highly protocol-driven. Early window noise and redundancy with ACEI make it low priority.

ARB use in 13–24h may indicate stability and preserved renal function (or absence may indicate AKI/hypotension), offering moderate proxy signal. Likely redundant with lisinopril as a general RAAS-blockade indicator.

ARB dosing in 25–36h is a relatively proximal indicator of whether clinicians perceive renal/hemodynamic recovery sufficient for RAAS blockade. Stronger sparse-model candidate than earlier epochs, though still partly redundant with ACEI.

Magnesium sulfate replacement early post-op is routine (electrolyte repletion/arrhythmia prophylaxis) and not specific for kidney injury. Dose is driven by protocols and early shifts, so it adds little unique predictive value.

Ongoing magnesium sulfate dosing mainly reflects electrolyte management and monitoring intensity rather than renal dysfunction per se. Likely redundant with other severity/monitoring signals and low priority for sparsity.

Later magnesium sulfate needs could weakly reflect reduced renal clearance or ongoing ICU complexity closer to the outcome. Still a nonspecific, protocol-driven signal and unlikely to beat direct renal/hemodynamic predictors.

Non-sulfate magnesium administration in 0–12h is typically routine repletion and heavily influenced by standard post-cardiac surgery pathways. Minimal direct linkage to Hour 60 creatinine_ratio and redundant with magnesium sulfate dosing.
